title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text: 1 Preface

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.1 A New Era of Programming
We are living through a fundamental transformation in how software is built.
Large Language Models (LLMs) have shifted the bottleneck from execution to definition. The question is no longer:

“Can we build this?”

But rather:

“What exactly should we build?”

This book is about navigating this shift—understanding what it means, how to adapt, and what skills are needed to thrive in this new era.

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.5 Who This Book Is For
This book is for anyone who wants to understand how programming is changing and how to adapt:

Programmers who want to understand how their role is evolving in the AI era
System designers seeking new frameworks for building with AI systems
Product builders who want to leverage AI to create better products faster
Anyone interested in the future of software development

No prior AI experience is required—only a willingness to think differently about programming.

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.6 How This Book Is Organized
The book is structured as a journey from first principles to practical application, spanning 34 chapters organized into four main phases.

1.6.1 Phase 1: Foundations (Chapters 1–8)
The first phase establishes the fundamental shift from execution to definition.

Chapter 1 introduces the historical evolution of programming and the discontinuity introduced by LLMs
Chapter 2 explains why definition, not execution, has become the new bottleneck
Chapter 3 redefines programming itself as the act of defining systems
Chapters 4–8 introduce the core framework: Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation, forming what we call the Minimal Loop



1.6.2 Phase 2: Definition Skills (Chapters 9–17)
With the framework established, this phase develops the skills needed to operate at the definition level.

Chapters 9–10 explore the Possibility Space and Structural Thinking—how to organize ideas into coherent systems
Chapters 11–15 delve into Composition, Goal Compression, Spec Engineering, Spec Structure, and Templates—practical methods for defining systems clearly
Chapters 16–17 cover Spec Agents and Refinement, exploring how AI can assist in the definition process and how specs evolve through iteration



1.6.3 Phase 3: System Building (Chapters 18–29)
This phase moves from defining individual components to building complete AI-powered systems.

Chapters 18–19 examine the transformation from Spec to Instruction and Instruction Design principles
Chapters 20–22 introduce Tool Use, Multi-Agent Systems, and Memory Systems—how to expand capabilities beyond generation
Chapters 23–29 cover Agent Systems, Chat Systems, Data Pipelines, AI Tools, and Reliability—practical patterns for building robust systems



1.6.4 Phase 4: From Systems to Reality (Chapters 30–34)
The final phase bridges the gap between systems and real-world impact.

Chapters 30–31 address the journey from Idea to Product and System Design as the new core discipline
Chapters 32–33 explore the concept of Language as an Operating System and Human-AI Cognition—thinking of AI not as a tool but as a cognitive partner
Chapter 34 concludes with the transformation from developers to builders and what this means for the future

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.7 A Note on Reading
This book is designed to be read sequentially, as each chapter builds upon concepts introduced earlier. However, certain chapters can be read independently for specific topics:

Want to improve your prompt engineering? → Chapters 7, 19
Interested in multi-agent systems? → Chapters 21, 23
Focusing on reliability and evaluation? → Chapters 8, 24, 25
Building your first AI tool? → Chapters 29, 30

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.8 Core Principles
Throughout this book, several themes recur:

Definition over execution — The primary skill is now defining what should exist, not writing how it should be implemented
Systems over code — The unit of work has shifted from functions to entire systems
Specs as first-class artifacts — Specifications are no longer documentation—they are executable artifacts
Control through constraints — In an era of powerful AI, control comes from defining boundaries clearly
Builder mindset — The future belongs to those who can orchestrate systems, not those who write code

title: AI Programming: From Intent to Execution
text:
1.9 The Journey Ahead
This is not a book about using AI tools. It is a book about rethinking what programming means when AI can execute.
The journey begins with a simple question: What are you trying to do?
By the end, you will have a complete framework for answering that question clearly—and for building systems that deliver on that definition.
Welcome to the era of AI programming.

LIN Mi
February 2026

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.1 Programming Has Always Evolved
Programming has never been static. Throughout the history of computing, it has continuously evolved through layers of abstraction, each transformation moving developers further from the raw machinery and closer to the problem domain itself.
This evolution is not about replacing previous skills—it is about shifting the boundary of responsibility. Each new layer of abstraction does not eliminate the need for understanding lower levels; rather, it allows developers to focus their cognitive energy on higher-level concerns while delegating lower-level details to automated systems.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.1.1 Phase 1 — Machine-Level Programming
In the earliest days of programming, developers worked directly with hardware through assembly language and machine code. Every instruction had to be explicitly specified, and every bit of memory had to be manually managed.

Direct manipulation of hardware registers and memory addresses
Writing assembly instructions mapped one-to-one to machine operations
Manual management of every computational resource

The core challenge in this era was straightforward but demanding:

Control the machine precisely

Programmers needed intimate knowledge of the underlying architecture—register configurations, memory layout, instruction timing. A single mistake could crash the system or produce unpredictable results. Success required an almost mechanical precision in thinking.



2.1.2 Phase 2 — High-Level Languages
The introduction of high-level programming languages like C, Java, and Python represented a massive leap forward. These languages abstracted away many hardware details, allowing developers to express logic in terms that resembled human thought rather than machine architecture.

Variables and data types instead of memory addresses
Control structures like loops and conditionals instead of jump instructions
Functions and procedures instead of raw instruction sequences

The core challenge shifted from machine control to something more intellectual:

Express logic clearly

Developers no longer needed to worry about register allocation or memory alignment. Instead, they focused on:

Designing algorithms that efficiently solved problems
Structuring control flow through clear conditional logic
Organizing data into appropriate structures
Writing code that other humans could understand and maintain

This was the first major shift toward definition over implementation—the language became a medium for expressing computational logic rather than specifying machine behavior.



2.1.3 Phase 3 — Frameworks and Libraries
As software systems grew more complex, developers realized that they were repeatedly solving similar problems. This led to the rise of frameworks and libraries—pre-built components that encapsulated common patterns and functionality.

Web frameworks handling routing, authentication, and database connections
Object-relational mappers abstracting database interactions
SDKs providing standardized interfaces to external services

The core challenge evolved once again:

Compose existing components

Instead of building everything from scratch, developers became integrators and composers:

Reusing established patterns rather than reinventing solutions
Integrating multiple libraries and frameworks into coherent systems
Focusing on the unique application logic rather than infrastructure concerns
Making architectural decisions about which components to use and how to connect them

This phase introduced a new way of thinking: developers were no longer building systems atomically—they were assembling them from well-defined parts.



2.1.4 Phase 4 — Cloud and APIs
The cloud revolution further abstracted away infrastructure concerns. Developers could now deploy and scale applications without managing physical servers, and could access powerful capabilities through well-defined APIs.

Infrastructure as a Service providing on-demand computing resources
Platform as a Service offering complete application hosting environments
API ecosystems connecting systems across organizational boundaries

The core challenge shifted to a new dimension:

Orchestrate distributed systems

Developers became system coordinators rather than implementers:

Calling services through APIs instead of building everything internally
Managing flows of data across multiple distributed components
Designing resilient architectures that could handle partial failures
Thinking in terms of system-level behavior rather than program-level logic

This phase represented the culmination of decades of abstraction—developers could now build remarkably complex systems while rarely touching low-level implementation details.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.2 A Pattern Across History
Looking across all these phases of evolution, a clear and consistent pattern emerges:

Each evolution reduces the need to focus on “how things are done” and increases the importance of defining “what should be done.”

Abstraction consistently moves upward, taking developers closer to the problem domain and further from implementation details. With each shift, the boundary of responsibility moves:

From low-level execution → to high-level definition
From specifying steps → to describing outcomes
From managing details → to orchestrating systems

This pattern suggests that the history of programming is the history of moving the developer’s attention up the abstraction ladder. The LLM era represents not a break from this pattern, but its acceleration and transformation.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.3 The LLM Breakpoint
Large Language Models introduce a fundamentally different kind of abstraction—one that does not merely add another layer to the stack, but fundamentally restructures the relationship between human intent and machine execution.
Previous abstractions—assembly languages, high-level languages, frameworks, cloud services—all required developers to specify how a task should be accomplished, albeit at increasing levels of abstraction. An algorithm still needed to be designed, control flow still needed to be specified, components still needed to be assembled.
LLMs abstract something more fundamental: the act of implementation itself.


2.3.1 What Changes?
To understand this shift, consider what changes in the development workflow:
Previously: - You define the logic that solves a problem - You break that logic into implementable steps - You write code that implements each step - You debug and refine the implementation
Now: - You describe the desired behavior in natural language - You specify constraints and requirements - The system generates the implementation automatically - You evaluate and refine the definition
The key difference is that implementation becomes a derived activity rather than a primary one. The focus shifts from crafting code to crafting definitions.



2.3.2 This is not just another layer
It is crucial to understand that LLMs do not represent a continuation of the existing abstraction hierarchy. They introduce a discontinuity—a break from the pattern of gradual abstraction.

Frameworks abstract code structure, but you still write the logic
Libraries provide functionality, but you still compose them manually
Cloud services abstract infrastructure, but you still orchestrate systems

LLMs abstract code creation itself. When you describe what you want in sufficient detail, the system can generate the implementation without your direct involvement in the coding process.

This is a discontinuity, not a continuation. It changes not just what we do, but how we think about what programming is.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
section: 2.4 From Writing Code to Describing Systems
text:
2.4 From Writing Code to Describing Systems
This shift manifests in concrete changes to how developers work. Consider a practical example: building a web scraper.
In the traditional approach: - Write HTTP request handling code - Implement HTML parsing logic - Design data extraction patterns - Handle edge cases and errors - Manage concurrent requests - Store results in a database
In the LLM-enhanced approach: - Describe the goal: “Extract product information from e-commerce pages” - Specify constraints: “Handle pagination, extract price and title, avoid duplicates” - Define output format: “JSON with product_id, title, price, url” - The system generates and executes the scraping logic
The developer’s activity has shifted from writing code to describing systems. Instead of implementing functionality, they define:

Intent—what they want to achieve
Constraints—what boundaries must be respected
Outcomes—what success looks like
Context—under what conditions the system operates
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
section: 2.5 The Shift: From How to What
text:
2.5 The Shift: From How to What
We can summarize this transformation with a simple yet profound change in perspective:
Before: How do we build this?
Now:    What exactly should be built?
This shift is not merely linguistic—it represents a fundamental change in the mental model of programming.


2.5.1 “How” does not disappear
It is important to clarify that this shift does not mean implementation ceases to matter. Implementation still exists, and systems still execute logic through code. The difference lies in responsibility:

Implementation details are increasingly handled by AI systems
Developers no longer need to specify every step manually
The “how” becomes a derived concern rather than a primary one

The system still needs to know how to accomplish tasks—but that knowledge is now encoded in the AI’s training and inference capabilities rather than being explicitly programmed by the developer.



2.5.2 “What” becomes the bottleneck
As execution becomes easier and more automated, a new difficulty emerges that was previously hidden by the complexity of implementation:

Vague goals that resist precise specification
Incomplete definitions that miss critical requirements
Hidden assumptions that lead to unexpected behaviors
Ambiguous constraints that allow multiple interpretations

The problem has shifted from technical capability to conceptual clarity. The question is no longer:

“Can we build it?”

But rather:

“Do we know exactly what we are building?”

This is a profound shift because it changes the source of difficulty. In traditional programming, challenges were primarily technical—how to make the code work, how to handle edge cases, how to optimize performance. In the LLM era, challenges become primarily conceptual—how to define what we want with sufficient precision that a system can reliably implement it.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.6 A Change in Responsibility
This shift in focus fundamentally changes the role of the developer. The identity and daily activities of programmers evolve in important ways:
From: - Implementer—translating designs into working code - Debugger—finding and fixing errors in implementation - Code optimizer—improving efficiency and performance
To: - Problem definer—clarifying what problem should be solved - System designer—specifying how components should interact - Specification author—writing clear, executable definitions
This is not a demotion of the developer’s role—it is an elevation. Instead of being a translator between requirements and implementation, the developer becomes a designer of systems and an architect of behavior.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.7 A New Kind of Skill
In this new paradigm, the most valuable skills are different from those traditionally prized:

Defining goals precisely—moving from vague ideas to specific, measurable objectives
Structuring problems clearly—breaking complex problems into well-defined components
Expressing constraints explicitly—articulating boundaries and limitations clearly
Designing evaluation criteria—defining how success will be measured

These are not traditional “coding skills” in the sense of syntax mastery or algorithm implementation. They are definition skills—the ability to think clearly about what should exist and to express that clarity in forms that systems can execute.
A developer who excels in this new era is someone who can: - Take a vague requirement and make it precise - Identify hidden assumptions and make them explicit - Design systems that behave predictably - Evaluate whether a system truly solves the intended problem
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.8 Why This Matters
Understanding and embracing this shift is not optional—it is essential for staying relevant in the evolving landscape of software development.
If you continue to operate under the old model: - You will focus your energy on implementation details that AI systems can handle - You will underutilize the capabilities of AI systems by using them as simple code generators - You will remain constrained by the limits of manual execution and human cognition - You will compete with AI on its strengths rather than leveraging its capabilities
If you adopt the new model: - You will shift your focus to definition, where human insight remains uniquely valuable - You will leverage AI for execution, amplifying your capabilities rather than competing with them - You will operate at a higher level of abstraction, building systems rather than writing code - You will position yourself as a system designer rather than an implementer
The choice is not between human and AI—it is between leveraging AI as a tool for definition versus using AI as a tool for implementation. The former approach yields exponentially greater leverage.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.9 The Core Insight
The evolution of programming has always been about abstraction—moving away from implementation details and toward the problem domain. But the LLM era introduces a qualitatively new level of abstraction:

The abstraction of implementation itself.

This is not just another step in a long progression—it is a transformation of what programming means. We are moving from an era where programmers specify how to accomplish tasks to an era where they define what should exist.
This transformation requires us to reconsider not just our tools and techniques, but our fundamental understanding of what programming is and what programmers do.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining
text:
2.10 Final Statement
Programming is no longer primarily about writing code. It is about defining systems—specifying intent, structuring behavior, and designing outcomes in ways that enable AI systems to execute reliably.

The future programmer is not the one who can implement everything,
but the one who can define exactly what should exist.

This shift is not a threat to programmers—it is an elevation of the role. The most valuable developers will be those who master the art of definition, who can think clearly about what should exist and express that clarity in forms that systems can execute.
crumbs: 2  The Shift: From Coding to Defining

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.1 Execution Has Been Outsourced
In any system of production, there is always a limiting factor—the bottleneck. This is the point in the system that constrains overall performance, the place where additional investment yields the greatest return. Understanding where the bottleneck lies is essential for effective system design and optimization.
In traditional software development, the bottleneck was clear and universally recognized:
In other words:
The primary limitation on what could be built was not the clarity of the idea, but the ability to implement it correctly and efficiently. Better tools, faster computers, and improved methodologies all focused on alleviating this execution bottleneck.
With the rise of Large Language Models and AI systems, execution is no longer the primary constraint in software development. This represents a fundamental shift in the economics of programming.
Today, a developer equipped with modern AI tools can:
What once required hours or days of manual effort can now be accomplished in seconds or minutes. The barrier to execution has dropped dramatically.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  The New Bottleneck
text:
Writing correct code
Managing complexity
Handling edge cases
Debugging failures



Execution was the bottleneck.

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
Generate working code from natural language descriptions in seconds
Scaffold entire application structures in minutes that would have taken hours
Reuse patterns and best practices automatically across projects
Delegate routine tasks to specialized agents that handle them reliably




3.1.1 What “outsourcing execution” really means
It is important to be precise about what this means. We are not saying that execution has disappeared or become unimportant. Systems still need to execute logic, code still needs to run, and implementation details still matter.
What has changed is who performs the execution:

Execution is no longer performed directly by humans.

Instead, it is handled by:

Language models that generate code from specifications
Agent systems that coordinate multi-step workflows
Toolchains and APIs that encapsulate complex functionality

The developer no longer “does the work” step by step, writing each line of code and handling each edge case manually. Instead, they define the work at a higher level, and the system performs the detailed execution.
This is analogous to the shift from hand-crafting furniture to managing an automated factory. The carpenter who once cut each board and drove each nail now designs the product and manages the machinery that produces it. The execution has been outsourced, but the need for design and quality assurance remains.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.2 The Illusion of Productivity
At first glance, this shift seems like a pure productivity gain—a straightforward improvement that makes everything faster and easier. Developers can generate code more quickly, iterate more rapidly, and prototype more extensively.

Faster development cycles as code generation accelerates
Less manual effort spent on routine implementation tasks
More output in less time, seemingly unlimited productivity

However, a new problem quickly emerges that is less immediately apparent but equally significant:

The system produces exactly what you ask for—no more, no less.

This seemingly straightforward statement has profound implications. If the input to the system is vague, incomplete, inconsistent, or ambiguous, then:

The output will faithfully reflect those qualities
Errors will shift from implementation failures upstream to definition failures
Failures become harder to diagnose because they are not bugs in code but bugs in understanding

The illusion of productivity comes from assuming that faster execution automatically produces better results. In reality, faster execution of poorly defined tasks produces poorly defined results more quickly.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.3 The New Bottleneck: Definition
As execution becomes easier and more automated, another constraint becomes visible that was previously hidden by the effort of implementation. This constraint emerges precisely because execution is no longer the limiting factor.
We now face questions that were always present but rarely examined:

What exactly are we building?
What are the complete requirements?
What does “correct” mean in this context?
What constraints must the system respect?
How will we know when we have succeeded?

These questions were always important, but they were often addressed implicitly through the iterative process of implementation. As developers wrote code and encountered problems, they refined their understanding of what they were building. The feedback loop of implementation served as a discovery mechanism.
Now that execution is outsourced, this implicit discovery process is disrupted. We can no longer rely on the act of coding to clarify our thinking. We must be clear before we execute.

Definition becomes the bottleneck.



3.3.1 Symptoms of a Definition Bottleneck
How can you tell if you are facing a definition problem rather than an execution problem? Look for these common symptoms:

Prompt instability: You keep rewriting prompts or specifications, but results remain inconsistent and unpredictable
The “almost” problem: The system “almost works” but never fully satisfies the intended goal—there’s always something slightly off
Output inconsistency: Different runs with similar inputs produce significantly different outputs
Inability to articulate success: You cannot clearly explain what success looks like or how to measure it
Endless tweaking: You find yourself making endless small adjustments without meaningful improvement

These are not execution failures in the traditional sense. The system is not “broken” or “buggy”—it is following your definition faithfully.
They are definition failures—the system is doing exactly what you asked for, but what you asked for was not what you actually wanted.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
section: 3.4 From Code Errors to Definition Errors
text:
3.4 From Code Errors to Definition Errors
The nature of errors in software development has fundamentally changed. Understanding this change is crucial for effective debugging and improvement in the LLM era.


3.4.1 Before (Execution Errors)
In traditional programming, errors were primarily technical and localized:

Syntax errors—mistakes in the grammar of the programming language
Runtime exceptions—errors that occur during execution, such as null pointer exceptions
Logical bugs—incorrect implementations of intended logic
Performance issues—inefficient code that doesn’t meet performance requirements

These errors shared important characteristics:

They were localized—you could often trace them to specific lines of code or functions
They were deterministic—the same input consistently produced the same error
They were debuggable through code inspection—you could understand the problem by examining the code




3.4.2 Now (Definition Errors)
In LLM-based systems, errors have a different character:

Ambiguous goals—the specification doesn’t clearly define what should be achieved
Missing constraints—important boundaries or limitations are not specified
Undefined edge cases—the system doesn’t know how to handle unusual inputs
Weak evaluation criteria—there’s no clear way to measure success

These new types of errors are fundamentally different:

They are global—problems often stem from the overall definition rather than specific implementation details
They are context-dependent—the same definition may work in some contexts but fail in others
They are difficult to trace—the error is not in the code but in the thinking that produced the specification


The system is not “wrong”—it is following an incomplete definition.

The difference is subtle but crucial. In traditional debugging, you ask “Where is the code wrong?” In the new paradigm, you ask “Where is the definition incomplete?”
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.5 Productivity Reframed
The shift from execution bottleneck to definition bottleneck requires us to reframe our understanding of productivity in programming. The formula for productivity has changed.


3.5.1 Traditional Model
In the traditional model, productivity could be expressed as:
Productivity = Implementation Skill × Time
Improvement came from:

Writing faster—increasing typing speed and code fluency
Debugging better—finding and fixing errors more efficiently
Optimizing execution—making code run faster and use fewer resources

The focus was on doing more in less time through technical skill and efficiency.



3.5.2 LLM Era Model
In the new model, productivity looks different:
Productivity = Definition Quality × Execution Capability
Where:

Execution Capability is largely provided by AI systems and is increasingly commoditized
Definition Quality becomes the primary variable that differentiates outcomes

This is a profound shift. It means that:

Two developers using identical AI tools can produce vastly different results
The difference is not in technical implementation skill but in clarity of thinking
Improving productivity requires improving definition, not learning more syntax




3.5.3 Implication
Even with the most powerful AI systems available:

Poor definition → poor results: No amount of computational power can compensate for unclear requirements
Clear definition → high leverage: A well-defined problem enables exponential amplification through AI execution


AI amplifies your clarity, not your intent.

This is perhaps the most important insight of the LLM era. The value you bring is not in having good ideas—everyone has good ideas. The value is in the ability to define those ideas with sufficient precision that a system can execute them reliably.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.6 The Asymmetry of Leverage
This new model introduces an important asymmetry that has significant implications for how we approach work:

A small improvement in definition can produce large gains in output quality and consistency
A small ambiguity can cause large failures and unpredictable behavior

This makes definition a high-leverage activity—small investments in clarity yield large returns in outcome quality.


3.6.1 Example
Consider two developers using the same AI system to build a document summarization tool:

Developer A provides vague instructions: “Summarize this document”

Results vary widely between runs
Sometimes summaries are too long, sometimes too brief
Key information is sometimes missed
The tool is unreliable and frustrating to use

Developer B provides structured definitions:

“Extract the 3-5 most important points”
“Each point should be 10-15 words”
“Prioritize actionable insights”
“Exclude introductory and concluding material”
Results are consistent and predictable
The tool reliably produces useful summaries


The difference is not in the tools used—both have access to the same AI capabilities. The difference is in definition quality.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.7 The Hidden Work
In traditional programming, most of the effort involved in creating software was visible and measurable:

Writing code—the physical act of creating the implementation
Running tests—verifying that code works as intended
Fixing bugs—tracing and correcting errors

These activities produce tangible outputs that can be counted, measured, and tracked. They fit easily into project management frameworks and productivity metrics.
In the LLM paradigm, much of the critical work becomes invisible:

Clarifying intent—making vague ideas precise and actionable
Structuring requirements—organizing chaotic thoughts into coherent specifications
Defining constraints—articulating boundaries and limitations explicitly
Designing evaluation—creating criteria for measuring success

This work often produces no immediate visible output. It happens in thinking, in conversations, in documentation, and in planning. It feels like “thinking” rather than “building,” and as a result, it is often underestimated and under-resourced.
Yet this invisible work is now the dominant factor in determining success. Without it, the visible work—the rapid code generation, the quick prototyping, the easy iteration—produces noise rather than signal.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.8 A Change in Mindset
To operate effectively in this new paradigm, one must fundamentally shift mindset. The default mode of thinking for experienced developers must be updated.
From: - “How do I implement this?”
To: - “Have I defined this clearly enough for a system to execute?”
This seems like a small shift, but it represents a fundamental change in approach. The first question focuses on implementation technique. The second question focuses on definition quality.
Before starting any work, the effective developer in the LLM era asks:

What exactly do I want this to do?
What are the boundaries of this problem?
What does success look like?
How will I know when I’ve achieved it?
What am I not saying that needs to be explicit?

Only when these questions can be answered clearly should the developer proceed to execution.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.9 Final Insight
The bottleneck in programming has moved. Not from one tool to another, and not from one technique to another, but from one layer of thinking to another.

Execution has been outsourced.
Definition has become the constraint.

This shift represents both a challenge and an opportunity. The challenge is that we must develop new skills and new ways of thinking. The opportunity is that by mastering definition, we can achieve levels of productivity and capability that were previously impossible.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?
text:
3.10 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the quality of what you build is no longer limited by your ability to execute code. It is limited by your ability to define problems, structures, and outcomes with clarity and precision.

The better you define, the more powerful the system becomes.
crumbs: 3  Where Does Difficulty Come From?

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.1 The Question Must Be Reasked
If the core process of programming has changed so fundamentally—if execution has been outsourced and definition has become the bottleneck—then a more fundamental question must be reconsidered:
For decades, this question had a clear, implicit answer. Programmers, computer scientists, and software engineers all operated with a shared understanding that rarely needed to be articulated. That understanding was:
This definition was sufficient when execution was the primary constraint. When the challenge was making the computer do what you wanted, programming naturally meant the act of providing those instructions in a form the machine could execute.
But in the LLM era, this definition becomes incomplete and potentially misleading. If execution is handled by AI systems, and if the primary challenge is definition rather than implementation, then what does “programming” actually mean?

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
What is programming?



Writing code
Implementing logic
Building software through precise instructions

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.2 The Traditional Definition
Traditionally, programming can be described with reasonable accuracy as:

Translating human intent into machine-executable instructions through code.

This description encompasses several key activities:

Designing algorithms—figuring out the step-by-step procedures to solve problems
Writing functions—implementing reusable pieces of logic
Managing control flow—determining the order in which operations occur
Handling data structures—organizing and manipulating information

In this model, the developer acts as both translator and executor. They understand the human intent, figure out how to achieve it algorithmically, and then write the code that makes it happen. The cognitive load is spread evenly between understanding the problem and implementing the solution.
This model served us well for decades. It aligned with how programming languages worked, how software was developed, and how programmers were trained. But it was always an artifact of the technological constraints of the time.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.3 Why This Definition Breaks Down
With LLMs and AI systems, several fundamental assumptions of the traditional model no longer hold:

Code can be generated automatically—AI systems can write code from descriptions
Logic can be inferred from descriptions—systems can figure out implementation details
Execution can be delegated—agents can handle multi-step workflows

This creates a disconnect between our understanding of programming and the reality of what programmers actually do:

The developer no longer needs to manually translate every detail of intent into code
The system can fill in large parts of the implementation automatically
The role of the programmer shifts from doing to defining

As a result, the act of writing code is no longer the core activity of programming. It becomes a secondary, often automated, activity. The primary activity is something else entirely.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.4 Programming as Definition
We can now redefine programming more precisely in a way that captures the new reality:

Programming is the process of defining systems that produce desired outcomes.

This definition represents a fundamental shift in focus:

From Implementation → Definition
From Code → Structure
From Steps → Outcomes

Let’s examine each of these shifts in detail.


4.4.1 From Implementation to Definition
In the traditional model, programming was primarily about implementation—how to make the computer do something. The programmer’s mental model was focused on execution: what code to write, what algorithms to use, what data structures to employ.
In the new model, programming is primarily about definition—what should exist. The programmer’s mental model focuses on outcomes: what behavior is desired, what constraints must be respected, what success looks like.
The implementation becomes a derived concern. Once the system is defined clearly enough, the implementation can be generated automatically. The programmer’s value lies not in writing code, but in writing definitions that code can be generated from.



4.4.2 From Code to Structure
Code is a specific artifact—a sequence of instructions in a programming language. It is concrete, literal, and machine-readable. But it is also low-level and implementation-focused.
Structure is a higher-level abstraction—it describes the organization and behavior of a system without specifying the implementation details. Structure includes:

The components of the system and their relationships
The flows of information and control
The constraints and boundaries that govern behavior
The criteria for evaluating success

In the new model, the programmer works primarily with structure. Code becomes an output of the definition process, not the primary artifact.



4.4.3 From Steps to Outcomes
Traditional programming focuses on steps—what the system should do, in what order, under what conditions. This is a procedural mental model: break the problem down into small pieces, implement each piece, and compose them together.
The new model focuses on outcomes—what the system should produce, regardless of how it achieves it. This is a declarative mental model: describe the desired result, and let the system figure out how to achieve it.
This shift reflects the move from imperative programming (telling the computer what to do) to declarative programming (telling the computer what you want). LLMs make declarative programming practical for a much wider range of problems.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.5 What Does “Defining a System” Mean?
If programming is defining systems, we need to understand what it means to define a system. A system is more than code—it is a coherent entity that produces behavior.
To define a system is to specify:

What the system should do—the functions and capabilities it provides
Under what conditions it operates—the context and environment in which it runs
What constraints it must respect—the boundaries and limitations it must operate within
How success is evaluated—the criteria for determining whether the system works correctly

A system is not just code. It is a combination of:

Behavior—what the system does in response to inputs
Structure—how the system is organized internally
Interaction—how the system relates to other systems and users
Boundaries—what the system does not do

When a programmer defines a system, they are specifying all of these aspects at a level that enables automated execution.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.6 Code Becomes a Byproduct
In this new model, it is important to understand the changing role of code:

Code is still present—systems still execute instructions, and those instructions are still expressed as code
Systems still execute logic—computers still need to do things, and they still need programs to tell them how

However, the relationship between the programmer and code has fundamentally changed:

Code is no longer the primary artifact. It is a byproduct of definition.

The programmer’s role is no longer:

“Write the code that does X”

But rather:

“Define X clearly enough that a system can produce code that does it”

Code becomes something that happens as a result of good definition, not something the programmer focuses on directly. The focus is on the definition, and the code emerges from that definition.
This is analogous to how a compiler translates high-level code into machine instructions. The programmer doesn’t write machine code directly; they write high-level code, and the compiler handles the translation. In the LLM era, the programmer writes definitions, and the AI handles the translation to code.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.7 A Shift in Abstraction
This change represents a move to a higher level of abstraction. To understand this shift, consider what programmers think about in each model:


4.7.1 Before
Programmers thought in terms of implementation details:

Think in functions—what code to write, how to organize it
Think in control flow—what order operations should happen in
Think in implementation steps—how to break a problem down into code

The mental model was procedural and concrete. Programmers had to hold the entire implementation in their heads and ensure it was correct.



4.7.2 Now
Programmers think in terms of system structure:

Think in systems—what components are needed and how they relate
Think in specifications—what the system should do, not how it does it
Think in transformations—what inputs become what outputs

The mental model is structural and declarative. Programmers focus on defining the system at a high level, trusting that the implementation can be generated from that definition.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.8 Programming as System Design
Under this lens, programming aligns more closely with the discipline of system design than with traditional coding. It involves:

Modeling problems—understanding the problem domain and the entities within it
Structuring components—organizing the system into coherent, well-defined parts
Defining interactions—specifying how components communicate and coordinate
Managing constraints—ensuring the system operates within acceptable boundaries

The developer becomes:

A designer of systems—architecting behavior rather than implementing it
A definer of behavior—specifying what should happen rather than how
An orchestrator of capabilities—composing components into coherent wholes

This is a different kind of skill than traditional programming. It requires different aptitudes and different training. But it is no less demanding or valuable.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.9 The Role of Language
In this new model, language becomes central to programming. Not just programming languages, but language in a broader sense:

Natural language—for expressing intent and requirements
Structured specifications—for precise definition of system behavior
Hybrid representations—for combining natural language with formal structure

Why is language so central? Because:

The system now interprets definitions expressed in language.

This elevates language from a communication tool to an execution interface. The programmer’s ability to use language precisely becomes a core technical skill, not just a soft skill.
This is a significant change. In traditional programming, natural language was used for documentation and comments but not for the actual program. Now, natural language (or structured language) becomes the primary medium for defining systems.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.10 From Control to Specification
Traditional programming emphasizes control:

Explicit instructions—telling the system exactly what to do, step by step
Deterministic flows—ensuring that the same input always produces the same output
Precise operations—controlling every aspect of execution

Modern programming emphasizes specification:

Desired outcomes—describing what the result should be, not how to achieve it
Constraints—defining the boundaries within which the system must operate
Evaluation criteria—specifying how to determine whether the system succeeded

Instead of controlling every step, we define the space within which the system operates. The system then figures out how to navigate that space to achieve the desired outcome.
This is analogous to the difference between giving someone turn-by-turn directions versus giving them a destination and letting them find their own route. Both get you to the same place, but the approach is fundamentally different.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.11 Implications for Developers
This redefinition of programming has significant implications for what it means to be skilled and how developers should approach their work.


4.11.1 Less emphasis on:

Syntax mastery—knowing the precise syntax of programming languages
Manual implementation—writing code line by line
Low-level optimization—tweaking code for performance

These skills are still valuable, but they are no longer the primary differentiators. Many aspects of implementation can now be automated.



4.11.2 More emphasis on:

Problem definition—clarifying what problem should be solved
Structural clarity—organizing systems in coherent, understandable ways
Constraint design—specifying boundaries and limitations precisely
Evaluation thinking—defining how to measure success and detect failure

These skills are becoming the core competencies of programming in the LLM era.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.12 A New Mental Model
To adapt to this new paradigm, one must shift from a specific mental model:
From: - “How do I build this step by step?”
To: - “What system, if defined correctly, would produce this outcome?”
This seems like a subtle shift, but it represents a fundamental change in approach. The first question leads to implementation thinking. The second question leads to design thinking.
The effective programmer in the LLM era thinks about systems, not code. They think about outcomes, not steps. They think about definitions, not implementations.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.13 Final Definition
We can now state a refined definition of programming that captures the new reality:

Programming is the act of defining systems—through structured intent, specification, and constraints—so that machines can produce desired and verifiable outcomes.

This definition emphasizes:

Definition as the primary activity
Structure as the medium of definition
Outcomes as the focus of attention
Verification as a critical concern

It moves away from implementation as the core activity and toward definition as the core activity.

title: 4  What is Programming Now?
text:
4.14 Closing Insight
This shift does not remove the need for technical knowledge or programming skill. It reorganizes and reorients them.
The technical knowledge required is different—more focused on system design, specification, and evaluation, less focused on implementation details and optimization. But it is no less demanding or sophisticated.

The power of a programmer is no longer measured by how much they can implement,
but by how precisely they can define what should exist.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.1 Why a New Loop?
In traditional software development, the development process is often described through a familiar sequence:
This model served developers well for decades. The core difficulty in this model was implementation—the challenge of translating ideas and designs into working code that executed correctly. Most tools, methodologies, and best practices focused on alleviating this implementation burden.
However, in the LLM era, this assumption no longer holds. Several fundamental changes have occurred:
As a result, the bottleneck has shifted. The challenge is no longer how to implement, but how to define clearly what should be implemented. The implementation layer has been abstracted away, revealing a new layer of difficulty that was previously hidden.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
Design → Implement → Debug → Deploy



Code can be generated—AI systems can write code from natural language descriptions
Patterns can be reused automatically—systems can identify and apply established patterns
Execution can be delegated to AI—agents can handle multi-step workflows autonomously



This shift requires a new fundamental loop—a new mental model for programming.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.2 The Minimal Loop
At its core, programming in the LLM era can be reduced to a simple, elegant loop:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation
This loop is called “minimal” because:

Each component is necessary—you cannot remove any component without breaking the loop
No component can be reduced—each component plays an essential role
Together, they form a complete system—the loop is self-contained and sufficient

Despite its simplicity, this loop captures the essence of programming in the new paradigm. Let’s examine each component in detail.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.3 Overview of the Components

5.3.1 Intent — What do you want?
Intent is the human-level definition of a goal. It represents the starting point of any programming effort—the desire to create something or solve a problem.
Intent answers fundamental questions:

What are we trying to achieve?
What change do we want to create in the world?
What does success look like from a human perspective?

Intent is inherently:

Semantic—it carries meaning and intention, not just structure
Contextual—it depends on the situation, environment, and stakeholders
Often ambiguous at first—it starts as a vague idea that needs clarification

Example (raw intent): “Build a system that allows users to chat with an AI assistant.”
This intent captures the general idea but lacks specificity. It needs to be transformed into something more precise before it can guide execution.



5.3.2 Spec — What exactly does that mean?
Spec (Specification) transforms intent into a structured, unambiguous definition. It is the bridge between human thinking and machine execution.
A spec defines:

Inputs—what information the system receives
Outputs—what the system produces
Constraints—what boundaries and limitations the system must respect
Success criteria—how we determine whether the system works correctly

The spec removes ambiguity by making implicit assumptions explicit and vague concepts precise.
Example (partial spec): - Users can send messages through a web interface - The system returns responses within 2 seconds - Conversation history is preserved across sessions - Responses should be relevant to the conversation context - The system should handle at least 100 concurrent users

Spec is where thinking becomes precise. It is the translation layer between human intent and machine understanding.




5.3.3 Instruction — How should the system execute?
Instruction converts the specification into a machine-executable form. It is the protocol that tells the system how to act.
Instruction includes:

Task definition—what specific operation should be performed
Input format—how inputs should be structured and provided
Output structure—what format outputs should take
Constraints for execution—what rules must be followed during execution

This is not merely a prompt or a natural language description. It is an execution protocol—a precise set of instructions that a system can follow deterministically.
Example: - Task: Generate a response based on user input and conversation history - Input: {message: string, history: Array<{role, content}>} - Output: {response: string, confidence: number} - Constraints: - Response length: 50-200 words - Response must reference relevant context from history - Confidence score must be between 0 and 1 - Response should be polite and helpful



5.3.4 Execution — Let the system run
Execution is where the system actually does work. It is performed by:

LLMs—language models that generate text based on instructions
Agents—autonomous systems that can reason and act
Toolchains—orchestrated systems that combine multiple capabilities

At this stage, the human is no longer implementing. The system is producing results based on the definitions provided.
The key insight is that execution is now a derived activity—it follows from the definitions provided in the earlier stages. The better those definitions, the better the execution.



5.3.5 Evaluation — Is the result correct?
Evaluation determines whether the output satisfies the original intent and specification. It is the mechanism that closes the loop.
Evaluation answers critical questions:

Did we achieve the goal we set out to achieve?
Does the result meet all the constraints we specified?
Is the output acceptable for the intended use case?

Evaluation can take multiple forms:

Human judgment—manual review by a person
Rule-based checks—automated validation against explicit criteria
Automated metrics—quantitative measures of quality
Model-based evaluation—using another AI to assess the output

Without evaluation, there is no way to know whether the system is working correctly, and no basis for improvement.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.4 The Loop is Iterative
This process is not linear. It is a loop—a cycle that repeats until the system produces satisfactory results.
If the result is not satisfactory, the issue is rarely in execution. AI systems are generally good at following instructions. The issue is usually in:

Unclear intent—the goal was not properly defined
Incomplete spec—important constraints or requirements were missing
Weak instruction—the execution protocol was not precise enough

Therefore:

Programming becomes the process of refining definitions.

The loop evolves as follows:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation
                      ↓
                  Refinement
Each iteration sharpens the definitions, moving the system closer to producing the desired outcome.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.5 Where Errors Actually Come From
In traditional programming, errors were primarily in the code—in the implementation. In the LLM era, errors shift to earlier stages:

5.5.1 1. Intent Errors

The goal is unclear—the problem is not well understood
The problem is misunderstood—the intent doesn’t match the real need
Stakeholder needs are misaligned—different people want different things

Intent errors are the most fundamental and hardest to detect. If you’re solving the wrong problem, even perfect execution won’t help.


5.5.2 2. Spec Errors

Missing constraints—important boundaries were not specified
Ambiguous definitions—terms were used without clear meaning
Incomplete structure—required components were left out

Spec errors are common because it’s hard to anticipate every requirement and constraint.


5.5.3 3. Instruction Errors

Poorly defined inputs/outputs—the data structures are not clear
Weak constraints—the boundaries are too loose or too tight
Inconsistent formatting—the instruction is not structured properly

Instruction errors are often easier to fix than spec or intent errors, but they can still cause significant problems.

Most failures are not execution failures.
They are definition failures.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.6 A New Definition of Programming
From this perspective, we can redefine programming:

The process of transforming an intent into a structured specification, and then into executable instructions that produce verifiable results.

Or more concisely:

Programming is the act of defining systems.

This definition emphasizes:

Definition as the primary activity
Transformation as the core process
Verification as the essential outcome

It moves away from code as the central artifact and toward specification as the central artifact.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.7 Implications
This shift has several important implications for how we approach programming:

5.7.1 1. The Core Skill Changes
From: - Writing code efficiently - Debugging implementation errors - Optimizing performance
To: - Defining problems clearly - Structuring specifications precisely - Designing evaluation criteria thoughtfully
The skills that differentiate programmers are no longer primarily technical implementation skills. They are conceptual and analytical skills related to definition and design.



5.7.2 2. The Role of Code Changes
Code becomes:

An implementation detail—a concern that can often be automated
A byproduct of specification—something that emerges from good definition
Less visible and less important in the daily work of programmers

Programmers spend less time writing code and more time writing specifications.



5.7.3 3. Iteration Becomes Central
Instead of: - Writing once, debugging later—a linear process
We now have: - Define → Execute → Evaluate → Refine—a cyclical process
The focus shifts from getting it right the first time to converging on the right definition through iteration.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.8 Mental Model Upgrade
To work effectively in this paradigm, you must adopt a new mental model:

Think in terms of systems, not scripts
Think in terms of definitions, not implementations
Think in terms of loops, not linear pipelines

This mental model shift is not optional—it is necessary for effective work in the LLM era. Programmers who cling to the old model will find themselves increasingly ineffective.

title: 5  The Minimal Loop
text:
5.9 Final Summary
The Minimal Loop is the foundation of programming in the LLM era:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation
Each stage transforms the problem into a more structured and executable form:

Intent captures the human goal
Spec makes it precise and structured
Instruction makes it executable
Execution produces the result
Evaluation verifies correctness and drives improvement

The key insight is simple but profound:

The quality of the output is determined by the clarity of the definition.

Once this is understood, programming is no longer about controlling machines through precise instructions. It becomes about precisely defining reality so that machines can act on it.

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.1 The Starting Point of All Systems
Every system begins with an intention—a desire to create something, solve a problem, or achieve an outcome. Before any specification is written, before any instruction is executed, before any code is generated, there is a fundamental question:
This question seems simple, almost trivial. Yet it is often the most difficult to answer effectively. In the LLM era, where execution has become easy and automated, the quality of the outcome depends almost entirely on the clarity and precision of this initial definition.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
What are we trying to do?



Intent is the foundation of everything that follows.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.2 What is Intent?
Intent is the human-level definition of a desired outcome. It represents the starting point of any system-building effort—the seed from which all else grows.
Intent describes:

What change we want to create—the difference between the current state and desired state
What problem we want to solve—the issue or challenge that motivates the effort
What result we expect to observe—the tangible outcomes that would indicate success

Intent is not code, and it is not yet a specification. It is:

Conceptual—it exists in the realm of ideas and understanding
Context-dependent—it derives meaning from the situation and environment
Often incomplete at first—it starts as a vague idea that needs refinement

Intent exists in human cognition, not in machine logic. It is the bridge between human desire and system capability.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.3 Intent as a Transformation
A useful way to understand intent is as a transformation—a movement from one state to another:
Current State → Desired State
This framing makes intent concrete and operational. Intent answers two fundamental questions:

Where are we now?—the current situation, problem, or limitation
Where do we want to go?—the desired outcome, solution, or capability

For example, consider the intent to build a customer service chatbot:

Current state: Customers must wait hours for email responses to simple questions
Desired state: Customers receive immediate, accurate answers to common questions 24/7

This transformation framing makes intent specific and actionable. It provides a clear direction for the system-building effort.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.4 The Minimum Structure of Intent
Although intent often begins as a vague idea, it must eventually be structured in order to guide system development effectively. A well-formed intent should clarify several essential elements:

6.4.1 1. Objective
What outcome is desired? What is the primary goal?
This is the “what” of intent—the core objective that the system should achieve.


6.4.2 2. Object
What is the action applied to? What entity or domain is being affected?
This defines the scope and focus of the effort.


6.4.3 3. Context
Under what conditions does the intent apply? What is the environment or situation?
Context provides the boundaries within which the system must operate.


6.4.4 4. Constraints
What limits exist? What boundaries must be respected?
Constraints define what the system cannot do, as well as what it must do.


6.4.5 5. Success Criteria
How do we know when the intent has been achieved? What indicates success?
Success criteria provide a way to measure whether the system has accomplished its goal.
Without these elements, intent remains ambiguous and cannot effectively guide system development.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.5 The Illusion of Clarity
A common and dangerous mistake is believing that an idea is clear simply because it feels clear in our minds. Our intuition often creates an illusion of understanding that does not survive scrutiny.
For example, consider this common intent:

“Build a smart assistant.”

This statement appears meaningful at first glance. It seems to communicate a clear goal. Yet on closer examination, it lacks:

Defined behavior—what should the assistant actually do?
Scope—what domain or problem does it address?
Constraints—what are the limitations on its capabilities?
Evaluation criteria—how do we know if it’s working?

It creates an illusion of understanding while providing no real guidance for implementation.

Clarity in thought must be tested through expression.

If you cannot specify:

What it does—the concrete functions it performs
For whom—the intended users or stakeholders
Under what conditions—the context and environment
What counts as success—the measurable outcomes

Then the intent is not yet clear enough to guide system development.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
section: 6.6 Why Most People “Haven’t Thought It Through”
text:
6.6 Why Most People “Haven’t Thought It Through”
The difficulty of formulating clear intent is not primarily technical—it is cognitive. Several common factors contribute to this difficulty:

6.6.1 1. Lack of Exposure to Possibility
If you do not know what is possible, you cannot define what you want with precision. Your intent is bounded by your awareness of what can be built.

Limited experience → limited imagination of what’s possible
No pattern awareness → vague goals that don’t leverage known capabilities
** unfamiliarity with AI capabilities** → underestimation or overestimation of what can be achieved



6.6.2 2. Mixing Intent with Implementation
People often jump directly to implementation concerns without first clarifying the intent:

Tools—“I want to use React and Node.js”
Frameworks—“I need to use LangChain”
Code—“I’ll write a function that…”

Instead of first asking:

“What exactly should exist?”

This leads to premature decisions and poor system design. The tool should serve the intent, not the other way around.


6.6.3 3. Ambiguity Tolerance
Humans are comfortable with vague ideas and incomplete specifications. We can say things like:

“Make it better”
“Improve performance”
“Make it user-friendly”

And feel that we’ve communicated something meaningful. But machines cannot operate on ambiguity. They need precision.

What is acceptable for humans is insufficient for systems.



6.6.4 4. Missing Evaluation Criteria
Many intents fail because they lack a clear definition of success. Without evaluation criteria:

There is no way to know if the system works
Iteration becomes random and directionless
Results feel inconsistent and unpredictable

Success criteria provide the feedback loop necessary for systematic improvement.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.7 Intent vs Idea
It is important to distinguish between an idea and an intent. These are related but distinct concepts:

6.7.1 Idea

Vague—lacks specificity and precision
Inspirational—captures a general direction or aspiration
Open-ended—does not constrain possibilities

Example: > “An AI tool for productivity”
An idea is a starting point, but it is not yet actionable.


6.7.2 Intent

Structured—has clear components and boundaries
Bounded—defines what is included and what is excluded
Testable—can be evaluated against specific criteria

Example: > “A system that summarizes daily meeting transcripts into actionable bullet points within 5 seconds, with a maximum of 10 bullet points per meeting.”

Ideas inspire. Intent defines.

The transformation from idea to intent is the process of adding structure, precision, and testability to an initial inspiration.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.8 Intent as a Skill
Intent is not a one-time decision or a natural talent. It is a skill that can be developed and improved through practice.
It involves:

Observing real problems—developing sensitivity to genuine needs and pain points
Understanding system capabilities—knowing what tools and technologies can achieve
Structuring goals clearly—organizing complex desires into coherent objectives
Reducing ambiguity—eliminating vagueness and making implicit assumptions explicit

The better your intent-formulation skills, the more effective every subsequent step in the system-building process becomes. A well-formed intent makes spec creation easier, instruction design more straightforward, and evaluation more meaningful.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.9 The Cost of Weak Intent
If intent is unclear or poorly structured, the consequences propagate through the entire system development process:

Spec becomes inconsistent—the specification lacks coherence and clarity
Instruction becomes unstable—execution protocols produce unreliable results
Execution becomes unreliable—outputs vary unpredictably
Evaluation becomes subjective—there’s no clear standard for success

The entire system degrades. Weak intent is not a small problem—it is a fundamental flaw that undermines everything that follows.

Weak intent propagates errors through the entire pipeline.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.10 Strengthening Intent
To improve intent, one must engage in deliberate practice. This is not a technical exercise—it is a thinking discipline.
Key strategies include:

Expand awareness of possibilities—study existing systems, explore new technologies, understand what’s feasible
Practice structuring goals—use frameworks and templates to organize thinking
Define explicit success criteria—make it clear how you’ll know when you’ve succeeded
Separate intention from implementation—focus first on what you want, not how you’ll build it

These practices build the cognitive muscles necessary for effective intent formulation.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.11 Final Insight
Intent is where human cognition meets system design. It is the point where:

Reality is interpreted—understanding the current situation and needs
Goals are defined—articulating what should change and why
Systems begin to take shape—providing the direction for all subsequent work

Intent is not a preliminary step to be rushed through. It is the foundation upon which everything else depends.

If you cannot define what you want, no system can reliably produce it.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do
text:
6.12 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the most powerful developers are not those who can execute the fastest or write the most code. They are those who can define the clearest intent.

The quality of your systems begins with the clarity of your intent.
crumbs: 6  Intent: Defining What to Do

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.1 From Intent to Precision
Intent defines what we want—it captures the human goal and aspiration. But intent alone is not enough to guide system development effectively.
Intent, by its nature, is:
To make a system work reliably, intent must be transformed into something more precise and actionable. This transformation is the role of Spec (Specification).

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
Incomplete—it doesn’t specify all necessary details
Context-dependent—it relies on unstated assumptions and background knowledge
Often ambiguous—it can be interpreted in multiple ways



Spec is where vague intention becomes structured definition.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.2 What is a Spec?
A Spec is a structured, unambiguous description of a system’s behavior. It is the bridge between human intent and machine execution.
A spec defines:

What inputs the system receives—the data and information the system works with
What outputs it produces—the results and responses the system generates
What constraints it must follow—the boundaries and limitations the system must respect
What conditions determine success—the criteria for evaluating whether the system works correctly

Unlike intent, which is human-oriented and can be expressed in natural language, spec is:

System-oriented—designed for machine interpretation and execution
Explicit—leaves nothing implicit or assumed
Verifiable—can be tested against objective criteria

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.3 Spec as Constraint, Not Description
A common and fundamental misunderstanding is to treat a spec as a “detailed description” of a system. This is incorrect and leads to poor specifications.
A spec is not about describing everything a system does in exhaustive detail. It is about defining boundaries—specifying what the system must do, must not do, and how it must behave.


7.3.1 Description vs Specification
Consider this example:
Description: > “The system should respond quickly and accurately to user questions.”
This description feels helpful but is fundamentally useless for system development. “Quickly” and “accurately” are subjective terms that cannot be measured or tested.
Specification: > “Response time must be under 2 seconds for 95% of queries. Answers must reference retrieved documents when available. When no relevant documents are found, the system must explicitly state this limitation.”
This specification is actionable. It defines concrete, measurable constraints that can be implemented and tested.


Description explains. Specification constrains.

The purpose of a spec is not to tell the system everything it might do. It is to tell the system what it must do and what it must not do.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.4 The Function of Spec
Spec performs three critical functions in system development:


7.4.1 1. Remove Ambiguity
Natural language allows multiple interpretations. A single statement can mean different things to different people or systems.
Spec reduces this by:

Defining explicit inputs and outputs—specifying exactly what data goes in and comes out
Clarifying edge cases—determining how the system should handle unusual situations
Making assumptions visible—explicitly stating what is being assumed about context or environment

Without spec, different implementations will interpret the same intent in different ways, leading to inconsistent behavior.



7.4.2 2. Align Humans and Systems
Spec serves as a shared contract between different stakeholders:

Humans—designers, developers, users, and stakeholders
Systems—LLMs, agents, and execution engines

It ensures:

Everyone is operating under the same definition
There is no hidden interpretation gap between human intent and machine execution
Success criteria are agreed upon upfront

Without this alignment, systems may behave in ways that surprise their human counterparts, and humans may have unrealistic expectations of what systems can achieve.



7.4.3 3. Enable Execution
Without spec:

Systems must guess at what is intended
Outputs vary widely between runs
Behavior is unpredictable and unreliable

With spec:

Systems have clear constraints to operate within
Outputs become consistent and predictable
Behavior becomes controllable and verifiable

Spec provides the guidance necessary for automated execution to produce reliable results.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
section: 7.5 Why Spec is the Core Layer
text:
7.5 Why Spec is the Core Layer
In the minimal loop:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation
Spec sits at the center of the loop. This is not accidental—it reflects spec’s critical role.


7.5.1 It connects both sides

Upstream: human intent and understanding
Downstream: machine execution and behavior

Spec is the translation layer that converts human thinking into machine-understandable form.



7.5.2 It transforms meaning

From semantic → structural: converting meaning into formal structure
From vague → explicit: making implicit assumptions clear
From idea → system: transforming abstract concepts into concrete definitions

This transformation is essential for bridging the gap between human cognition and machine execution.


Spec is the bridge between thinking and execution.

Without spec, there is no reliable way to convert human intent into machine behavior.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.6 The Cost of Missing Spec
When spec is weak or absent from the development process, several problems emerge:


7.6.1 1. Systems behave unpredictably

Different outputs for similar inputs
Inconsistent behavior across different runs
Unexpected responses in edge cases

The system becomes unreliable and difficult to trust.



7.6.2 2. Iteration becomes inefficient

Changes are trial-and-error rather than systematic
Improvements are not cumulative—each iteration starts from scratch
Progress is slow and directionless

Without clear specs, you don’t know what to change or how to measure improvement.



7.6.3 3. Errors are misdiagnosed
Developers often blame:

The model—“This LLM isn’t smart enough”
The tool—“This framework has bugs”
The framework—“This approach doesn’t work”

But the real issue is often:

The system was never clearly defined.

Blaming tools and technologies when the real problem is poor spec leads to wasted effort and frustration.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.7 Spec vs Code
Traditionally, code served as the primary definition of a system’s behavior. The code was the specification—if you wanted to know what a system did, you read the code.
Now, that role is shifting. Spec is becoming the primary definition, with code serving as one possible realization of that spec.


7.7.1 Before

Code = behavior
Logic embedded in implementation details
Understanding required reading code




7.7.2 Now

Spec = behavior
Code = one possible implementation of the spec
Understanding requires reading the spec



Code implements. Spec defines.

This shift is profound. It means that behavior is no longer determined by implementation details, but by explicit specification. Multiple different code implementations could satisfy the same spec, and changing the implementation doesn’t necessarily change the behavior.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.8 Spec as a First-Class Artifact
In the LLM era, spec becomes:

A primary artifact—as important as code, perhaps more so
A reusable asset—can be shared, improved, and applied across projects
A unit of iteration—the thing we refine to improve systems

Instead of focusing on:

Code repositories—managing and organizing code

We begin to focus on:

Spec repositories—managing and organizing specifications

This shift requires new tools, new practices, and new ways of thinking about what constitutes the “source code” of a system.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.9 Spec Enables Iteration
Iteration in traditional programming often focuses on code changes—finding and fixing bugs, optimizing performance, refactoring for clarity.
In the new paradigm:

Iteration focuses on refining the spec

The process becomes:

Define spec
Execute
Evaluate
Adjust spec


Improvement comes from better definition, not more code.

This is a more efficient form of iteration because spec is at a higher level of abstraction than code. Small changes in spec can lead to large improvements in system behavior.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
