7.10 Spec and Control
One concern that often arises with this approach is the loss of control:

“If the system executes automatically based on a spec, do we lose control?”

In reality, control shifts rather than disappears:

From controlling steps—specifying every operation and decision
To controlling constraints—defining the boundaries within which the system operates

You no longer dictate every action the system takes. Instead, you define the space within which actions occur. You control the boundaries, not the path.
This is analogous to herding sheep versus directing individual sheep. The shepherd doesn’t control each sheep’s movement directly but defines boundaries that keep the flock moving in the right direction.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.11 Common Mistakes


7.11.1 1. Treating Spec as Optional
Skipping spec or treating it as a mere formality leads to:

Unstable systems with unpredictable behavior
Poor reproducibility—different runs produce different results
Difficulty debugging and improving the system

Spec is not optional. It is essential.



7.11.2 2. Over-specifying
Trying to define every detail, every edge case, every possible behavior:

Reduces flexibility and adaptability
Conflicts with the probabilistic nature of LLM systems
Creates maintenance burden

Good spec finds the right balance between clarity and flexibility.



7.11.3 3. Under-specifying
Leaving too much implicit, assuming the system will figure it out:

Leads to ambiguity and multiple interpretations
Produces inconsistent and unpredictable results
Makes evaluation impossible

You must be explicit about what matters.


Good spec balances clarity and flexibility. It specifies what must be true without dictating how it must be achieved.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.12 Final Insight
Spec is not an intermediate step or a preliminary activity. It is the core of the system. It determines:

What the system does—the behavior it exhibits
How it behaves—the constraints it operates within
Whether it succeeds—whether it meets the intended goals

Without a good spec, no amount of clever code or powerful tools can produce a reliable system. With a good spec, implementation becomes straightforward and almost mechanical.

title: 7  Spec: Eliminating Ambiguity
text:
7.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the most important artifact is no longer code. It is specification.

If intent defines what you want, spec determines whether you can actually get it.

The ability to write good specs is now a core programming skill—perhaps the most important skill.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.1 From Definition to Action
Intent defines what we want—the human goal and aspiration.
Spec defines what it means—the structured, precise specification of behavior.
But neither of these produces results. A perfectly clear intent and a perfectly precise spec, by themselves, do nothing. They are static descriptions of what should exist, not dynamic processes that create results.
To make a system act, to transform definitions into outcomes, we need one more step:
Instruction is where a defined system becomes operational. It is the bridge between static definition and dynamic execution.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text: Instruction — the executable form of definition

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.2 What is an Instruction?
An Instruction is a machine-executable task definition. It translates a specification into a form that a system can act upon directly and reliably.
An instruction defines:

What task to perform—the specific operation the system should execute
What inputs to use—the data and parameters the system should work with
What outputs to produce—the format and structure of the results
What constraints to follow—the rules and boundaries the system must respect during execution

Instruction is not abstract or descriptive. It is operational. It is the protocol that tells a system exactly how to behave.

If Spec defines the system, Instruction runs the system.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.3 Instruction vs Prompt
A common and persistent misconception is to equate instruction with prompt. While related, they are fundamentally different concepts that serve different purposes.


8.3.1 Prompt
A prompt is:

Informal—conversational and flexible in structure
Flexible—designed for exploration and experimentation
Often ambiguous—leaves room for interpretation
Designed for one-off interaction—intended for single-use conversations

Example: > “Summarize this text in a few sentences.”
This prompt works for casual interaction but lacks the precision needed for reliable system execution.



8.3.2 Instruction
An instruction is:

Structured—organized according to a defined format
Explicit—leaves nothing implicit or assumed
Repeatable—produces consistent results across multiple executions
Designed for system execution—intended for automated, reliable operation

Example: > - Task: Summarize the input text > - Input: {text: string} > - Output: {summary: string, key_points: Array<string>} > - Constraints: > - Maximum 5 key points > - Each key point under 20 words > - Summary must include all key points > - Preserve original meaning and tone
This instruction is precise, structured, and executable.


A prompt asks. An instruction defines.

The difference is crucial. Prompts are for conversation and exploration. Instructions are for operation and execution.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.4 Why Prompts Are Not Enough
Prompts work well for certain contexts:

Exploration—trying out ideas and seeing what happens
Experimentation—testing hypotheses and gathering information
Quick interaction—casual conversations with AI systems

But prompts fail when:

Consistency is required—the system must produce reliable, repeatable results
Systems must be reliable—behavior must be predictable and trustworthy
Outputs must be structured—results need to be processed by other systems

Problems with prompts include:

Ambiguity—multiple interpretations are possible
Lack of constraints—no boundaries on what the system can produce
Unstable results—different runs produce different outputs


Prompts are conversational. Instructions are operational.

For building production systems, we need instructions, not prompts.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.5 Instruction as Execution Protocol
Instruction should be understood as an execution protocol—a formal set of rules that govern how a system should behave.
A protocol defines:

The format of communication—how information is exchanged
The rules of interaction—what operations are permitted and how they should be performed
The expected behavior—what the system should do in different situations



8.5.1 An Instruction Protocol Typically Includes:

Task definition—clear statement of what should be accomplished
Input schema—precise specification of input format and structure
Output schema—precise specification of output format and structure
Constraints—rules that must be followed during execution
Optional steps or procedures—algorithms or workflows that should be followed


This protocol-based approach makes the system:

Predictable—behavior follows defined rules
Reusable—the same instruction can be used multiple times
Composable—instructions can be combined into larger systems

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.6 Structure of a Good Instruction
A well-designed instruction has several key components that work together to ensure reliable execution.


8.6.1 1. Task
What should be done? This is the core operation the system must perform.
Example: > “Classify the sentiment of the input text”
The task should be specific and unambiguous.



8.6.2 2. Input
What data is provided? This specifies what the system will work with.
Example: > {text: string, language: string}
The input schema should be precise and include all relevant information.



8.6.3 3. Output
What format is expected? This defines the structure of the results.
Example: > {sentiment: "positive" | "negative" | "neutral", confidence: number}
The output schema should be structured and match the needs of downstream systems.



8.6.4 4. Constraints
What rules must be followed? These are the boundaries within which the system must operate.
Example: > - Use only one sentiment label > Do not add explanation or justification > - Confidence must be between 0 and 1
Constraints ensure that the system produces appropriate results.



8.6.5 5. Evaluation (optional but critical)
How do we know it is correct? This defines how to assess the quality of the output.
Example: > - Output must match one of the predefined categories > - Confidence should correlate with human judgment > - Processing time should be under 1 second
Evaluation criteria provide a basis for improving the instruction.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.7 Determinism vs Flexibility
Instructions operate in a challenging space between two extremes:

Deterministic systems—strict rules that always produce the same output
Probabilistic systems—LLMs that generate variable outputs based on probability



8.7.1 Too vague

Leads to unpredictable outputs
Behavior varies widely between runs
System cannot be relied upon for consistent results




8.7.2 Too rigid

Reduces the strengths and capabilities of the model
Prevents the system from handling novel situations
Makes the instruction brittle and fragile




8.7.3 A good instruction

Constrains structure—specifies the format and organization of outputs
Allows flexibility in generation—lets the model use its capabilities within those structural constraints



Define the boundaries, not every step.

The art of instruction design is finding the right balance between structure and flexibility.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.8 Instruction as Interface
Instruction is the interface between:

Human-defined systems—specs, constraints, and requirements
Machine-executed behavior—the actual execution performed by LLMs and agents

It plays a role similar to:

APIs in traditional systems—defining how to interact with a component
Function signatures in programming—specifying inputs, outputs, and behavior



8.8.1 Example Analogy
In traditional programming:

Spec = what a function should do (the contract)
Instruction = how to call the function (the invocation)

The spec defines the behavior, while the instruction enables execution.

This interface-based perspective makes instructions:

Modular—can be developed, tested, and improved independently
Reusable—can be used in multiple contexts and systems
Composable—can be combined into larger workflows

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.9 Composing Instructions
Complex systems are not built from a single instruction. They are composed of multiple instructions that work together.


8.9.1 Example
A document processing pipeline may include multiple instructions:

Retrieve data

Task: Fetch document from storage
Input: {document_id: string}
Output: {content: string, metadata: object}

Process data

Task: Extract key information
Input: {content: string, metadata: object}
Output: {entities: Array<object>, topics: Array<string>}

Generate output

Task: Create summary
Input: {entities: Array<object>, topics: Array<string>}
Output: {summary: string, highlights: Array<string>}



Each step:

Has its own instruction
Produces structured output
Feeds into the next step


Systems are networks of instructions.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.10 Common Mistakes


8.10.1 1. Treating Instructions as Prompts

Leads to inconsistent and unpredictable behavior
Makes systems unreliable for production use

Instruction requires structure and precision, not conversational flexibility.



8.10.2 2. Ignoring Output Structure

Results become hard to use
Breaks downstream processes
Makes integration difficult

Output structure is essential for building reliable systems.



8.10.3 3. Missing Constraints

Outputs drift away from intended behavior
Quality degrades over time
System becomes unpredictable

Constraints define the boundaries within which the system must operate.



8.10.4 4. Overloading Instructions

Trying to do too much in one step
Reduces clarity and control
Makes debugging and improvement difficult

Each instruction should have a single, clear responsibility.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.11 Instruction and Control
In traditional programming, control is explicit:

Step-by-step execution
Precise control over every operation
Deterministic behavior

In instruction-based systems, control is indirect:

Through constraints—defining boundaries
Through structure—specifying formats
Through evaluation—measuring and correcting behavior



You do not control every step. You control the shape of execution.

This is a fundamental shift in how we think about control in software systems.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.12 Final Insight
Instruction is where definition becomes action. It is the point where:

Systems stop being conceptual descriptions
Systems begin to produce actual results

Without instruction, even the best spec remains a static document. With instruction, specs become operational systems.

title: 8  Instruction: Making It Executable
text:
8.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, writing instructions is not about asking better questions or crafting clever prompts. It is about defining executable protocols.

A well-defined instruction does not request behavior. It produces it.

The ability to design good instructions is now a core skill for system builders.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.1 Why Execution Is Not the End
In traditional software development, execution often marks completion. When code runs successfully and produces output, the task is often considered done:
This mental model made sense when implementation was the primary challenge. Getting code to run correctly was difficult enough that once it worked, we tended to move on.
In the LLM paradigm, this assumption no longer holds. Execution produces a result, not a guarantee of correctness or quality.
Because:
Therefore:

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
The code compiles without errors
The system produces output
The task is considered complete





Outputs are probabilistic—the same input can produce different outputs
Behavior depends on input variation—slightly different inputs can lead to very different results
Systems interpret rather than strictly follow instructions—LLMs use semantic understanding, not literal rule-following



Execution is not the end of the process. It is the beginning of validation.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.2 What is Evaluation?
Evaluation is the process of determining whether the system’s output satisfies the original intent and specification. It is the mechanism that closes the loop and enables systematic improvement.
Evaluation answers critical questions:

Is the result correct?—does it achieve the intended goal?
Does it meet the constraints?—does it respect all specified boundaries?
Does it achieve the intended outcome?—does it solve the problem we set out to solve?

Evaluation connects output back to definition, creating the feedback loop necessary for systematic improvement.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.3 Why Evaluation Is Necessary
Without evaluation, system development is fundamentally flawed:

There is no notion of correctness—any output is acceptable
There is no direction for improvement—changes are random rather than purposeful
There is no system stability—behavior drifts without correction



9.3.1 In the LLM Era
The need for evaluation is even greater because execution is so easy:

Systems produce outputs quickly and cheaply
Iterations happen rapidly and frequently
It’s tempting to move quickly without measuring results

But without evaluation:

Iteration becomes random—you change things without knowing whether they help
Improvements are accidental—you might get better results, but you don’t know why
Quality cannot be controlled—you have no way to ensure consistency or reliability


Speed without evaluation leads to chaos, not progress.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.4 Types of Evaluation
Evaluation can take multiple forms depending on the system, the context, and the requirements. Different types of evaluation serve different purposes.


9.4.1 1. Rule-Based Evaluation
Rule-based evaluation uses explicit, deterministic rules to assess outputs. It is simple, fast, and unambiguous.
Examples: - Output must be valid JSON - Response time must be under 2 seconds - Output must contain all required fields
Characteristics: - Deterministic—the same evaluation always produces the same result - Fast—can be executed automatically at scale - Limited—cannot assess qualitative aspects like relevance or quality



9.4.2 2. Human Evaluation
Human evaluation relies on subjective judgment to assess outputs. It can capture nuanced aspects that automated methods cannot.
Examples: - Is the answer helpful? - Does it align with user expectations? - Is the tone appropriate?
Characteristics: - Subjective—different humans may evaluate differently - Context-aware—can understand subtle aspects of meaning and appropriateness - Expensive—requires human time and attention - Non-scalable—difficult to apply to large volumes of data



9.4.3 3. Model-Based Evaluation
Model-based evaluation uses another AI model to assess the output. This can provide automated evaluation of qualitative aspects.
Examples: - Score relevance on a scale of 1-10 - Detect hallucinations or factual errors - Assess writing quality or clarity
Characteristics: - Automated—can be applied at scale - Nuanced—can assess qualitative aspects - Imperfect—the evaluating model may have its own biases and limitations - Requires validation—the evaluating model itself must be validated



9.4.4 4. Hybrid Evaluation
Hybrid evaluation combines multiple evaluation methods to get a more complete picture of output quality.
Examples: - Rule-based checks for structure + human review for quality - Model-based scoring for relevance + rule-based checks for format - Multiple evaluators (human and automated) with disagreement resolution
Characteristics: - Comprehensive—captures multiple dimensions of quality - Complex—requires integrating different types of feedback - More reliable—less likely to miss important issues


Strong systems rely on layered evaluation—combining multiple approaches to ensure comprehensive assessment.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.5 Evaluation Defines Quality
Quality is not an inherent property of output. It is defined by evaluation. Without evaluation criteria:

“Good” becomes subjective and variable
Systems drift over time without correction
Results cannot be compared or measured



9.5.1 Example
Consider an intent to “summarize a document.”
Without evaluation: - Any summary is acceptable - No way to determine if one summary is better than another - System may produce summaries that miss important information
With evaluation: - Must include key points from the original document - Must be concise (under 100 words) - Must avoid redundancy - Must preserve the original meaning
Now quality is defined and measurable. The system can be improved systematically by refining its performance against these criteria.


Evaluation makes quality measurable and therefore improvable.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.6 The Role of Iteration
Evaluation alone is not enough. It must lead to iteration—systematic improvement based on evaluation feedback.


9.6.1 Iteration Loop
Define → Execute → Evaluate → Refine
This loop is the engine of progress. Each cycle should move the system closer to the desired behavior.



9.6.2 Refinement typically occurs in:

Intent—clarifying or adjusting the goal if the original intent was wrong
Spec—adding missing constraints, clarifying ambiguous definitions
Instruction—improving the execution protocol for better results

The key insight is that most improvements happen upstream—by improving definitions, not by tweaking execution parameters.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.7 Where to Fix Problems
A critical principle for effective iteration:

Most problems should not be fixed in execution. They should be fixed upstream.



9.7.1 If output is wrong:
Do not immediately: - Change the model or model parameters - Add more prompts or instructions - Adjust temperature or other generation parameters
Instead, ask: - Is the intent clear? Are we solving the right problem? - Is the spec complete? Are we missing important constraints? - Is the instruction precise? Is the execution protocol clear?

Better definition produces better results.


This is a counterintuitive principle. When we see problems, our instinct is often to tweak the execution parameters. But most problems originate in definition, not execution.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.8 Programming as Spec Iteration
In traditional programming:

You write code
You debug code—find and fix errors
You optimize code—improve performance

The focus is on improving the implementation.
In the LLM paradigm:

You define spec
You refine spec—improve the definition based on evaluation
You iterate spec—systematically improve through evaluation

The focus is on improving the definition.


9.8.1 New Definition

Programming is the process of iteratively refining specifications until the system consistently produces correct results.


This shifts the developer’s activity from:

Debugging implementation—finding and fixing code errors

To:

Debugging definition—finding and fixing specification gaps

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.9 Stability Through Iteration
A system becomes stable when:

Outputs are consistent across multiple runs
Behavior matches expectations reliably
Evaluation passes consistently

This stability is not achieved through:

One-time design—getting it right the first time

But through:

Repeated refinement—systematic improvement through iteration



9.9.1 Convergence Condition
A system is considered stable when:

Outputs consistently meet success criteria
Variations are within acceptable bounds
Further iterations produce diminishing returns

Stability is an emergent property that comes from good iteration practices.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.10 Common Mistakes


9.10.1 1. Skipping Evaluation

Leads to false confidence—you think the system works but it doesn’t
Systems appear to work but fail in edge cases
No basis for improvement

Evaluation is not optional. It is essential.



9.10.2 2. Evaluating Informally

No clear criteria—evaluation is subjective and variable
Results cannot be compared—no baseline for improvement
Feedback is vague—“it doesn’t feel right”

Evaluation must be systematic and explicit.



9.10.3 3. Fixing the Wrong Layer

Adjusting execution instead of improving spec
Tweaking parameters instead of clarifying definitions
Wasting effort on the wrong problems

Most problems originate in definition, not execution.



9.10.4 4. Over-Iterating Without Direction

Changing things randomly without evaluation
No clear evaluation signal to guide improvements
Iteration becomes noise rather than progress

Iteration must be guided by evaluation.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.11 Evaluation as Feedback Signal
Evaluation provides the signal that drives improvement. Without evaluation, there is no learning, no convergence, no systematic progress.
Without evaluation: - There is no learning—system doesn’t know what’s working - There is no convergence—no way to approach optimal behavior - There is no improvement—changes are random rather than purposeful
With evaluation: - Systems can evolve—learning from feedback - Definitions become sharper—clarifying through iteration - Outputs improve over time—systematic progress toward goals


Evaluation turns iteration into progress.

Without evaluation, iteration is just change. With evaluation, iteration becomes improvement.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.12 Final Insight
Execution produces outputs. Evaluation determines value. Iteration produces improvement.
Together, they form the dynamic part of the system—the mechanism that enables systems to get better over time.

The quality of a system is not determined by how well it executes, but by how well it evaluates and iterates.

title: 9  Evaluation & Iteration
text:
9.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, programming is no longer about writing correct code once. It is about refining imperfect definitions until they become reliable.

You do not debug the system by changing what it does. You debug it by clarifying what it should do.

The ability to design effective evaluation and iteration processes is now a core programming skill.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.1 Why Possibility Comes First
Before defining what to build, one must first understand a fundamental question:
This seems obvious, yet it’s the most common barrier to progress.
Most people struggle with “not knowing what to do” not because they lack intelligence, creativity, or technical ability. They struggle because they lack exposure to the space of possibilities.
If you cannot see an option, you cannot choose it.
If you cannot imagine a structure, you cannot construct it.
If you don’t know what’s possible, your intent will always be constrained by your limited vision.
You cannot define what you do not know exists. You cannot build what you cannot imagine.
This is why understanding possibility space is foundational to everything that follows.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
What can be built at all?









The first step in Intent formation is not creativity.
It is recognition.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.2 What is a Possibility Space?
A Possibility Space is:

A structured space of all buildable goals, paths, and options within a domain.


It is not a flat list of ideas. It is a navigable structure that answers three fundamental questions:

What outcomes (Z) are possible? — What can we achieve?
What paths (A → Z) exist? — How do we get there?
What options exist at each step? — What choices do we have?


Think of it like a map. A map doesn’t just list destinations—it shows routes, intersections, and alternatives. It enables navigation.
A Possibility Space is the map of what’s buildable in a domain.

title: 10  Possibility Space
section: 10.6 The Expansion of Possibility in the LLM Era
text:
10.6 The Expansion of Possibility in the LLM Era
LLMs dramatically expand all three layers of possibility:


10.6.1 Goal Space expands

New products become possible—things that were impractical before
New interfaces emerge—natural language becomes a UI
New applications—AI enables new use cases

Example: Before LLMs, “personal research assistant” was impractical. Now it’s achievable.



10.6.2 Path Space expands

Agent systems—autonomous reasoning and action
RAG systems—knowledge retrieval and generation
Multi-step pipelines—complex processing flows
Human-AI collaboration—new interaction patterns

Example: New paths like “retrieve → reason → act” were not feasible before LLMs.



10.6.3 Option Space expands

Natural language control—specify behavior in language
Tool calling—access external capabilities easily
Hybrid workflows—combine AI and traditional systems
Rapid iteration—try many options quickly

Example: Instead of choosing between a few parsing tools, you can use LLM-based extraction, regex, NER, or hybrid approaches.


LLMs do not just improve execution. They expand the space of what can exist. They open new goals, paths, and options.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.7 The Problem of Invisible Options
A critical limitation in thinking:

Invisible possibilities


If a developer has only seen and worked with: - CRUD systems—create, read, update, delete operations - Databases—structured data storage - APIs—service interfaces
Then their solution space becomes constrained to: - Databases and tables - API endpoints - UI forms for data entry

They may never consider: - Agent systems that reason and act autonomously - Pipelines that process information in flows - AI workflows that combine generation and retrieval - Event-driven architectures that respond to changes


The constraint is not reality.
It is awareness.


Your limitation is not what’s possible—it’s what you can see. Expanding visibility expands possibility.

title: 10  Possibility Space
section: 10.5 Pattern Library: Structuring the Possibility Space
text:
10.5 Pattern Library: Structuring the Possibility Space
To make the Possibility Space usable, it must be organized.
This is where the concept of a Pattern Library becomes essential.
A Pattern Library is a structured collection of reusable system patterns.
It answers:

How are common problems typically solved?
What are the standard building blocks?
How can systems be composed?

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.9 What is a Pattern?
A Pattern is:

A reusable structure for moving from A to Z.


It is not: - Code—specific implementation details - Implementation—how to build it step by step
It is: - A form—the shape of the solution - A flow—how components connect - A logic structure—how the system operates

Patterns are abstract enough to apply across many situations, but specific enough to provide clear guidance.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.10 Core Pattern Categories
While there are many possible patterns, six core categories cover most system building needs:


10.10.1 1. CRUD Systems
The simplest pattern—managing structured data.
User → Database → UI
Use cases: User management, content management, data administration
Characteristics: Create, read, update, delete operations on structured data



10.10.2 2. Pipeline Systems
Sequential data processing.
Input → Transform → Output
Use cases: Data processing, ETL workflows, content transformation
Characteristics: Linear flow, each step transforms data, passes to next



10.10.3 3. Event-Driven Systems
Trigger-based execution.
Event → Trigger → Action
Use cases: Notifications, automation, reactive systems
Characteristics: Responds to events, asynchronous execution, loose coupling



10.10.4 4. Agent Systems
Autonomous or semi-autonomous decision-making.
Goal → Reason → Act → Observe → Refine
Use cases: Task automation, multi-step reasoning, autonomous operation
Characteristics: Goal-oriented, reasoning-based, adaptive behavior



10.10.5 5. Retrieval-Augmented Systems (RAG)
Knowledge plus generation.
Query → Retrieve → Generate
Use cases: Knowledge assistants, search systems, context-aware responses
Characteristics: Combines knowledge retrieval with language generation



10.10.6 6. Human-in-the-Loop Systems
Human and AI collaboration.
AI → Human Feedback → Refine
Use cases: Content review, decision support, iterative workflows
Characteristics: AI generates, human evaluates, system refines


These patterns define the available paths. They provide the structural vocabulary for system building.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.11 From Patterns to Systems
Real systems are rarely single patterns. They are compositions—combinations of multiple patterns working together.


10.11.1 Example: A Chat System
A comprehensive chat system might combine:

Agent pattern—for reasoning and response generation
Memory pattern—for maintaining conversation history
Retrieval pattern—for accessing knowledge bases
Event-driven pattern—for handling user interactions



Systems are combinations of paths and options. Patterns are the building blocks; composition creates the whole.

title: 10  Possibility Space
section: 10.9 How to Build Your Own Pattern Library
text:
10.9 How to Build Your Own Pattern Library
A Pattern Library is not something you memorize—it is something you build.

10.9.1 Step 1: Observe

Study existing systems
Analyze open-source projects
Break down real products




10.9.2 Step 2: Extract

Identify recurring structures
Remove implementation details
Keep only the core logic




10.9.3 Step 3: Categorize

Group similar patterns
Define clear boundaries
Create mental models




10.9.4 Step 4: Reuse

Apply patterns to new problems
Combine patterns into systems

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.13 Possibility as a Skill
Possibility Space is not static knowledge. It is:

The ability to see, structure, and navigate options.


This skill determines:

How fast you generate solutions—can you quickly see options?
How many alternatives you see—can you see multiple approaches?
How well you design systems—can you choose optimal paths?


Like any skill, it improves with practice. The more you build possibility spaces, the better you become at:

Recognizing patterns
Structuring problems
Navigating complexity

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.14 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes undermine effective use of possibility space:


10.14.1 1. Thinking from Implementation First
Starting with: > “How do I code this?”
Instead of: > “What kinds of systems could solve this problem?”
Impact: Constrained to familiar approaches, misses innovative solutions



10.14.2 2. Missing Path Awareness
Not knowing system structures and patterns.
Impact: Cannot decompose problems effectively, stuck on “how to start?”
Solution: Study patterns. Build a pattern library. Learn system architectures.



10.14.3 3. Limited Option Space
Only using familiar tools and methods.
Impact: Cannot optimize, cannot compare alternatives, stuck in suboptimal solutions
Solution: Explore tools beyond your current stack. Learn new methods. Understand trade-offs.



10.14.4 4. Confusing Possibility with Implementation
Trying to implement before understanding possibilities.
Impact: Premature commitment to suboptimal solutions
Solution: Map the full possibility space first, then choose the optimal path.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.16 Final Insight
Possibility Space defines:

The boundary of your thinking—what you can conceive

Pattern Library defines:

The structure of your thinking—how you organize what you conceive



You do not create from nothing.
You navigate, select, and compose from what is possible.


The quality of your systems is bounded by the quality of your possibility space. Expand your space, and you expand what you can build.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.13 Building a Possibility Space (Executable Method)
Understanding the Possibility Space is not enough.
It must be constructed deliberately.
This section provides a repeatable method that can be applied to any domain.


10.13.1 Step 0 — Define the Domain
Start with a clear problem domain.
Examples:

Build an AI product
Improve language skills
Design a knowledge system
Create a business

Do not start with a solution.
Start with a domain of action.



10.13.2 Step 1 — Decompose Using a Fixed Schema
To avoid vague thinking, always use the same structure.
Objects:      What entities exist in this domain?
Actions:      What can be done to these objects?
States:       What states can objects be in?
Flows:        What are the typical sequences of actions?
Constraints:  What limits or conditions exist?
This schema forces clarity.
It transforms intuition into structure.



10.13.3 Step 2 — Expand Aggressively (Without Judgment)
At this stage, do not optimize.
Do not filter.
Do not evaluate.
Only expand.
Example (AI System):
Objects:
  - user
  - data
  - model
  - interface
  - API

Actions:
  - collect
  - process
  - generate
  - evaluate
  - store
  - retrieve

States:
  - raw
  - processed
  - trained
  - deployed

Flows:
  - collect → process → train → deploy
  - input → generate → refine

Constraints:
  - cost
  - latency
  - accuracy
Goal:

Maximize coverage of the space.




10.13.4 Step 3 — Use External Expansion Sources
Individual knowledge is limited.
Expansion must be assisted.
Use four reliable sources:

10.13.4.1 1. Product Reverse Engineering
Analyze real systems:

What objects exist?
What actions are performed?
What flows are implemented?




10.13.4.2 2. Textbook Structures (Table of Contents)
Do not read details.
Extract:

Chapters
Sections
Concept hierarchy

These represent pre-structured possibility spaces.



10.13.4.3 3. Pattern Libraries
Use known system patterns:

CRUD
Pipeline
Event-driven
Agent
RAG
Feedback loop

Patterns expand your thinking beyond personal experience.



10.13.4.4 4. LLM-Assisted Expansion
Ask:

“What actions or system structures am I missing?”

LLMs act as a space expander, not a decision-maker.




10.13.5 Step 4 — Cluster and Structure
Raw lists are not usable.
Group elements into layers:
Data Layer:
  - collect
  - clean
  - store

Processing Layer:
  - transform
  - analyze
  - generate

Application Layer:
  - UI
  - API
  - interaction
Goal:

Convert chaos into navigable structure.




10.13.6 Step 5 — Extract the Skeleton
Reduce the system to its minimal form.
Example:
System Skeleton:
  Input → Process → Output → Feedback
A good skeleton is:

Simple
Generalizable
Reusable




10.13.7 Step 6 — Build a Possibility Map
The final result is not a list.
It is a map:

You can navigate it
You can select paths
You can combine structures

At this point, the domain becomes searchable by thought.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.14 Cross-Domain Possibility (Optional Extension)
Possibility Space becomes exponentially more powerful when combined across domains.
Method:
Select Domain A
Select Domain B

Check:
  - Shared objects
  - Transferable actions
  - Compatible flows
Example:

AI + Education → AI Tutor
AI + Writing → AI Writing System
AI + Data → Data Intelligence Platform

Innovation emerges from composition, not invention.

title: 10  Possibility Space
section: 10.15 Cognitive Model: What to Store in the Brain
text:
10.15 Cognitive Model: What to Store in the Brain
Do not attempt to memorize everything.
Store only:
Field:
  Purpose:   What problem does this domain solve?

Core Objects:
  Key entities

Core Actions:
  What is typically done

Core Models:
  3–5 essential theories or patterns
This is Skeleton-level cognition.
The brain stores structure.
External systems store detail.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.16 Daily Training Protocol
This skill must be trained.
A minimal daily exercise:
1. Choose one domain
2. Write:
   - 5 Objects
   - 10 Actions
   - 3 Flows

3. Analyze one real product
4. Extract its structure
5. Compress into a skeleton (3 lines)
Time required: 20–30 minutes.

title: 10  Possibility Space
text:
10.17 Final Principle
Possibility Space is not knowledge.
It is:

The ability to generate structured options.

The more structured your space,
the more powerful your decisions.
You do not think by guessing.
You think by navigating a constructed space.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.1 From Ideas to Structures
Having explored the Possibility Space and discovered what can be built, the next step is not to generate more ideas or expand the space further. The next step is to organize what we have discovered.
In the previous chapter, we introduced the three-layer model of possibility: Goal Space (what outcomes are possible), Path Space (what structures connect start to goal), and Option Space (what choices exist at each step). We saw that possibilities exist as a graph, not a linear path.
Now we must navigate that graph effectively. To do this, we need to transform the raw possibilities we’ve discovered into organized, workable structures.
Ideas alone are insufficient for building systems. They are:
To build effective systems, ideas must be transformed into structures. This transformation is the essence of structural thinking.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
Fragmented—scattered and disconnected thoughts
Unbounded—lacking clear boundaries or limits
Difficult to execute—cannot be directly translated into working systems



Structural thinking is the ability to organize components into a coherent system. It is the art of navigating the Path Space we discovered in the Possibility Space.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.2 What is Structural Thinking?
Structural thinking is the process of defining how a system is organized and how its parts work together. It is the bridge between having ideas and building systems.
Structural thinking involves:

How a system is organized—what components exist and how they relate
How its parts interact—how components communicate and coordinate
How information flows through it—how data moves and transforms

Structural thinking answers fundamental questions:

What are the main steps in the process?
What components are involved?
How does the system change over time?
What are the key relationships between parts?

Without structural thinking, we have ideas but no way to realize them. With structural thinking, we have the blueprint for building.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.3 The Three Core Elements
Any system, regardless of its complexity, can be described and understood using three fundamental elements. These elements provide a framework for structural thinking:

Main Flow—the primary sequence of actions that defines the system’s behavior
Modules—the components that perform specific functions within the system
State & Data Flow—how information moves through the system and what the system remembers

These three elements form the backbone of structural thinking. Mastering them allows you to understand and design any system.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
section: 11.4 Main Flow: The Backbone of the System
text:
11.4 Main Flow: The Backbone of the System
The main flow defines the primary path through the system—the sequence of actions that transforms inputs into outputs.


11.4.1 What is the Main Flow?
The main flow is:

What happens, in what order

It is the core narrative of the system—the story of how inputs become outputs.



11.4.2 Example
Consider a simple chat system. Its main flow might be:
User Input → Process Input → Generate Response → Display Output
This flow describes the primary sequence of actions from start to finish.



11.4.3 Characteristics of a Good Main Flow
A well-defined main flow should have:

Clear sequence—the order of steps is unambiguous
Minimal ambiguity—each step is well-defined
Covers the core use case—captures the primary path through the system




11.4.4 Why It Matters
Without a defined main flow:

Systems become fragmented—components operate independently without coordination
Components lack direction—there’s no clear order of operations
Execution becomes unpredictable—different runs may follow different paths

The main flow provides direction and coherence to the system.


The main flow provides direction. Without it, systems wander aimlessly.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.5 Modules: The Building Blocks
Modules are the components that perform specific functions within the system. They are the units of work that accomplish the tasks defined by the main flow.


11.5.1 What are Modules?
A module is a component that:

Has a clear responsibility—does one thing well
Operates on defined inputs—accepts specific data
Produces defined outputs—returns structured results




11.5.2 Example Modules in a Chat System
A chat system might include these modules:

Input Handler—receives and validates user input
Context Manager—maintains conversation history and context
Response Generator—creates responses based on context
Output Renderer—formats and displays responses to the user




11.5.3 Principles of Good Modules
Effective modules follow key principles:

Single responsibility—each module does one thing
Clear boundaries—each module has a well-defined interface
Reusable—modules can be used in different contexts




11.5.4 Why It Matters
Without modularization:

Systems become monolithic—everything is tangled together
Changes are difficult—modifying one thing breaks others
Reuse is limited—code cannot be shared or repurposed

Modules provide structure and make systems manageable.


Modules provide structure. Without them, systems are chaotic and unmanageable.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.6 State: What the System Knows
State represents the information that the system maintains over time. It is the system’s memory—what it knows and remembers from past interactions.


11.6.1 What is State?
State answers:

What does the system remember?

It includes:

Current session data
User information and preferences
Historical information
Intermediate results




11.6.2 Examples of State
In a chat system:

Conversation history—previous messages in the current conversation
User preferences—settings and choices the user has made
Current session data—temporary information about the active session




11.6.3 Types of State
State can be categorized in several ways:

Temporary (session-based)—information that persists only during a single interaction
Persistent (long-term)—information that is stored and accessed across sessions




11.6.4 Why It Matters
Without state:

Systems cannot maintain continuity—each interaction is isolated
Behavior becomes stateless and limited—systems cannot remember or learn
Complex workflows become impossible—multi-step processes cannot be supported

State enables systems to be adaptive and context-aware.


State provides memory. Without it, systems are forgetful and reactive.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.7 Data Flow: How Information Moves
Data flow describes how information moves between modules within the system. It defines the pathways through which data travels and transforms.


11.7.1 What is Data Flow?
Data flow defines:

Where data originates—its sources and inputs
How it is transformed—the processing it undergoes
Where it is consumed—its destinations and uses




11.7.2 Example Data Flow
In a chat system:
User Input → Input Handler → Context Manager → Response Generator → Output
Data flows from the user through each module, being transformed at each step.



11.7.3 Characteristics of Good Data Flow
Effective data flow should be:

Explicit—it’s clear where data comes from and where it goes
Traceable—you can follow data through the system
Consistent—data flows in predictable patterns




11.7.4 Why It Matters
Without clear data flow:

Systems become opaque—you can’t understand how data moves
Debugging becomes difficult—you can’t trace where problems originate
Errors propagate unpredictably—you can’t isolate issues

Data flow provides clarity and makes systems understandable.


Data flow provides clarity. Without it, systems are opaque and mysterious.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.8 Putting It Together
A complete structural view combines all three elements—main flow, modules, and data flow—into a coherent picture of how the system works.


11.8.1 Example System
Consider a chat system with this structure:
Main Flow:
User Input → Generate Response → Output
Modules: - Input Handler - Context Manager - Response Generator
State: - Conversation History
Data Flow:
Input → Handler → Context → Generator → Output

This representation allows you to:

Understand the system at a glance
Identify missing components
Improve clarity before implementation
Communicate the design to others

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.9 Structural Thinking vs Implementation Thinking
The shift to structural thinking represents a fundamental change in how we approach system design.


11.9.1 Implementation Thinking
Focuses on:

Code—writing the actual implementation
Tools—choosing frameworks and libraries
Syntax—mastering programming languages

Implementation thinking is necessary but not sufficient. It focuses on the “how” without first establishing the “what” and “why.”



11.9.2 Structural Thinking
Focuses on:

System design—understanding the overall architecture
Relationships—how components connect and interact
Flow and state—how information moves and what is remembered

Structural thinking operates at a higher level of abstraction. It focuses on the “what” and “why” before addressing the “how.”


Implementation builds details. Structure defines the system.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.10 Common Mistakes


11.10.1 1. Skipping Structure
Jumping directly to coding without defining:

Flow—what happens in what order
Modules—what components are needed
State—what information is maintained

This leads to tangled, hard-to-maintain code.



11.10.2 2. Mixing Concerns
Creating modules without clear boundaries:

Overlapping responsibilities—modules do too many things
Hard to maintain—changes ripple through the system
Difficult to test—each module is hard to isolate




11.10.3 3. Ignoring State
Treating systems as stateless when they require memory:

Can’t maintain context—each interaction is isolated
Limited functionality—can’t support complex workflows
Poor user experience—systems seem forgetful




11.10.4 4. Implicit Data Flow
Making data movement unclear or hidden:

Hard to understand—data appears and disappears mysteriously
Difficult to debug—can’t trace where problems occur
Errors propagate—issues spread without clear paths

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.11 Structural Thinking as a Skill
Structural thinking is not a natural talent—it is a skill that can be developed and improved through practice.
It requires:

Breaking systems into components—identifying the natural divisions in a system
Mapping flows explicitly—drawing or describing how information moves
Identifying state transitions—understanding how the system changes over time

The more systems you analyze and build, the stronger this skill becomes. With practice, you can look at a complex system and quickly understand its structure.

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.12 Final Insight
Structure is what makes a system understandable, buildable, and scalable. Without structure:

Intent remains abstract—ideas cannot be realized
Spec becomes incomplete—requirements are missed
Execution becomes unreliable—systems are unpredictable

With structure:

Ideas become systems—abstractions become concrete
Specifications become actionable—requirements guide implementation
Execution becomes predictable—systems behave reliably

title: 11  Structural Thinking
text:
11.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the ability to define structure is as important as the ability to define intent. Both are essential for building effective systems.

A clear structure turns ideas into systems.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.1 From Structure to System
After defining structure—main flow, modules, and data flow—we have a blueprint for how a system should be organized. But a blueprint is not a building. A specification is not a system.
Recall from our exploration of Possibility Space that the Option Space represents “what choices exist at each step.” When we navigate the possibility graph, we face countless decision points. Composition is the art of making those choices effectively.
The next step is:
Structure tells us what exists and how parts relate. Composition determines how those parts actually work together to produce behavior. It is how we navigate the Option Space and make the right choices at each intersection.
Without composition:

title: 12  Composition
text:
Composition — combining components into a working system




Modules remain isolated—they exist but don’t interact
Capabilities remain unused—potential is unrealized
Systems never emerge—we have parts but no whole
Possibilities remain unrealized—the Option Space is explored but not actualized

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.2 What is Composition?
Composition is the process of:

Combining smaller units of capability into a larger, functional system

These units can take many forms:

Modules—independent components with defined interfaces
Functions—reusable pieces of logic
Agents—autonomous systems that can reason and act
Services—external capabilities accessed through APIs
Patterns—repeated structures and approaches

Composition answers fundamental questions:

Which components are needed to achieve the goal?
How do they connect and communicate?
In what order do they operate?

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.3 Capability as a Unit
To understand composition, we must first define a key concept: capability.


12.3.1 What is a Capability?
A capability is something a system can do—a unit of functionality that produces a specific result.
Examples: - Generate text—create written content - Retrieve information—find and fetch data - Store data—save information for later use - Classify input—categorize or label data
Each module, agent, or service provides one or more capabilities.



12.3.2 Example
A language model provides several capabilities:

Text generation—create written content
Summarization—condense longer texts
Reasoning—draw inferences and conclusions

A database provides different capabilities:

Storage—save and persist data
Retrieval—find and fetch stored data
Querying—search and filter data



Systems are built by combining capabilities, not by writing everything from scratch.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.4 From Components to Systems
A system is not a single capability. It is a composition of capabilities—multiple units working together to achieve a goal that no single capability could accomplish alone.


12.4.1 Example: Chat System
Consider building a chat system. No single capability can provide all the required functionality. We need to combine multiple capabilities:
Capabilities involved: - Input handling—receive and validate user messages - Context storage—maintain conversation history - Response generation—create relevant replies - Output rendering—format and display responses



12.4.2 Composition
User Input
→ Input Handler (capability: input handling)
→ Context Manager (capability: context storage)
→ Response Generator (capability: response generation)
→ Output Renderer (capability: output rendering)

Each component contributes a capability. Together, they form a complete system. None of the components alone could provide the full functionality—the system emerges from their composition.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.5 Types of Composition
There are several common patterns for combining components into systems. Different patterns create different system behaviors.
Remember that in the Possibility Space, we navigate a graph where each node represents a decision point. These composition patterns represent the different ways we can traverse that graph—the different paths we can choose from the Option Space.


12.5.1 1. Sequential Composition
Components operate in sequence, with each component’s output feeding into the next.
Structure:
A → B → C
Used for: - Pipelines—data processing workflows - ETL—extract, transform, load operations - Step-by-step processing
Example:
Raw Text → Cleaner → Processor → Analyzer → Results



12.5.2 2. Parallel Composition
Components operate simultaneously on different aspects of the problem.
Structure:
A
↘
  → Merge → Output
↗
B
Used for: - Multi-source processing—handling multiple inputs - Aggregation—combining results from different sources - Distributed computing—parallel execution
Example:
Input → [Analyzer A, Analyzer B, Analyzer C] → Aggregate → Result



12.5.3 3. Conditional Composition
Different paths are taken based on conditions or decisions.
Structure:
Input → Decision → A or B
Used for: - Routing—directing inputs to appropriate handlers - Decision systems—choosing between alternatives - Branching logic—if-then-else flows
Example:
Input → Classify → [Text Handler OR Image Handler] → Output



12.5.4 4. Iterative Composition
Components repeat until a condition is met or convergence is achieved.
Structure:
Process → Evaluate → [Repeat if needed]
Used for: - Optimization—iteratively improving results - Refinement loops—progressively clarifying outputs - Convergence algorithms—repeating until stable
Example:
Draft → Evaluate → Refine → [Repeat until quality threshold met]


Different compositions create different system behaviors. The choice of composition pattern is a key design decision.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.6 Composition in the LLM Era
LLMs introduce new forms of composition that go beyond traditional code-based approaches.


12.6.1 Agent-Based Composition
Multiple agents work together, each with a specialized role and responsibility.

Multiple agents with different capabilities
Each agent has a defined role
Coordinated interaction toward a shared goal

Example:
User Query → Planner Agent → Researcher Agent → Writer Agent → Synthesizer Agent → Output



12.6.2 Tool-Augmented Composition
LLMs are combined with external tools and services to extend their capabilities.

LLM + external tools
Retrieval systems, APIs, databases
Enhanced capabilities through integration

Example:
Query → LLM → [Search Tool, Database API, Calculator] → LLM → Output



12.6.3 Human-in-the-Loop Composition
AI and humans work together in an iterative workflow.

AI generates initial output
Human evaluates or corrects
System refines based on feedback

Example:
AI → Human Review → Corrections → AI Refinement → Final Output


Composition is no longer limited to code. It includes models, tools, and humans working together.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.7 Interfaces Between Components
For composition to work effectively, components must connect properly. This requires interfaces—well-defined contracts that specify how components interact.


12.7.1 What is an Interface?
An interface defines:

Input format—what data a component accepts
Output format—what data a component produces
Expected behavior—how the component should respond




12.7.2 Example
If one module outputs:
{summary: "Text summary...", key_points: ["Point 1", "Point 2"]}
The next module must accept that exact format as input:
input: {summary: string, key_points: Array<string>}


Composition fails when interfaces are unclear or mismatched.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.8 Loose vs Tight Coupling
A critical design decision in composition is how tightly components are coupled to each other.


12.8.1 Tight Coupling
Components depend heavily on each other’s internal details.

Components are interdependent
Hard to modify or replace
Changes ripple through the system
Difficult to test independently




12.8.2 Loose Coupling
Components interact through well-defined interfaces but remain independent.

Clear interfaces define interactions
Components are independent
Easy to replace or extend
Can be tested independently



Good composition favors loose coupling. It makes systems more flexible and maintainable.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.9 Emergence of Systems
A key insight about composition:

Systems are not explicitly written—they emerge from composition.

When components are properly combined:

New behavior appears that no single component possesses
Capabilities amplify each other
Complexity becomes manageable through structure



12.9.1 Example
Combining three simple capabilities:

Retrieval—find relevant information
Generation—create written content
Memory—store and retrieve past interactions

Creates:

A knowledge assistant—can answer questions using retrieved knowledge, remember previous conversations, and generate coherent responses

None of the individual capabilities could do this alone. The knowledge assistant emerges from their composition.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.10 Common Mistakes


12.10.1 1. Overloading Components

One module does too much—violates single responsibility
Hard to reuse—tangled capabilities are difficult to extract
Hard to maintain—changes affect multiple concerns

Solution: Break large components into smaller, focused ones.



12.10.2 2. Ignoring Interfaces

Data mismatch between components
Integration failures and errors
Systems that don’t work together

Solution: Explicitly define and document all interfaces.



12.10.3 3. Overcomplicating Composition

Too many components—unnecessary complexity
Unnecessary indirection—components that don’t add value
Hard to understand and debug

Solution: Use the simplest composition that achieves the goal.



12.10.4 4. Lack of Clear Flow

No defined order of operations
Unpredictable behavior
Components don’t coordinate properly

Solution: Explicitly define the flow and ensure components follow it.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.11 Composition as a Skill
Composition is a higher-level skill than implementation. It requires:

Understanding capabilities—knowing what each component can do
Designing connections—creating effective interfaces
Managing interactions—ensuring components work together properly

The better your composition skills:

The more powerful your systems—complex behaviors emerge from simple parts
The less code you need to write—you reuse and compose existing capabilities
The faster you can build—you assemble systems rather than building from scratch

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.12 Final Insight
You do not build systems by writing everything from scratch. You build systems by combining the right parts in the right way.

The power of a system lies not in its components, but in how they are composed.

A master composer can create remarkable systems from simple parts, while a novice might struggle even with sophisticated components. The difference is composition.

title: 12  Composition
text:
12.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, programming is increasingly about assembling capabilities into coherent systems.

From components to systems, composition is the bridge.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.1 From Complexity to Clarity
In our exploration of Possibility Space, we identified Goal Space as the first layer—the set of all possible outcomes (Z) we might aim for. We discovered that many people struggle with “I don’t know what to build” precisely because they cannot see the Goal Space clearly.
But even when we can see the Goal Space, we face another challenge: the goals we discover are often vague, complex, and unstructured. They capture an intuition or aspiration but lack the precision necessary for system development.
Consider these common initial goals:
These statements feel meaningful and inspiring. They communicate a general direction and aspiration. But they are not actionable. They cannot guide system development effectively.
To build systems, we must do more than identify possible goals from the Goal Space. We must transform them. They must move from vague aspirations to precise, testable definitions.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
“Build something useful”
“Improve user experience”
“Make it smarter”
“Enhance productivity”




Goal Compression is the process of turning vague intentions into precise, testable definitions.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.2 What is Goal Compression?
Goal compression is the act of:

Reducing ambiguity while preserving intent

It does not simplify the goal by removing meaning or value. Instead, it simplifies by:

Eliminating vagueness—making implicit assumptions explicit
Defining boundaries—specifying what is included and excluded
Making outcomes observable—defining how success can be measured



13.2.1 Key Idea
A compressed goal is:

Clear—unambiguous and specific
Bounded—has defined scope and limits
Verifiable—can be tested against objective criteria

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.3 Why Compression is Necessary
Without goal compression, the entire system development process is compromised:

Intent remains unclear—the team doesn’t agree on what they’re building
Spec becomes inconsistent—requirements conflict or contradict each other
Instruction becomes unstable—execution produces unreliable results
Evaluation becomes subjective—there’s no way to measure success

In other words:

The entire system becomes unreliable and difficult to manage.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.4 From Vague to Verifiable
Goal compression transforms goals along a spectrum, moving them from vague inspiration to precise, testable specification:
Vague → Clear → Structured → Verifiable


13.4.1 Example Transformation
Consider the goal: “Build a smart assistant”
Vague: > “Build a smart assistant.”
This captures the aspiration but provides no guidance for implementation.
Clear: > “Build an assistant that answers user questions.”
Better—we now know what the system should do. But still not specific enough.
Structured: > “Build a system that answers user questions using retrieved knowledge.”
Good—we now understand the mechanism. But still missing important details.
Verifiable: > “Given a user query, the system retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and generates an answer grounded in those documents, with a response time under 2 seconds and an accuracy of at least 80% as measured by human evaluators.”
Now we have a goal that can guide implementation and be tested for success.


The final stage allows evaluation and systematic improvement.

title: 13  Goal Compression
section: 13.5 The Core Dimensions of a Clear Goal
text:
13.5 The Core Dimensions of a Clear Goal
A well-compressed goal typically defines five essential dimensions. These dimensions provide a complete picture of what the goal entails.


13.5.1 1. Objective
What outcome is expected? What is the primary purpose?
This defines the “what” of the goal—the core result that should be achieved.
Example: > “Answer user questions”



13.5.2 2. Object
What is being acted upon? What entity or domain is the target?
This defines the scope and focus of the effort.
Example: > “User queries”



13.5.3 3. Context
Under what conditions does the goal apply? What is the environment or situation?
This provides the boundaries within which the system must operate.
Example: > “Within a web application with 1000+ concurrent users”



13.5.4 4. Constraints
What limits exist? What boundaries must be respected?
Constraints define both what must be done and what must not be done.
Example: > “Response time < 2 seconds, 95% accuracy”



13.5.5 5. Success Criteria
How do we measure success? What indicates that the goal has been achieved?
Success criteria provide the basis for evaluation and improvement.
Example: > “Answers are relevant and grounded in retrieved documents, as measured by human evaluation”


Without these dimensions, a goal is incomplete and cannot effectively guide system development.

title: 13  Goal Compression
section: 13.6 Compression as Reduction of Degrees of Freedom
text:
13.6 Compression as Reduction of Degrees of Freedom
Another way to understand goal compression is in terms of reducing the number of possible interpretations.


13.6.1 Before Compression

Many possible meanings—the goal can be interpreted in multiple ways
Many possible outputs—the system could produce many different results
High uncertainty—it’s unclear what the right approach is




13.6.2 After Compression

Narrowed interpretation—the goal has a single, clear meaning
Constrained outputs—the system has limited freedom in what it produces
Reduced uncertainty—the path forward is clear


This is critical for systems:

Systems cannot choose among infinite interpretations—they need explicit guidance
They require bounded spaces to operate effectively
Ambiguity leads to unpredictable and unreliable behavior

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.7 Compression vs Over-Specification
There is an important distinction between:

Compressing a goal—removing ambiguity while preserving intent
Over-constraining a goal—adding unnecessary restrictions that limit flexibility



13.7.1 Compression

Removes ambiguity—makes the goal clear and specific
Defines boundaries—specifies what is included and excluded
Preserves intent—maintains the original purpose and value




13.7.2 Over-Specification

Fixes too many details—specifies implementation choices unnecessarily
Limits flexibility—reduces the system’s ability to adapt
Adds constraints without purpose—creates unnecessary complexity




13.7.3 Example
Compressed: > “Generate a summary under 100 words.”
This specifies a clear constraint without dictating the content or style.
Over-specified: > “Use exactly 3 sentences, each with 12–15 words, using formal academic tone, beginning with a clear topic sentence.”
This specifies too many details and unnecessarily limits the system’s flexibility.


Good compression clarifies. Over-specification restricts unnecessarily.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.8 Techniques for Goal Compression
Several practical techniques can help compress goals effectively:


13.8.1 1. Ask “What Does Success Look Like?”
Force yourself to define observable, measurable outcomes:

What will we see when the goal is achieved?
What will be different?
How will we know it works?

This forces specificity and creates evaluation criteria.



13.8.2 2. Identify the Transformation
Define the change the system should create:
Current State → Desired State
What is the situation now? What should it be after the system is deployed?



13.8.3 3. Remove Ambiguous Terms
Replace subjective or vague terms with specific, measurable ones:

“better” → “20% faster”
“fast” → “under 2 seconds”
“smart” → “accurate on 95% of test cases”




13.8.4 4. Add Constraints
Define limits explicitly:

Time constraints—“must complete within X seconds”
Format constraints—“must output JSON”
Scope constraints—“only process documents under 10MB”




13.8.5 5. Test for Interpretability
Ask yourself:

Can another person (or system) execute this without guessing?

If the answer is no, the goal needs more compression.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.9 Common Mistakes


13.9.1 1. Staying at the Idea Level

Never defining details—remaining abstract and vague
Remaining inspirational—failing to become actionable
Moving to implementation prematurely—without clarifying the goal




13.9.2 2. Mixing Goals with Implementation

Jumping to tools or frameworks—before clarifying what’s needed
Focusing on how to build—before understanding what to build
Losing focus on outcome—getting distracted by implementation details




13.9.3 3. Ignoring Evaluation

No way to verify success—no criteria for measurement
Can’t assess improvement—no baseline for comparison
Subjective assessment—relying on gut feel rather than data




13.9.4 4. Overcomplicating the Goal

Adding unnecessary details—cluttering the goal with irrelevant information
Reducing clarity—too much information obscures the core objective
Making it hard to remember—complex goals are difficult to communicate

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.10 Goal Compression as a Skill
Goal compression improves with practice. It is not a natural talent but a learnable skill.
It requires:

Clear thinking—the ability to distill complex ideas to their essence
Precision in language—choosing words carefully and specifically
Awareness of system behavior—understanding what systems need to execute effectively

The more you practice, the faster you can:

Move from vague to precise—compressing goals rapidly
Define systems effectively—creating actionable specifications
Communicate clearly—ensuring everyone understands the goal

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.11 Final Insight
Goal compression is the bridge between intention and specification. Without it:

Intent remains unusable—ideas cannot be realized
Spec cannot be formed—requirements cannot be specified
Systems cannot be built—there’s no clear direction

With it:

Intent becomes actionable—ideas can guide development
Spec becomes possible—requirements can be specified
Systems become buildable—there’s a clear path forward



You cannot build what you cannot define.

title: 13  Goal Compression
text:
13.12 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the ability to compress goals is a core capability. It is the foundation of effective system development.

The clearer the goal, the more reliable the system.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.1 From Concept to Discipline
In the previous chapters, we established the foundational elements of programming in the LLM era:
We also explored the three-layer model of Possibility Space: Goal Space (what outcomes are possible), Path Space (what structures connect start to goal), and Option Space (what choices exist at each step). We saw that navigating this space requires structure and precision.
This raises a critical question:
The answer is not ad hoc thinking or relying on intuition. It is not simply “being careful” or “thinking harder.” The answer is a disciplined approach:

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
Intent defines what we want—the human goal and aspiration
Spec defines what it means—the structured, precise specification of behavior
Instruction makes it executable—the protocol for system execution




How do we systematically create good specifications that effectively capture and navigate the Possibility Space?



Spec Engineering

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.2 What is Spec Engineering?
Spec Engineering is the practice of:

Designing, structuring, and refining specifications so that systems can reliably produce desired outcomes.

It is a systematic approach to creating specs that includes:

Translating intent into spec—converting human goals into structured definitions
Defining structure and constraints—specifying what the system must do and must not do
Eliminating ambiguity—making implicit assumptions explicit
Iteratively improving definitions—refining specs based on evaluation and feedback



14.2.1 Key Distinction
Spec Engineering is not:

Writing prompts—crafting natural language requests for AI systems
Writing code—implementing logic in programming languages

It is:

Defining systems in a structured, executable way—creating precise, testable specifications
Operating at the level of definition rather than implementation—focusing on what should exist

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.3 The Role of Spec
To understand Spec Engineering, we must first clarify the role of spec within the system and why it deserves focused attention.


14.3.1 Spec as the Central Layer
In the minimal loop:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation
Spec sits at the center of the loop, playing a critical bridging role.



14.3.2 It connects both sides

Upstream: human intent and understanding
Downstream: machine execution and behavior

Spec is the translation layer that converts human thinking into machine-executable form.



14.3.3 It transforms meaning

From semantic → structural: converting meaning into formal structure
From vague → explicit: making implicit assumptions clear and concrete
From idea → system: transforming abstract concepts into concrete definitions




14.3.4 Spec as Control Mechanism
In traditional programming:

Control is achieved through code—explicit instructions that define behavior
Developers write the logic that controls execution

In the LLM paradigm:

Control is achieved through specification—defining boundaries and constraints
Developers define the space within which the system operates



Spec defines the boundaries within which the system operates.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.4 Why Spec Engineering is Necessary
Without a systematic approach to spec creation, several problems emerge that undermine system development:

Definitions are inconsistent—different parts of the system have conflicting requirements
Systems behave unpredictably—outputs vary widely between runs
Iteration becomes inefficient—improvements are not cumulative

Spec Engineering introduces structure and discipline to address these problems:

Structure—standardized formats and approaches for creating specs
Repeatability—consistent quality across different projects and teams
Reliability—predictable system behavior based on clear definitions



14.4.1 The Problem Without Spec Engineering
When spec creation is ad hoc and unstructured:

Each task is handled differently—no consistent approach
No standard format—specs vary wildly in structure and detail
Hard to debug or improve—problems are difficult to isolate and fix




14.4.2 The Benefit With Spec Engineering
When spec creation is systematic and disciplined:

Reusable patterns—approaches that work across different contexts
Clear definitions—specs that are precise and unambiguous
Scalable systems—approaches that work as complexity grows

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.5 The Shift in Core Skills
In traditional programming, the core skill was:

Implementation—writing code that executes correctly

In the LLM era, the core skill shifts to:

Definition—creating precise, unambiguous specifications

More specifically:

The ability to engineer specifications



14.5.1 Why This Matters
Because:

Execution is increasingly automated—AI systems handle implementation
Systems depend on clear definitions—behavior emerges from specification
Quality is determined upstream—good specs lead to good systems

The limiting factor is no longer how well you can implement—it’s how well you can define.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
section: 14.6 Spec Engineering as a New Bottleneck Skill
text:
14.6 Spec Engineering as a New Bottleneck Skill
As discussed earlier in the book:

Execution has been outsourced—AI systems handle implementation
Definition has become the bottleneck—creating good specs is now the primary challenge

Spec Engineering is the discipline that addresses this bottleneck. It provides the systematic approach needed to overcome the new primary constraint.


14.6.1 Implication
The most valuable developers are those who can:

Define systems clearly—creating precise, unambiguous specifications
Structure specifications effectively—organizing complex requirements
Refine definitions iteratively—improving specs based on evaluation

The competitive advantage has shifted from implementation skill to definition skill.


The limiting factor is no longer how well you code, but how well you define.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.7 Spec as a First-Class Artifact
In this paradigm, spec is not a temporary step or a preliminary document. It becomes a central artifact of the development process.
It becomes:

A core artifact—as important as code, perhaps more so
A reusable asset—can be shared, improved, and applied across projects
A unit of iteration—the thing we refine to improve systems



14.7.1 Example
Instead of focusing on:

Code reuse—sharing and reusing code libraries

We begin to focus on:

Spec reuse—sharing and reusing specification patterns




14.7.2 Why This Matters

Specs can be shared—across teams, projects, and organizations
Specs can be improved—systematically refined based on experience
Specs can be applied across systems—similar specifications guide similar implementations

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.8 The Lifecycle of a Spec
Spec Engineering is not a one-time activity. It is an ongoing lifecycle that continues throughout the system’s existence.


14.8.1 1. Creation

From intent to initial spec—converting the human goal into a first draft specification




14.8.2 2. Execution

System produces output—the spec is used to guide system behavior




14.8.3 3. Evaluation

Compare output with expectations—assess whether the spec achieved the intended outcome




14.8.4 4. Refinement

Improve spec based on feedback—adjust and update the specification


Spec → Execution → Evaluation → Refine → Spec


Specs evolve through iteration. They are not static but living documents.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.9 Spec Engineering vs Traditional Engineering
The shift to Spec Engineering represents a fundamental change in how we approach software engineering.


14.9.1 Traditional Software Engineering

Focus on code structure—organizing code for maintainability
Optimize performance—making code run faster and more efficiently
Ensure correctness through implementation—testing and debugging code




14.9.2 Spec Engineering

Focus on definition structure—organizing specifications for clarity
Optimize clarity—making specs as unambiguous as possible
