Ensure correctness through specification—defining behavior precisely



The focus shifts from building systems to defining them.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.10 Common Misconceptions
Several misconceptions about spec and Spec Engineering can hinder effective practice:


14.10.1 1. “Spec is just documentation”
Incorrect.

Documentation describes—it explains what exists
Spec defines and constrains—it specifies what should exist and how it should behave

Spec is not a description of what was built—it is a prescription for what should be built.



14.10.2 2. “Spec is optional”
Incorrect.

Without spec, systems are unstable—behavior varies unpredictably
Without spec, systems are unreliable—outputs don’t meet requirements
Without spec, systems are unimprovable—there’s no basis for iteration

Spec is essential, not optional.



14.10.3 3. “Spec is static”
Incorrect.

Spec evolves through iteration—based on evaluation and feedback
Spec adapts to changing requirements—responding to new information
Spec improves over time—becoming clearer and more precise

Spec is dynamic, not static.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
section: 14.11 Spec Engineering as a System Design Discipline
text:
14.11 Spec Engineering as a System Design Discipline
Spec Engineering sits at the intersection of several disciplines:

System design—understanding how components work together
Language—using natural and formal language precisely
AI interaction—understanding how AI systems interpret and execute definitions

It requires a combination of skills:

Structural thinking—organizing complex requirements
Precision in language—choosing words carefully
Understanding of system behavior—knowing how systems will interpret specs

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.12 Final Insight
Spec Engineering is the discipline that enables reliable systems in the LLM era. It is the bridge between human intent and machine execution.
It is where:

Intent becomes usable—ideas can guide implementation
Systems become controllable—behavior is predictable and reliable
Iteration becomes meaningful—improvements are systematic and cumulative



If programming is defining systems, Spec Engineering is the craft of doing it well.

title: 14  What is Spec Engineering?
text:
14.13 Closing Statement
As execution becomes commoditized, definition becomes the source of leverage. Spec Engineering is not an optional skill—it is the new core of programming.

The future belongs to those who can define with precision.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.1 Why Structure Matters
A spec without structure is just text—a collection of words and ideas that may contain useful information but cannot reliably guide system execution.
A spec without structure:
To be effective, a spec must be:
This requires structure.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
May contain useful ideas—the concepts might be sound
May describe a system accurately—the understanding might be correct
But cannot reliably guide execution—the system cannot interpret it consistently



Interpretable—the system can understand it unambiguously
Consistent—different parts of the spec don’t conflict
Reusable—the spec can be applied across different contexts



Structure turns specification into an operational artifact.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.2 Structure Without Rigidity
A common mistake is to assume that a spec must follow a fixed, rigid format. This is not necessary—and can even be counterproductive.
Different systems may require:

Different levels of detail—some specs need more granularity than others
Different representations—some systems benefit from visual formats, others from text
Different formats—JSON, YAML, natural language, or hybrid approaches

However, all effective specs share a common underlying structure. The format may vary, but the essential elements are the same.

The goal is not a fixed template, but a consistent set of elements.

title: 15  Spec Structure
section: 15.3 The Core Components of a Spec
text:
15.3 The Core Components of a Spec
At a minimum, any effective spec must define four essential components. These components form the foundation of any executable definition.

Input—what the system receives
Output—what the system produces
Constraints—what the system must respect
Success Criteria—what counts as correct

These elements are not optional. Without all four, the spec is incomplete and cannot reliably guide execution.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.4 Input — What Goes In
Input defines:

What information the system receives



15.4.1 Examples

User query—a natural language question
Text document—the content to be processed
Structured data—JSON objects, arrays, or other formatted data
Configuration settings—parameters that control behavior




15.4.2 Why Input Matters
If input is unclear or undefined:

The system does not know what to operate on—leading to errors or unexpected behavior
Behavior becomes inconsistent—different interpretations lead to different results
Integration becomes difficult—other systems don’t know how to provide input




15.4.3 Good Input Definition
A well-defined input specification is:

Explicit—clearly states what data is required
Typed or structured when possible—specifies data types and formats
Clearly scoped—defines what is included and what is excluded



Clear input reduces ambiguity at the source—the system knows exactly what to work with.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.5 Output — What Comes Out
Output defines:

What the system produces



15.5.1 Examples

Generated text—the system’s written response
Classification label—a category or decision
Structured JSON—formatted data with specific fields
File output—a document or other file




15.5.2 Why Output Matters
If output is undefined or vague:

Results become unpredictable—the system may produce anything
Downstream systems break—other components can’t process the output
Evaluation becomes impossible—there’s no way to assess quality




15.5.3 Good Output Definition
A well-defined output specification is:

Structured—specifies the format and organization
Consistent—the same type of output every time
Aligned with use case—matches the needs of downstream systems



Output is the interface to the rest of the system—downstream components depend on it.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.6 Constraints — What Must Be Respected
Constraints define:

The boundaries within which the system operates



15.6.1 Examples

Performance constraints—“Response time < 2 seconds”
Length constraints—“Maximum output of 100 words”
Content constraints—“Must use retrieved documents when available”
Behavioral constraints—“No external knowledge beyond provided context”




15.6.2 Why Constraints Matter
Without constraints:

The system has too many degrees of freedom—outputs vary widely
Outputs drift away from intended behavior—quality degrades over time
The system may violate requirements—producing unacceptable results




15.6.3 Types of Constraints

Functional constraints—what must be included or achieved
Non-functional constraints—performance, format, resource limits
Behavioral constraints—tone, style, limits on behavior



Constraints shape behavior—they define the space within which the system must operate.

title: 15  Spec Structure
section: 15.7 Success Criteria — What Counts as Correct
text:
15.7 Success Criteria — What Counts as Correct
Success criteria define:

How we evaluate whether the system works



15.7.1 Examples

Content criteria—“Output must contain key points from the input”
Accuracy criteria—“Classification must be at least 90% accurate”
Relevance criteria—“Answer must be relevant to the user’s question”
Grounding criteria—“Claims must be supported by provided sources”




15.7.2 Why Success Criteria Matter
Without success criteria:

There is no definition of correctness—any output could be considered acceptable
Evaluation becomes subjective—different people have different opinions
Improvement is impossible—there’s no way to measure progress




15.7.3 Good Success Criteria
Effective success criteria are:

Observable—can be seen or measured
Measurable when possible—quantifiable and objective
Aligned with intent—directly connected to the original goal



Success criteria make quality testable—they provide the basis for evaluation and improvement.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.8 Putting It Together
A minimal spec can be expressed simply as:
Input → Output
+ Constraints
+ Success Criteria


15.8.1 Example
Consider a document summarization system:
Input: - Text document (string, max 10,000 words)
Output: - Summary (string) - Key points (array of strings, 3-5 items)
Constraints: - Summary must be under 150 words - Key points must be under 20 words each - Must preserve original meaning and tone - Must not introduce new information
Success Criteria: - Summary captures main ideas - Key points are accurate and relevant - Human evaluators rate quality >= 4/5

This structure is simple but sufficient for many systems. It provides everything needed for reliable execution and evaluation.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.9 Optional Extensions
Depending on complexity, a spec may include additional elements beyond the core four:

Context—the environment or scenario in which the system operates
Assumptions—what is being taken for granted
Edge cases—how the system should handle unusual inputs
Error handling—what happens when things go wrong

These extend the core structure but do not replace it. The core four components are always necessary.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.10 Spec as a Contract
A useful way to think about spec:

It is a contract between definition and execution.

The spec defines:

What is expected—the requirements and goals
What is allowed—the boundaries and constraints
What is considered correct—the success criteria



15.10.1 Implication
If execution fails:

Either the system is wrong—the implementation doesn’t meet the spec
Or the spec is incomplete—the spec doesn’t capture all requirements

Either way, the spec provides the basis for diagnosis and improvement.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.11 Balancing Completeness and Simplicity
A good spec balances two competing objectives:

Completeness—enough detail to guide execution effectively
Simplicity—not overloaded with unnecessary constraints



15.11.1 Too Simple

Leaves ambiguity—the system must guess at missing details
Leads to inconsistent results—different interpretations produce different outputs
Makes evaluation difficult—no clear basis for assessment




15.11.2 Too Complex

Hard to maintain—difficult to understand and update
Limits flexibility—unnecessarily constrains the system
Creates overhead—more information than needed for effective execution



The goal is sufficient clarity, not maximum detail.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.12 Common Mistakes


15.12.1 1. Missing Components

No clear output specification—results are unpredictable
No defined constraints—behavior drifts over time
No success criteria—evaluation is subjective or impossible

Solution: Ensure all four core components are present and well-defined.



15.12.2 2. Implicit Assumptions

Relying on unstated expectations—the system makes wrong assumptions
Hidden requirements—critical aspects are not documented
Unclear context—the system doesn’t understand the environment

Solution: Make all assumptions explicit and visible.



15.12.3 3. Mixing Levels

Combining intent, spec, and instruction in one layer—creating confusion
Mixing high-level goals with low-level details—losing clarity
Failing to separate concerns—making the spec hard to use

Solution: Maintain clear separation between intent, spec, and instruction.



15.12.4 4. Over-Specification

Defining unnecessary details—cluttering the spec with irrelevant information
Fixing implementation choices—constraining the system unnecessarily
Reducing flexibility—making the spec brittle and hard to adapt

Solution: Include only what is necessary for reliable execution.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.13 Final Insight
Spec structure is not about format—whether you use JSON, YAML, or natural language. It is about completeness and clarity.
If a spec clearly defines:

Input—what the system receives
Output—what the system produces
Constraints—what the system must respect
Success—how we know it works

Then it is usable, regardless of the specific format.

title: 15  Spec Structure
text:
15.14 Closing Statement
A well-structured spec turns ideas into executable definitions.

Structure is what makes specification reliable.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.1 Why Templates Exist
Once we understand what a good spec looks like—the core components, the need for structure, the importance of clarity—a practical question arises:
Without guidance and structure, spec creation becomes:
To address this systematic problem, we introduce:

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
How do we produce specs consistently?



Different people define specs differently—leading to inconsistency
Important elements are missed—critical components are forgotten
Quality varies widely—some specs are good, others are poor



Templates — structured guides for creating specs

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.2 What is a Template-based Spec?
A Template-based Spec is a specification created using a predefined structure. The template acts as a scaffold that guides the spec creation process.
The template defines:

What fields must be filled—the required components of the spec
What information is required—the type of content needed for each field
How the spec is organized—the structure and order of the specification



16.2.1 Example Template (Conceptual)
Objective:
Input:
Output:
Constraints:
Success Criteria:
The user fills in the template instead of starting from scratch, ensuring that all essential elements are included.


Templates standardize thinking. They make good spec creation a repeatable process.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.3 The Role of Templates
Templates serve multiple important functions in the spec creation process:

A checklist—ensuring no critical component is missed
A structure—organizing thinking in a systematic way
A constraint on thinking—forcing attention to what matters

Templates ensure that:

No critical component is missing—all necessary elements are included
Specs are consistent across tasks—different specs follow the same format
Quality is more predictable—standardization leads to better outcomes

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.4 Designing a Good Template
A good template is not just a form with blank fields to fill. It is a thinking framework—a tool that guides and structures the thought process.


16.4.1 Principles of Template Design
Effective templates follow key principles:


16.4.1.1 1. Completeness
The template should cover all essential elements:

Input—what the system receives
Output—what the system produces
Constraints—what the system must respect
Success criteria—how we know it works

If a template omits essential components, it cannot ensure complete specs.



16.4.1.2 2. Clarity
Each field in the template should:

Have a clear purpose—its role in the spec is obvious
Avoid ambiguity—what should be entered is clear
Include guidance—if helpful, provide examples or prompts




16.4.1.3 3. Simplicity

Avoid too many fields—don’t overwhelm the user
Keep it usable—the template should be easy to fill out
Focus on essentials—include only what’s necessary




16.4.1.4 4. Flexibility

Allow variation—adapt to different contexts and needs
Avoid over-constraining—don’t force unnecessary specificity
Enable extension—allow for additional fields when needed



A good template guides, but does not restrict. It provides structure without limiting creativity.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.5 Template Granularity
Templates can exist at different levels of detail, depending on the complexity of the task and the experience of the user.


16.5.1 High-Level Templates

Fewer fields—only the most essential components
More flexible—allows for interpretation and adaptation
Less prescriptive—provides guidance without excessive detail

Used for: - Early-stage exploration—when requirements are still evolving - Simple tasks—when complexity is low - Rapid prototyping—when speed is more important than precision



16.5.2 Detailed Templates

More fields—comprehensive coverage of all aspects
More constraints—specific guidance on what to include
More prescriptive—detailed instructions for each field

Used for: - Complex systems—when many components must be specified - Production environments—when reliability and consistency are critical - Teams with varying experience—when standardization is essential


The level of detail should match the complexity of the task and the needs of the context.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.6 Advantages of Template-based Spec
Using templates for spec creation provides several significant benefits:


16.6.1 1. Consistency

All specs follow the same structure—making them easier to understand and review
Standardized format—comparing specs across projects is straightforward
Reduced cognitive load—don’t need to figure out how to organize each spec




16.6.2 2. Efficiency

Faster to create specs—don’t start from scratch each time
Reduced mental effort—structure is provided, just fill in the details
Streamlined review process—reviewers know where to look for what information




16.6.3 3. Reusability

Templates can be reused across tasks—applying the same structure to different problems
Standardization enables scaling—consistent approach as the team grows
Knowledge capture—best practices are encoded in the template




16.6.4 4. Accessibility

Easier for beginners—provides guidance and structure
Reduces learning curve—new team members can follow the template
Helps build mental models—users learn what makes a good spec



Templates lower the barrier to good specification. They make it easier for everyone to create high-quality specs.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.7 Limitations of Templates
While templates are powerful tools, they have limitations that must be understood:


16.7.1 1. Rigidity

May not fit all scenarios—some problems don’t fit the template structure
Can constrain creative solutions—forcing problems into a predefined format
May miss unique aspects—template may not capture important domain-specific details




16.7.2 2. Over-Reliance

Users may fill fields mechanically—without deep understanding
Without understanding the system—just completing the form without thinking
False sense of completeness—filling all fields doesn’t guarantee a good spec




16.7.3 3. Incomplete Coverage

Templates cannot anticipate all edge cases—some situations require custom specifications
May miss domain-specific requirements—general templates may not capture specialized needs
Can become outdated—templates need to evolve as practices change




16.7.4 4. False Sense of Quality

A filled template does not guarantee a good spec—content matters more than format
Structure without substance—a well-structured spec can still be poor if the content is weak
Complacency—relying on templates without critical thinking



Templates guide structure, not thinking. They provide the form, but the substance comes from understanding.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.8 When to Use Templates
Templates are most effective in specific contexts. Knowing when to use them—and when not to—is important.


16.8.1 When to Use Templates
Templates are most effective when:


16.8.1.1 1. Tasks Are Repetitive

Similar structures across tasks—common patterns recur
Predictable requirements—what’s needed is known in advance




16.8.1.2 2. Teams Need Consistency

Multiple contributors—different people creating specs
Shared standards—ensuring everyone follows the same approach
Onboarding new team members—providing clear guidance




16.8.1.3 3. Systems Require Reliability

Production environments—consistency and predictability are critical
Stable outputs—reproducible behavior is essential
Quality requirements—high standards must be met consistently




16.8.1.4 4. Users Are Learning

Beginners need guidance—structure helps them learn
Building mental models—templates teach good spec practices
Training and development—providing scaffolding for skill development





16.8.2 When Not to Use Templates
Templates are less suitable when:


16.8.2.1 1. Problems Are Novel

No established structure—the problem doesn’t fit known patterns
High uncertainty—requirements are unclear or evolving
Exploratory work—figuring out what’s needed as you go




16.8.2.2 2. Exploration is Needed

Early-stage ideation—requirements are still being discovered
Rapid iteration—changing direction frequently
Research and experimentation—testing different approaches




16.8.2.3 3. Flexibility is Critical

Unique constraints—standard templates don’t capture the needs
Unusual system behavior—non-standard patterns are required
Creative solutions—rigid structure would limit innovation



Templates are tools, not rules. Use them when they help, avoid them when they hinder.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.9 When Not to Use Templates
Templates are less suitable when:


16.9.1 1. Problems Are Novel

No established structure
High uncertainty




16.9.2 2. Exploration is Needed

Early-stage ideation
Rapid iteration




16.9.3 3. Flexibility is Critical

Unique constraints
Unusual system behavior



Templates are tools, not rules.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.9 Templates as Scaffolding
A useful way to think about templates:

They are scaffolding for thinking.

Like scaffolding in construction, templates provide temporary support that helps you build something solid. Once the structure is in place and you understand what you’re doing, you may no longer need the scaffolding.
Templates help you:

Start with structure—you don’t have to figure out organization from scratch
Avoid missing key elements—the template reminds you what’s important
Develop consistency—following the same pattern builds good habits

But over time:

Internalize the structure—you no longer need the external support
Adapt as needed—you modify the template to fit specific situations
Move beyond templates—you can create good specs without them

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.10 Evolution of Templates
Templates are not static. They should evolve based on experience and feedback:

Based on experience—what works well, what doesn’t
Based on failures—what’s missing, what causes problems
Based on system requirements—what the team and projects need



16.10.1 Example Evolution
A template might evolve over time:

Add new fields—edge cases that were missed initially
Refine definitions—clarify what each field should contain
Remove unnecessary complexity—simplify based on actual usage
Add examples—provide guidance for each field



Good templates evolve with practice. They improve as we learn what works and what doesn’t.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.11 Final Insight
Template-based specs provide a practical way to:

Standardize definition—consistent approach across projects
Improve consistency—predictable quality and format
Scale spec creation—efficient as teams and projects grow

But they are only one approach within Spec Engineering. Templates are a tool, not the entire discipline.

title: 16  Template-based Spec
text:
16.12 Closing Statement
Templates do not replace thinking. They structure it.

A good template does not tell you what to think. It ensures that you think about what matters.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.1 From Manual Definition to Assisted Definition
In Template-based Spec, humans define specifications directly, using templates to guide their thinking. This approach works well when:
However, in many real-world scenarios, these conditions are not met:
This creates a challenge: how do we create good specs when human expertise is limited or the problem is poorly understood?
This leads to a new approach:

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
The problem is familiar—you understand the domain and requirements
The structure is clear—you know what components are needed
The user understands the system—you have the expertise to create good specs



The intent is vague—the goal is not yet clear or precise
The structure is unclear—you don’t know what components are needed
The user cannot easily translate ideas into specs—lacking expertise or experience




Using agents to generate specifications

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.2 What is a Spec Agent?
A Spec Agent is a system that:

Transforms human intent into a structured specification

It acts as an intelligent assistant that helps bridge the gap between vague human ideas and precise machine-executable specifications.
A Spec Agent takes as input:

Natural language descriptions of what you want
Partial ideas or fragments of requirements
Incomplete goals that need refinement

And produces:

A structured spec with defined inputs, outputs, constraints, and success criteria



17.2.1 Core Function
Intent (informal) → Spec Agent → Spec (structured)


The agent does not execute the system. It defines it.

This is a crucial distinction. A Spec Agent is not an implementation tool—it’s a definition tool. It helps you clarify what you want before you try to build it.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.3 Why Spec Agents Are Needed
Spec Engineering is cognitively demanding. It requires:

Precision—choosing words carefully to avoid ambiguity
Structure—organizing complex requirements into coherent form
Awareness of system behavior—understanding how systems interpret specifications

Many users struggle with these demands:

Missing constraints—forgetting important limitations or requirements
Unclear outputs—not specifying what results should look like
Weak evaluation criteria—not defining how to measure success

Spec Agents help by:

Filling gaps—identifying and adding missing components
Suggesting structure—proposing ways to organize the specification
Making implicit assumptions explicit—revealing hidden assumptions that should be stated



They reduce the cognitive load of definition, making good specs accessible to more people.

title: 17  Spec Agent
section: 17.4 The Shift: AI Writes the Spec
text:
17.4 The Shift: AI Writes the Spec
The evolution of AI in programming has followed a clear trajectory:
Traditionally: - Humans write code—manually implementing logic and behavior
Then: - Humans write prompts—crafting natural language requests for AI systems
Now: - AI can write specifications—generating structured definitions from intent


17.4.1 This is a critical shift
Instead of:

Using AI to generate output—code, text, or other results

We use AI to:

Define the system that generates output

This represents a move up the abstraction ladder. AI is no longer just doing the work—it’s helping us define what work should be done.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.5 The Spec Generation Process
A typical Spec Agent workflow follows a clear sequence of steps, with human oversight and refinement at key points.


17.5.1 Step 1: Input Intent
The user provides their intent in natural language, often as a rough or incomplete description.
Example: > “Build a system that summarizes articles.”
This is vague and incomplete, but it captures the general idea.



17.5.2 Step 2: Spec Generation
The agent analyzes the intent and produces a structured specification, including suggested components.
Generated Spec: - Input: Text article (string, max 5000 words) - Output: Summary (string, 100-200 words), Key points (array, 3-5 items) - Constraints: Must preserve main ideas, must not add new information - Success Criteria: Human evaluation score >= 4/5 for relevance and accuracy
The agent fills in gaps and proposes structure.



17.5.3 Step 3: Human Review
The user reviews the generated spec, making corrections and additions:

Editing the spec to better match their intent
Adding missing details or constraints
Refining the success criteria

This ensures the spec reflects human understanding and priorities.



17.5.4 Step 4: Final Spec
The refined spec is used for:

Instruction generation—creating executable protocols
System execution—guiding AI behavior
Evaluation—measuring success


Human Intent → Agent → Draft Spec → Human Refinement → Final Spec

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.6 Human–AI Collaboration Model
Spec Agents introduce a new interaction pattern between humans and AI:

Human + AI co-create the definition

This is not AI replacing human thinking, nor is it humans doing all the work alone. It’s a collaborative process that leverages the strengths of both.


17.6.1 Roles


17.6.1.1 Human

Defines high-level intent—provides the overall goal and direction
Provides context—shares domain knowledge and requirements
Makes final decisions—approves, modifies, or rejects the spec




17.6.1.2 Agent

Structures information—organizes vague ideas into coherent form
Suggests constraints—proposes boundaries and limitations
Identifies missing elements—finds gaps that need to be filled



The human defines meaning. The agent defines structure.

Together, they create specifications that neither could produce alone as effectively.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.7 Benefits of Spec Agents
Using Spec Agents provides several significant advantages:


17.7.1 1. Speed

Faster spec creation—generates initial structure in seconds
Reduced manual effort—less time spent organizing and structuring
Rapid iteration—quickly explore different specifications




17.7.2 2. Coverage

Fewer missing components—agent identifies required elements
More complete definitions—comprehensive coverage of requirements
Better structure—organized and coherent specifications




17.7.3 3. Consistency

Standardized structure—specs follow consistent patterns
Reduced variability—similar problems get similar specs
Improved quality—systematic approach reduces errors




17.7.4 4. Accessibility

Lower barrier for beginners—people new to spec creation can produce good specs
Enables non-experts—domain experts without technical training can define systems
Democratizes specification—more people can participate in system definition



Spec Agents democratize specification, making it accessible to a broader range of people.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.8 Limitations of Spec Agents
Despite their benefits, Spec Agents have important limitations that must be understood:


17.8.1 1. Dependence on Input Quality

Poor intent leads to poor spec—garbage in, garbage out
Vague input produces vague output—clarity begets clarity
Incomplete requirements create incomplete specs

Solution: Invest time in clarifying intent before using the agent.



17.8.2 2. Over-Trust

Users may accept generated specs without review—blind trust is dangerous
Hidden errors remain—spec may contain mistakes that aren’t caught
Loss of critical thinking—relying too much on the agent

Solution: Always review and refine generated specs—never accept them blindly.



17.8.3 3. Lack of Context

Agents may miss domain-specific constraints—general knowledge has limits
Cannot fully replace human judgment—context and nuance matter
May misunderstand specialized requirements

Solution: Provide detailed context and review carefully for domain-specific issues.



17.8.4 4. Generic Outputs

Specs may be too general—lacking specificity for unique situations
Lack depth—may not capture subtle but important requirements
Miss edge cases—unusual scenarios may be overlooked

Solution: Add domain-specific details and edge case handling manually.


Spec Agents assist, but do not replace thinking. Human oversight remains essential.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.9 When to Use Spec Agents
Spec Agents are most useful in specific contexts. Understanding when to use them—and when to rely on human expertise—is important.


17.9.1 When to Use Spec Agents
Spec Agents are most effective when:


17.9.1.1 1. Intent is Vague

Early-stage ideas—requirements are not yet clear
Unstructured problems—you don’t know where to start
Exploratory work—figuring out what’s needed




17.9.1.2 2. Users Lack Experience

Beginners—people new to spec creation
Non-technical users—domain experts without technical training
Learning phase—building skills and understanding




17.9.1.3 3. Speed is Important

Rapid prototyping—quick iteration and experimentation
Iterative development—frequent refinement and adjustment
Time-constrained projects—deadlines are tight




17.9.1.4 4. Complexity is Moderate

Enough structure to guide—the problem is not completely undefined
Not too domain-specific—general knowledge applies
Manageable scope—spec can be reasonably comprehensive





17.9.2 When Not to Rely on Them Fully
Spec Agents are less effective when:


17.9.2.1 1. Requirements Are Highly Specific

Domain expertise required—specialized knowledge is essential
Precise constraints needed—generic specs won’t suffice
Industry-specific standards—regulations or best practices apply




17.9.2.2 2. Systems Are Critical

High reliability required—failure has serious consequences
Strict validation needed—spec must be perfect
Safety-critical applications—errors are unacceptable




17.9.2.3 3. Deep Understanding is Needed

Complex interactions—subtle relationships between components
Subtle edge cases—unusual but important scenarios
Deep domain knowledge—expertise beyond general understanding



In these cases, human refinement becomes essential. Agents provide scaffolding, but experts must complete the work.

title: 17  Spec Agent
section: 17.10 When Not to Rely on Them Fully
text:
17.10 When Not to Rely on Them Fully
Spec Agents are less effective when:


17.10.1 1. Requirements Are Highly Specific

Domain expertise required
Precise constraints needed




17.10.2 2. Systems Are Critical

High reliability required
Strict validation needed




17.10.3 3. Deep Understanding is Needed

Complex interactions
Subtle edge cases



In these cases, human refinement becomes essential.

title: 17  Spec Agent
section: 17.10 Spec Agents as a Layer in the System
text:
17.10 Spec Agents as a Layer in the System
Spec Agents are not standalone tools or isolated activities. They are part of a larger pipeline:
Intent → Spec Agent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation

They occupy the transition between:

Human thinking—intent, goals, and aspirations
System definition—structured, executable specifications


As part of this pipeline, Spec Agents enable faster and more reliable movement from ideas to working systems.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.11 Evolution of Spec Agents
As systems improve and we learn more about effective spec creation, Spec Agents may evolve:

Learn from past specs—improve based on successful specifications
Adapt to user preferences—customize approach for different users
Incorporate domain knowledge—specialize for different industries or problems

This leads to:

Personalized specification systems—tailored to individual needs
Adaptive definition workflows—improving over time
More effective human-AI collaboration—better integration of strengths

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.12 Final Insight
Spec Agents represent a shift in how we use AI. They are not just tools for execution or generation. They are tools for definition.


The most powerful use of AI is not to do the work, but to help define the work.

This shift has profound implications for how we approach system development and what it means to be a programmer.

title: 17  Spec Agent
text:
17.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the boundary between human thinking and system definition becomes collaborative.

Spec Agents turn vague intent into structured possibility, but only humans can decide what truly matters.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.1 Specification Is Not Static
A common misconception in software development is that a specification can be written once and remain correct forever. This misconception has led to countless failed projects and wasted effort.
In reality:
Therefore:

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
Initial specs are incomplete—we never know everything upfront
Assumptions are often hidden—we take things for granted without realizing it
Edge cases are not fully known—we can’t anticipate every situation



A spec is not a final artifact. It is a starting point.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.2 The Reality of Imperfect Definitions
Even with careful design, deep domain knowledge, and extensive planning, the first version of a spec will likely have issues:

Miss constraints—important limitations are not specified
Leave ambiguity—terms or concepts are not clearly defined
Fail under certain inputs—edge cases emerge during execution

This is not a failure of the team or the process. It is a natural consequence of:

Complex problems—we cannot fully understand everything at the start
Limited initial understanding—we learn more as we build and test
Evolving requirements—what we need changes as we learn



You do not discover the correct spec before execution. You discover it through execution.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.3 The Refinement Loop
Spec Engineering is inherently iterative. It is not a linear process of “write spec → implement → done.” It is a cyclical process of continuous improvement.
The core loop is:
Spec → Execution → Deviation → Refinement → Spec


18.3.1 Step 1: Spec

Define initial structure—create the first version of the specification
Set inputs, outputs, constraints—establish the basic framework
Make assumptions explicit—state what you’re taking for granted




18.3.2 Step 2: Execution

Run the system—use the spec to guide behavior
Produce outputs—generate results based on the spec
Observe behavior—see what actually happens




18.3.3 Step 3: Deviation

Compare output with expectations—did we get what we wanted?
Identify mismatches—where does behavior differ from intent?
Recognize patterns—are certain types of errors recurring?




18.3.4 Step 4: Refinement

Adjust spec—update the specification based on observations
Add constraints—specify boundaries that were missing
Clarify definitions—make ambiguous terms precise



Each iteration reduces ambiguity and moves the system closer to the desired behavior.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.4 What is Deviation?
Deviation is the gap between what we expect and what we actually observe:
Expected Behavior ≠ Actual Output
Understanding the types of deviation helps diagnose where the spec needs improvement.


18.4.1 Types of Deviation


18.4.1.1 1. Missing Information
The output lacks required content or elements.
Example: - Expected: Summary includes all key points - Actual: Summary misses some important points - Diagnosis: Success criteria not specific enough



18.4.1.2 2. Incorrect Interpretation
The system misunderstands the intent or requirements.
Example: - Expected: Formal tone in responses - Actual: Casual language used - Diagnosis: Constraint not clearly specified



18.4.1.3 3. Overgeneralization
The output is too broad or includes unnecessary information.
Example: - Expected: Specific answer to a question - Actual: Long explanation with tangential information - Diagnosis: Output scope not constrained



18.4.1.4 4. Inconsistency
Different runs produce different outputs for similar inputs.
Example: - Expected: Consistent formatting across all responses - Actual: Format varies between runs - Diagnosis: Output structure not specified precisely


Deviation is not noise. It is a signal—a valuable indicator of where the spec needs improvement.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.5 Where to Apply Fixes
A critical principle for effective refinement:

Fix problems at the highest possible level

When you observe deviation, resist the urge to immediately tweak execution parameters. Instead, look upstream to find the root cause.


18.5.1 Incorrect Approach

Change the model—switch to a different AI system
Add more prompts—inject additional instructions
Adjust parameters—modify temperature, top-k, or other settings

These changes address symptoms, not causes. They may temporarily improve results but don’t solve the underlying problem.



18.5.2 Correct Approach

Improve spec—clarify the definition
Add constraints—specify missing boundaries
Define success criteria—make evaluation more precise

These changes address the root cause. They improve the definition, which leads to better execution.


Most issues originate in definition, not execution.

The vast majority of problems with AI systems are not bugs in the implementation—they are gaps or ambiguities in the specification.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.6 Refinement Strategies
When you identify deviation, use these strategies to refine the spec effectively:


18.6.1 1. Add Constraints
If outputs are too broad or uncontrolled:

Introduce limits—specify maximum length, number of items, etc.
Define boundaries—what is included and what is excluded
Specify format—exact structure of the output




18.6.2 2. Clarify Output Structure
If outputs are inconsistent or unpredictable:

Define format—specify exact structure (JSON, array, etc.)
Specify required elements—what must always be included
Provide examples—show what good output looks like




18.6.3 3. Define Edge Cases
If the system fails under certain inputs:

Add explicit rules—how to handle unusual situations
Extend spec—cover more scenarios
Provide fallback behavior—what to do when standard approach fails




18.6.4 4. Strengthen Success Criteria
If evaluation is unclear or subjective:

Make criteria measurable—use quantitative metrics where possible
Reduce subjectivity—make evaluation more objective
Provide evaluation examples—show what counts as success

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.7 Convergence of the System
With each iteration, the system should move toward convergence—stable, predictable behavior that meets the requirements.


18.7.1 Convergence Condition
A system is considered stable when:

Outputs consistently meet success criteria—95%+ of runs pass evaluation
Variations are within acceptable bounds—minor differences don’t matter
Behavior is predictable—similar inputs produce similar outputs




18.7.2 Signs of Convergence

Deviations become less frequent—errors are rare
Refinements become smaller—adjustments are minor tweaks
Evaluation scores stabilize—quality metrics stop improving significantly



Stability emerges through iteration. It is not achieved instantly but develops over time.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
section: 18.8 From Debugging Code to Debugging Spec
text:
18.8 From Debugging Code to Debugging Spec
The shift to iterative refinement represents a fundamental change in how we think about debugging and improvement.


18.8.1 In Traditional Programming

You debug code—find and fix errors in the implementation
Trace execution paths—follow the code to find where things go wrong
Fix logic errors—correct mistakes in the algorithm or control flow




18.8.2 In the LLM Paradigm

You debug definitions—find and fix gaps in the specification
Clarify intent—make sure the goal is understood correctly
Adjust constraints—specify boundaries more precisely




18.8.3 Old Model

Fix logic errors—correct the implementation
Trace execution paths—find where code goes wrong
Optimize performance—make code run faster




18.8.4 New Model

Fix ambiguity—clarify what is meant
Clarify intent—make sure goals are understood
Adjust constraints—specify boundaries more precisely



The object of debugging has changed from code to definition.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.9 Iteration as Learning
Each refinement cycle improves more than just the spec—it improves understanding:

The spec—becomes clearer and more complete
Your understanding of the problem—deepens with each iteration
The system’s behavior—becomes more predictable and reliable


This creates a virtuous feedback loop:

Better understanding → better spec—as you learn more, you can define better
Better spec → better output—clearer definitions lead to better results
Better output → better understanding—seeing what works teaches you more



Iteration is not repetition. It is progressive clarification and improvement.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.10 Common Mistakes


18.10.1 1. Treating First Output as Final

Accepting early results—assuming the first version is good enough
Skipping refinement—moving on without improving
Settling for “good enough”—not pushing for excellence

Solution: Always assume the first version will need refinement. Plan for iteration from the start.



18.10.2 2. Fixing Symptoms Instead of Causes

Adjusting outputs instead of spec—tweaking results without addressing root cause
Adding patches instead of fixing definitions—applying band-aid solutions
Treating symptoms rather than disease—addressing what you see, not what causes it

Solution: Always look upstream. When you see a problem, ask “what in the spec caused this?”



18.10.3 3. Lack of Evaluation

No clear signal for improvement—can’t tell if changes help
Subjective assessment—relying on gut feeling rather than data
No baseline for comparison—don’t know if you’re getting better

Solution: Define explicit success criteria and measure consistently. Use data to guide refinement.



18.10.4 4. Overreacting to Noise

Changing spec based on single outputs—treating outliers as patterns
Making large changes for small issues—overcorrecting for minor problems
Chasing random variation—trying to fix things that aren’t really problems

Solution: Look for patterns across multiple runs. Don’t change the spec based on single observations.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.11 Refinement as a Core Skill
Iterative refinement is not optional. It is central to Spec Engineering and effective system development.
It requires:

Observation—carefully watching what the system actually does
Diagnosis—understanding why deviations occur
Controlled adjustment—making targeted, thoughtful changes

Developing this skill takes practice, but it is essential for success in the LLM era.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.12 Final Insight
A good spec is not written once. It is discovered through iteration.
The process of refinement is not a sign of failure—it is the path to success. Each iteration brings you closer to a specification that produces reliable, predictable results.


The path to correctness is not direct. It is iterative.

title: 18  Iterative Spec Refinement
text:
18.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, programming is no longer about writing perfect code once. It is about refining imperfect definitions until they become reliable.

You do not get the spec right the first time. You make it right over time.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.1 The Missing Link
By now, we have defined two critical elements of programming in the LLM era:
But systems do not execute specs directly. A spec, no matter how well-written, cannot be fed directly into an LLM or agent system and expected to produce results.
There is a necessary transformation that must occur:
This translation is the bridge between definition and execution—a critical step that enables systems to actually do work.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
Intent defines what we want—the human goal and aspiration
Spec defines what it means—the structured, precise specification of behavior




Spec must be translated into Instruction

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
section: 19.2 Why Spec Cannot Be Executed Directly
text:
19.2 Why Spec Cannot Be Executed Directly
A spec defines what a system should do, but it does not define how to invoke the system or what exact form the execution should take.
A spec defines:

Structure—the organization of the system
Constraints—the boundaries the system must respect
Success conditions—how to measure whether it works

But it does not define:

How to invoke the system—what calls to make, in what order
What exact inputs to pass—the specific format and content of inputs
What output format to enforce at runtime—how to structure the results

Spec is:

Declarative—it describes what should exist

Instruction is:

Operational—it specifies how to make it exist



Spec defines the system. Instruction runs the system.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.3 What Does “Compilation” Mean?
The transformation from spec to instruction can be understood through the concept of compilation.


19.3.1 Analogy
Think of it like traditional programming:

Spec → high-level definition, like source code in Python or Java
Instruction → executable form, like machine code or bytecode




19.3.2 Key Idea
Compilation does not change meaning—it changes form:
Spec (definition) → Instruction (execution protocol)
The transformation preserves the intent and requirements of the spec while making them executable by the system.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.4 The Transformation Process
Converting a spec into an instruction involves several steps, each of which transforms the specification into a more concrete, operational form.


19.4.1 Step 1: Extract Task
From the spec, identify the core action or operation that needs to be performed.
Example: - Spec: “Document summarization system” - Task: “Summarize the input text”



19.4.2 Step 2: Map Input
Define how input will be passed to the system. This includes format, structure, and validation.
Example: - Spec input: “Text document” - Instruction input: {text: string, max_length: 10000}



19.4.3 Step 3: Define Output Structure
Convert output expectations into a structured format that the system must follow.
Example: - Spec output: “Summary and key points” - Instruction output: {summary: string, key_points: Array<string>}



19.4.4 Step 4: Translate Constraints
Turn constraints into enforceable rules that the system must follow during execution.
Example: - Spec constraint: “Under 100 words” - Instruction constraint: "summary_length < 100"



19.4.5 Step 5: Encode Evaluation Signals (Optional)
Add guidance for correctness or hints that help the system produce better results.
Example: - Spec: “Preserve main ideas” - Instruction: “Prioritize information that appears most frequently in the source”

Spec → Task + Input + Output + Constraints → Instruction

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.5 Example Transformation
Let’s walk through a complete example of transforming a spec into an instruction.


19.5.1 Spec (Simplified)

Input: Text document
Output: Summary
Constraints: Max 100 words
Success: Captures key points




19.5.2 Instruction

Task: Summarize the input text
Input: {text: string}
Output: {summary: string}
Constraints:

Length: max 100 words
Content: include key points
Style: concise and clear



The meaning is preserved—the system still needs to summarize text—but the form becomes executable. The instruction provides the specific protocol the system will follow.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.6 What Changes During Transformation
The transformation from spec to instruction involves several important changes:


19.6.1 From Abstract to Concrete
Spec concepts become specific, operational requirements.
Example: - Spec: “Be concise” - Instruction: “Limit output to 100 words”



19.6.2 From Implicit to Explicit
Assumptions and general guidance become specific requirements.
Example: - Spec: “Use relevant information” - Instruction: “Use only the provided input text, do not add external knowledge”



19.6.3 From Flexible to Operational
General guidelines become specific rules for execution.
Example: - Spec: “Capture main ideas” - Instruction: “Extract 3-5 key points, each under 20 words, prioritizing frequently mentioned concepts”


The transformation reduces ambiguity at execution time, making behavior more predictable.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
section: 19.7 The Role of Instruction in the System
text:
19.7 The Role of Instruction in the System
Instruction acts as the interface between definition and execution—the point where abstract concepts become concrete operations.


19.7.1 It ensures:

The system knows what to do—clear task definition
The system knows how to respond—specific input/output formats
The system produces usable outputs—structured results that downstream systems can use


Without instruction:

Spec remains unused—a good idea that never becomes reality
Execution becomes inconsistent—different systems interpret the spec differently
Integration becomes difficult—no standard interface for interaction

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.8 Automation of the Transformation
In advanced systems, the transformation from spec to instruction can be automated, reducing manual effort and ensuring consistency.


19.8.1 Spec → Instruction Agent
An agent that:

Takes spec as input
Generates instruction automatically
Follows defined patterns and best practices




19.8.2 Benefits

Faster system creation—no manual translation required
Consistent instruction format—standardized across systems
Reduced manual effort—less human intervention needed




19.8.3 Risks

Incorrect mapping—automated translation may miss nuances
Loss of nuance—subtle requirements may be oversimplified
Over-generalization—generic instructions may lack specificity



Automation must be paired with validation. Human oversight ensures quality.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.9 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes can undermine the effectiveness of the spec-to-instruction transformation:


19.9.1 1. Mixing Spec and Instruction

Blurring definition and execution—combining both in one layer
Leads to confusion—hard to separate concerns
Makes maintenance difficult—changes affect both definition and execution

Solution: Keep spec and instruction separate. Each has a clear role.



19.9.2 2. Skipping the Transformation

Using raw spec as prompt—directly feeding spec to the system
Results become unstable—system may interpret spec inconsistently
Loses benefits of instruction—no standardized execution protocol

Solution: Always transform spec into instruction before execution.



19.9.3 3. Losing Constraints

Not translating constraints properly—critical requirements are dropped
Leads to unpredictable outputs—system violates important boundaries
Reduces reliability—system doesn’t meet requirements

Solution: Systematically translate each constraint into enforceable rules.



19.9.4 4. Overcomplicating Instructions

Adding unnecessary detail—cluttering the instruction with irrelevant information
Reduces clarity—main intent is obscured

Makes maintenance difficult—hard to understand and modify


Solution: Include only what’s necessary for reliable execution.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.10 Instruction as a Derived Artifact
Instruction should not be the starting point of system development. It should be:

Derived from spec



19.10.1 Incorrect Approach

Start with prompt—begin with natural language requests
Adjust until it works—tweak prompts through trial and error
No underlying spec—no formal definition of requirements

This approach is ad hoc and difficult to scale.



19.10.2 Correct Approach

Define spec first—create a formal specification
Generate instruction from spec—systematic transformation
Refine spec as needed—improve definition, not just prompts

This approach is systematic and scalable.


Stable systems are built from stable definitions. Instructions should always be derived from specs, not created independently.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.11 Toward a Compilation Pipeline
We can now define a complete pipeline that spans from intent to execution:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation

Each stage transforms the problem into a more executable form:

Intent → human goal and aspiration
Spec → structured, precise definition
Instruction → executable protocol
Execution → actual system behavior
Evaluation → assessment of results


This pipeline provides a complete framework for building reliable AI systems.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.12 Final Insight
The transition from spec to instruction is where:

Definition becomes action—abstract concepts become concrete operations
Structure becomes behavior—static definitions produce dynamic results

It is a critical step that enables systems to actually do work rather than just exist as ideas or specifications.


A system is only as good as its ability to execute its specification.

title: 19  From Spec to Instruction
text:
19.13 Closing Statement
Spec defines what should happen. Instruction ensures that it does.

The clearer the transformation, the more reliable the execution.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.1 From Execution to Design
Instruction is not just a simple translation from spec to execution. It is not merely a mechanical process of converting requirements into prompts.
Instruction is a design problem—a creative and analytical challenge that determines how well a system will perform.
A poorly designed instruction leads to:
A well-designed instruction leads to:

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
Inconsistent outputs—results vary unpredictably
Unstable behavior—different runs produce different outcomes
Difficult debugging—problems are hard to diagnose and fix



Predictable results—consistent, reliable outputs
Reusable components—can be applied across different contexts
Scalable systems—can grow and evolve without breaking



Instruction is not something you write once and forget. It is something you design carefully, test systematically, and refine continuously.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.2 What Makes a Good Instruction?
A good instruction is one that enables a system to operate effectively and reliably. It should empower the system to:

Produce correct outputs—results that meet requirements and expectations
Maintain consistency—similar inputs produce similar outputs
Be reusable across contexts—can be applied to different problems or domains



20.2.1 Core Properties
Effective instructions share several fundamental properties:


20.2.1.1 1. Clarity

No ambiguity in task—the purpose is crystal clear
Clear expectations—what should happen is obvious
No room for interpretation—multiple meanings are eliminated




20.2.1.2 2. Structure

Defined input and output—exact formats are specified
Explicit format—structure is predictable and consistent
Organized logically—information flows naturally




20.2.1.3 3. Constraint

Boundaries are enforced—what can and cannot happen is clear
Degrees of freedom are controlled—system operates within defined limits
Rules are explicit—constraints are stated unambiguously




20.2.1.4 4. Executability

The system can act without guessing—no implicit assumptions required
No missing information—everything needed is provided
Ready for automation—can be executed without human intervention



A good instruction removes the need for interpretation. The system knows exactly what to do.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.3 Instruction as a Design Artifact
Instruction should be treated with the same care and attention as other design artifacts in software development:

An API—defining how components interact
A function interface—specifying inputs, outputs, and behavior
A contract—establishing clear expectations and obligations


It defines:

What goes in—the input the system receives
What comes out—the output the system produces
How the system behaves—the transformation it performs



20.3.1 Example Analogy
Think of instruction like a function signature in programming:
f(input) → output
The instruction specifies:

Input schema—what data the function accepts
Output schema—what data the function returns
Constraints—rules the function must follow



Good instructions are predictable like functions. Given the same input, they always produce the same output.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.4 The Importance of Structured Output
One of the most critical aspects of instruction design is output structure. This is often overlooked but has profound implications for system reliability and usability.


20.4.1 Why Structure Matters
Without structured output:

Outputs are inconsistent—different runs produce different formats
Hard to parse—downstream systems cannot reliably process results
Difficult to reuse—results cannot be easily integrated into other systems

With structured output:

Outputs are predictable—format is consistent and reliable
Easy to process—downstream systems can parse results automatically
Compatible with systems—results can be integrated seamlessly

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.5 Types of Output Structure
Output structure exists on a spectrum from completely free-form to highly structured:


20.5.1 1. Free Text (Least Structured)

Flexible—system has maximum freedom
Hard to control—results vary widely
Difficult to parse—format is unpredictable

Example: > “Write a summary of this text.”
The summary could be any length, format, or style.



20.5.2 2. Semi-Structured

Some constraints apply—guidelines exist but not strict rules
More predictable than free text—format is somewhat consistent
Easier to parse—structure is recognizable

Example: > “Write a summary as 3-5 bullet points.”
The format is constrained but bullet points can vary in length and content.



20.5.3 3. Fully Structured

Strict format rules—exact structure is specified
Highly predictable—format is consistent
Easy to parse—structure is machine-readable

Example: > “Output JSON: {summary: string, key_points: Array, word_count: number}”
The format is completely specified and machine-readable.


The more structured the output, the more controllable the system. Structure enables reliability.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.6 Designing Output First
A useful principle for effective instruction design:

Design the output before designing the instruction



20.6.1 Why?

Output defines what success looks like—knowing the end state guides the process
Output determines system usability—downstream systems depend on output format
Output simplifies instruction—knowing the target makes the path clearer




20.6.2 Example
Instead of starting with: > “Summarize this text”
Start with: - Output format: - Summary: 50-100 words - Key points: 3-5 bullet points - Each bullet: under 20 words
Then design the instruction to achieve this output.


Output design drives instruction clarity. Know where you’re going before you start.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.7 Balancing Structure and Flexibility
Effective instruction design requires finding the right balance between structure and flexibility.


20.7.1 Too Little Structure

Outputs vary widely—results are unpredictable
Hard to control—system may produce unexpected results
Difficult to integrate—downstream systems cannot use results




20.7.2 Too Much Structure

Restricts model capabilities—limits what the system can do
Reduces natural generation—results feel artificial or constrained
Creates brittleness—small changes break the system




20.7.3 Good Balance

Structure where necessary—constrain format and key requirements
Flexibility where beneficial—allow freedom in content and style
Focus on what matters—constrain critical aspects, allow freedom elsewhere



Constrain format, not creativity. Structure the container, not the content.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.8 Instruction Granularity
Instructions can operate at different levels of detail and specificity. Choosing the right granularity is a key design decision.


20.8.1 High-Level Instruction

Broad tasks—large, complex objectives
More flexibility—system has freedom in approach
Less control—results may vary

Example: > “Analyze this document and provide insights.”



20.8.2 Low-Level Instruction

Specific tasks—narrow, well-defined objectives
More control—results are predictable
Less adaptable—may not handle variations well

Example: > “Extract all dates from the document and format them as YYYY-MM-DD.”



20.8.3 Trade-off

High-level → flexible but less predictable
Low-level → predictable but less adaptable



Choose granularity based on system needs. Consider how much control you need versus how much flexibility you want.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.9 Reusability of Instructions
Well-designed instructions can be reused across different contexts, amplifying their value and reducing development effort.


20.9.1 Characteristics of Reusable Instructions
Reusable instructions are:

Clear input/output—interfaces are well-defined
Minimal assumptions—don’t depend on specific contexts
Modular design—can be combined with other instructions




20.9.2 Example
A reusable instruction for text classification:

Input: {text: string, categories: Array<string>}
Output: {classification: string, confidence: number}
Task: Classify text into one of the provided categories

This can be reused for any classification task by changing the categories.


Reusability increases leverage. Design instructions that can be applied across multiple problems.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.10 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes can undermine instruction effectiveness:


20.10.1 1. Treating Instructions as Prompts

Informal—lacking structure and precision
Inconsistent—varying between executions
Unreliable—producing unpredictable results

Solution: Design instructions as structured protocols, not conversational prompts.



20.10.2 2. Ignoring Output Design

Leads to unusable results—downstream systems cannot process output
Makes integration difficult—results cannot be reliably used
Reduces system value—good execution produces bad output

Solution: Always design output first. Know exactly what format you need.



20.10.3 3. Missing Constraints

Outputs drift—behavior deviates from requirements over time
Quality degrades—results become less accurate or relevant
System becomes unreliable—predictability decreases

Solution: Explicitly define all constraints. What must the system do? What must it not do?



20.10.4 4. Overloading Instructions

Too many tasks in one step—instruction tries to do too much
Reduces clarity—main purpose becomes obscured
Makes maintenance difficult—hard to understand and modify

Solution: Keep instructions focused. One clear task per instruction. Use composition for complex workflows.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.11 Instruction and System Stability
Stable systems depend on stable instructions. If instructions are:

Clear—unambiguous and precise
Structured—organized and predictable
Consistent—same format every time

Then:

Outputs become predictable—results meet expectations
Behavior stabilizes—system performs reliably
Quality improves—consistency enables optimization



Stability is a property of design, not execution. Good instructions produce stable systems.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.12 Final Insight
Instruction design is the point where:

Specification becomes executable—abstract definitions become concrete operations
Systems become controllable—behavior becomes predictable and reliable

It is a critical design discipline that determines system effectiveness.


The quality of execution depends on the quality of instruction design.

title: 20  Instruction Design
text:
20.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, writing instructions is not about phrasing or crafting clever prompts. It is about structure.

A well-designed instruction does not guide the system. It defines how the system behaves.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.1 From Generation to Action
So far, we have focused on systems that:
These systems are powerful but limited. They operate entirely within the realm of language and knowledge, without the ability to interact with the external world.
However, many real-world tasks require more than generation. They require:
This introduces a new capability:

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
Process inputs—receive information
Generate outputs—produce text, code, or other content




Access to external data—retrieving information from databases, APIs, or the web
Interaction with systems—calling services, executing commands, controlling hardware
Execution of operations—performing calculations, accessing files, sending messages



Tool Use

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.2 What is Tool Use?
Tool use is the ability of an agent to:

Invoke external functions or systems to extend its capabilities

It transforms a system from a language processor into an operational agent that can interact with the world.


21.2.1 Examples of Tools

APIs—weather services, search engines, databases
Calculators—performing mathematical computations
File systems—reading and writing files
External services—sending emails, making payments, controlling devices


Without tools, an agent is limited to:

Its internal knowledge—what it learned during training
Its generative ability—what it can create from that knowledge
Its understanding—how it interprets and processes information

With tools, an agent can:

Retrieve real-time data—access current information not in training data
Perform computations—execute calculations with precision
Interact with the environment—control and modify external systems



Tools extend the system beyond language, enabling action in the real world.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.3 Why Tool Use is Necessary
LLMs alone have inherent limitations that restrict their usefulness for many real-world applications:

No real-time knowledge—training data has a cutoff date
Limited precision in computation—not designed for exact calculations
No direct interaction with external systems—cannot access databases, APIs, or services

Tool use addresses these limitations, enabling systems to overcome their inherent constraints.


21.3.1 Example
Without tools: - “What is the current weather in Tokyo?” - Response may be outdated or incorrect—system relies on training data
With tools: - Agent calls weather API → retrieves current data → provides accurate answer - Response is current and accurate—system accesses real-time information


Tools bridge the gap between language and reality. They enable systems to go beyond knowledge to action.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.4 Agents as Orchestrators
With tool use, the role of the agent changes fundamentally. It transforms from a processor of language into an orchestrator of capabilities.

Instead of:

Directly solving every problem—generating answers from internal knowledge

The agent:

Decides when to use a tool—determining what capabilities are needed
Selects the appropriate tool—choosing the right function for the task
Integrates the result—combining tool output with reasoning to produce final answers



21.4.1 Example Flow
User Query → Agent → Tool Selection → API Call → Result → Final Response
The agent doesn’t know the answer directly, but it knows how to get it.


The agent coordinates capabilities rather than performing everything itself. It becomes a manager of tools and resources.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.5 What is an API?
An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined way for systems to interact. It specifies how different software components can communicate with each other.
An API specifies:

What functions are available—the operations that can be performed
What inputs they require—the parameters and data needed
What outputs they return—the results and format of responses



21.5.1 Example
Weather API: - Input: location (city name or coordinates) - Output: temperature, conditions, humidity, forecast

APIs are:

Structured—follow defined formats and protocols
Predictable—same input produces same output
Machine-readable—designed for automated interaction



APIs are the standard interface for tool use. They provide a structured way for systems to interact.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.6 Function Calling
Function calling is a mechanism that allows an agent to select and invoke a predefined function with structured arguments.


21.6.1 Key Idea
Instead of generating free text as output, the model generates:

A function name—which tool to use
A set of parameters—what arguments to pass to the function




21.6.2 Example
Function:
get_weather(location)
Agent Output:
call get_weather with {location: "Seattle"}
System executes: - Calls the weather API with “Seattle” as parameter - Returns the weather data for Seattle - Agent processes the result and formulates final response


Function calling makes tool use structured and reliable. The agent doesn’t just guess—it follows a defined protocol.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.7 The Tool Use Loop
Tool use introduces a new loop within the execution phase—a cycle of reasoning, action, and observation.
Reason → Select Tool → Call Tool → Observe Result → Continue

This is often referred to as:

ReAct (Reasoning + Acting)—a framework for tool-using agents



21.7.1 Steps


21.7.1.1 1. Reason

Understand the task—analyze what needs to be done
Determine if a tool is needed—assess whether external capabilities are required
Identify the right tool—choose the appropriate function for the situation




21.7.1.2 2. Select Tool

Choose appropriate function—match tool to task
Prepare parameters—format arguments correctly
Validate inputs—ensure parameters are correct




21.7.1.3 3. Call Tool

Provide structured input—pass parameters in correct format
Execute the function—make the API call or run the operation
Handle responses—process the tool’s output




21.7.1.4 4. Observe Result

Receive output—get the result from the tool
Analyze the result—understand what the tool returned
Determine next steps—decide whether to continue or conclude




21.7.1.5 5. Continue

Use result to produce final output—integrate tool results into response
Decide if more tools are needed—assess whether additional capabilities are required
Complete the task—produce the final answer or action



Tool use integrates reasoning with action. The agent doesn’t just think—it acts, observes, and continues.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.8 Designing Tools for Agents
Not all tools are equally usable by AI agents. Designing effective tools requires understanding how agents think and interact.

Good tools for agents have:


21.8.1 1. Clear Interface

Well-defined inputs and outputs—exact specifications of parameters and results
Clear function names—descriptive names that indicate purpose
Consistent behavior—predictable responses




21.8.2 2. Structured Data

Machine-readable formats—JSON, XML, or other structured formats
Explicit schemas—clear definition of data structures
Type information—specification of data types




21.8.3 3. Minimal Ambiguity

No hidden assumptions—all requirements are explicit
Clear documentation—comprehensive descriptions of behavior

Explicit error handling—defined behavior for error conditions





21.8.4 4. Focused Functionality

One clear purpose per tool—single responsibility
Composable design—tools can be combined
Atomic operations—tools do one thing well



Tools should be designed for machines, not humans. They need precision, structure, and clarity.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.9 Integration with Instruction Design
Tool use must be reflected in system instructions. The instruction should specify:

Available tools—what functions the agent can use
When to use them—under what conditions tools should be invoked
How to format calls—the correct structure for tool invocations



21.9.1 Example
Instruction: - “If real-time data is required, call the appropriate API” - “Use get_weather for weather queries” - “Use search_api for information retrieval” - “Format tool calls as JSON: {tool: string, parameters: object}”


Instruction defines the tool usage policy. It tells the agent when and how to use tools.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.10 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes can undermine effective tool use:


21.10.1 1. Overusing Tools

Calling tools unnecessarily—invoking functions when not needed
Reducing efficiency—wasting time and resources
Complicating simple tasks—adding complexity where it’s not needed

Solution: Only use tools when necessary. Assess whether the task requires external capabilities.



21.10.2 2. Underusing Tools

Ignoring available capabilities—not using tools when they would help
Limiting system potential—failing to leverage available resources
Producing inferior results—answers could be better with tools

Solution: Recognize when tools would improve results. Don’t hesitate to use appropriate capabilities.



21.10.3 3. Poor Tool Design

Ambiguous interfaces—unclear inputs or outputs
Hard to integrate—difficult to use with agents
Unreliable behavior—inconsistent or unpredictable responses

Solution: Design tools with agents in mind. Clear interfaces, structured data, and consistent behavior.



21.10.4 4. Lack of Error Handling

Tool failures not handled—system breaks when tools fail
No fallback mechanisms—no alternative when tools are unavailable
Poor error recovery—cannot recover from tool errors

Solution: Implement robust error handling. Define fallback behavior and recovery mechanisms.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.11 Tool Use as Capability Expansion
Tool use fundamentally changes what systems can do. It transforms systems from language processors into operational agents.


21.11.1 Without Tools

Generate text—create written content
Perform reasoning—draw inferences and conclusions
Process information—analyze and synthesize knowledge




21.11.2 With Tools

Interact with real systems—access databases, APIs, and services
Execute actions—perform operations in the external world
Access external knowledge—retrieve current and specific information



Tool use turns language systems into operational systems. It enables action, not just understanding.

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.12 Final Insight
Tool use extends the Minimal Loop, transforming systems from processors to agents:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution (with Tools) → Evaluation

Execution is no longer just generation. It becomes:

Coordination of capabilities—managing multiple tools and resources
Integration of results—combining tool outputs with reasoning
Action in the world—interacting with external systems

title: 21  Tool Use & APIs
text:
21.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the most powerful systems are not those that generate the best text. They are those that can:

Use the right tools—select appropriate capabilities for each task
At the right time—invoke tools when needed
In the right way—format calls correctly and handle responses


Tool use is how systems move from understanding to action.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.1 From Single Agent to Systems of Agents
So far, we have considered systems where:
This architecture works well for:
However, as systems grow in complexity and scope, a single agent becomes insufficient. Tasks become:
A single agent lacks the:
This leads to a new paradigm:

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
A single agent receives input—accepts user queries or tasks
Processes it—applies reasoning and knowledge
Produces output—generates a response or result



Simple tasks—straightforward questions or operations
Moderately complex problems—multi-step reasoning within one agent’s capabilities
Isolated systems—independent tasks that don’t require coordination



Multi-step—requiring many sequential or parallel operations
Knowledge-intensive—requiring expertise across different domains
Decision-heavy—involving complex trade-offs and choices



Specialization—deep expertise in specific areas
Scalability—ability to handle increasing complexity
Coordination—mechanisms for managing multiple tasks simultaneously



Multi-Agent Systems

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.2 What is a Multi-Agent System?
A Multi-Agent System is a system where:

Multiple agents collaborate to achieve a shared goal
