Each agent in the system:

Has a defined role—a specific responsibility or function
Performs specific tasks—operates within its domain of expertise
Interacts with other agents—coordinates and communicates to achieve shared objectives



22.2.1 Key Idea
Instead of one agent doing everything:

Work is distributed across multiple specialized components
Responsibilities are separated according to expertise and function
Collaboration enables emergence—the whole is greater than the sum of parts

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.3 Why Multiple Agents?
There are several compelling reasons to move beyond a single agent architecture:


22.3.1 1. Specialization
Different tasks require different capabilities and expertise:

Retrieval—searching and accessing information
Reasoning—logical inference and analysis
Planning—creating strategies and sequences
Execution—carrying out actions and operations

A single agent cannot be expert in all areas simultaneously.



22.3.2 2. Complexity Management
Breaking a problem into parts reduces cognitive and computational load:

Reduces complexity—each agent handles a smaller, focused problem
Improves clarity—boundaries and responsibilities are clear
Enables focus—each agent specializes in its domain




22.3.3 3. Modularity
Agents can be reused and recombined:

Reused—same agent can serve different systems
Replaced—agents can be upgraded or changed independently
Extended—new capabilities can be added without disrupting existing agents




22.3.4 4. Scalability
Systems can grow by adding more agents:

Adding capabilities—new agents provide new functions
Increasing capacity—multiple agents can work in parallel
Expanding scope—systems can handle more complex problems



Multi-agent systems scale by composition, not by making individual agents more complex.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.4 Roles and Responsibilities
In a multi-agent system, each agent must have:

A clear role—a well-defined function and responsibility
Defined responsibilities—what the agent is accountable for



22.4.1 Example Roles


22.4.1.1 Planner

Breaks down complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
Creates execution strategies and sequences
Anticipates dependencies and ordering requirements




22.4.1.2 Executor

Performs actions and operations
Executes tasks according to plans
Handles implementation details




22.4.1.3 Evaluator

Checks results and outcomes
Provides feedback on quality
Identifies errors or issues




22.4.1.4 Retriever

Fetches external information
Searches databases and knowledge bases
Accesses real-time data



Each agent should do one thing well. Specialization enables excellence.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.5 Division of Labor
Division of labor is central to effective multi-agent systems. It ensures that:

Work is distributed efficiently
Responsibilities are clear
Collaboration is coordinated



22.5.1 Principles



22.5.2 1. Separation of Concerns

Each agent handles a distinct function
No overlap in responsibilities
Clear boundaries between agents




22.5.3 2. Clear Boundaries

Inputs and outputs are well-defined
Interfaces are explicit and structured
Communication protocols are established




22.5.4 3. Minimal Overlap

Avoid duplication of responsibilities
Each agent has unique capabilities
No redundancy in function




22.5.5 Example Flow
User Query
→ Planner Agent (breaks down task)
→ Executor Agents (perform steps)
→ Evaluator Agent (checks results)
→ Final Output
Each agent contributes its specialized capability to achieve the shared goal.


Clear division improves reliability. When responsibilities are separated, errors are easier to identify and fix.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.6 Communication Between Agents
Agents must communicate to coordinate actions and share information. This requires:

Defined communication protocols



22.6.1 What is Communicated?

Tasks—what needs to be done
Intermediate results—outputs from one agent to another
Feedback—evaluations and corrections




22.6.2 Forms of Communication


22.6.2.1 Structured Messages

JSON—standardized format for data exchange
Key-value pairs—clear mapping of information
Type safety—defined data structures

Advantages: - Clear and explicit - Machine-readable - Easy to validate



22.6.2.2 Natural Language

Flexible—can express complex ideas
Natural—easier for humans to understand
Context-rich—can convey nuance

Disadvantages: - Ambiguous—multiple interpretations possible - Less reliable—parsing is difficult - Verbose—can be inefficient



22.6.2.3 Hybrid

Structured + descriptive—combines structure with flexibility
Type-safe with context—data format plus explanatory text
Best of both worlds—clarity and expressiveness



Structured communication improves consistency and reliability. It reduces ambiguity and makes integration easier.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.7 Communication Patterns
Different patterns of communication suit different system architectures and requirements:


22.7.1 1. Sequential Communication
Agents pass results in order, one after another.
Agent A → Agent B → Agent C
When to use: - Tasks have clear dependencies - Each step depends on previous output - Sequential processing is natural



22.7.2 2. Central Coordinator
One agent manages and coordinates all others.
Coordinator → Agents → Coordinator
When to use: - Centralized control is preferred - Coordination logic is complex - Single point of direction is beneficial



22.7.3 3. Peer-to-Peer
Agents interact directly with each other.
Agent A ↔ Agent B ↔ Agent C
When to use: - Distributed decision-making is needed - Agents have equal status - - Local interactions are sufficient


The pattern depends on system complexity and requirements. Choose based on coordination needs and task structure.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.8 Coordination Strategies
How agents are coordinated affects system behavior, reliability, and efficiency:


22.8.1 1. Centralized Control

One agent orchestrates all activities
Clear hierarchy and authority
Easier to manage and debug

Advantages: - Simpler coordination logic - Clear responsibility - Easier to understand
Disadvantages: - Single point of failure - - Bottleneck in decision-making - Less flexible



22.8.2 2. Decentralized Control

Agents decide independently
No central authority
Distributed decision-making

Advantages: - More flexible and adaptable - - No single point of failure - - Scalable to many agents
Disadvantages: - Harder to coordinate - - More complex to debug - - Potential for conflicts



22.8.3 Trade-off

Centralized → control, simplicity, but less flexibility
Decentralized → adaptability, resilience, but more complexity

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.9 Challenges in Multi-Agent Systems
Multi-agent systems introduce new challenges that must be addressed:


22.9.1 1. Coordination Complexity

Managing interactions—ensuring agents work together effectively
Avoiding conflicts—preventing agents from working at cross-purposes
Synchronization—timing and sequencing of agent activities




22.9.2 2. Communication Overhead

More messages—inter-agent communication increases
Increased latency—time spent coordinating
Bandwidth usage—more data transferred between agents




22.9.3 3. Error Propagation

One agent’s mistake affects others—errors cascade through the system
Harder to isolate problems—difficult to identify which agent failed
System-wide impact—local failures can have global consequences




22.9.4 4. Debugging Difficulty

Harder to trace issues—multiple agents, multiple paths
Complex interactions—non-linear dependencies
Emergent behavior—system behavior arises from agent interactions



More agents increase power, but also complexity. The benefits must outweigh the costs.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.10 Designing Multi-Agent Systems
To design effective multi-agent systems:


22.10.1 1. Define Roles Clearly

Avoid ambiguity—each agent’s purpose should be crystal clear
Specify responsibilities—what each agent does and doesn’t do
Establish boundaries—clear limits on agent scope




22.10.2 2. Design Interfaces

Standardize communication—define protocols for agent interaction
Specify input/output formats—clear contracts between agents
Document behavior—comprehensive descriptions of agent capabilities




22.10.3 3. Control Interaction

Limit unnecessary communication—only share what’s needed
Manage message flow—avoid information overload
Optimize coordination—minimize overhead while ensuring effectiveness




22.10.4 4. Add Evaluation Layers

Validate intermediate results—check agent outputs at each step
Provide feedback—enable agents to improve based on results
Ensure quality—maintain standards throughout the system



Structure is critical for coordination. Well-designed systems have clear roles, defined interfaces, and controlled interactions.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.11 When to Use Multi-Agent Systems
Multi-agent systems are not always the right solution. Use them when:


22.11.1 Suitable Cases

Complex workflows—tasks with many interdependent steps
Multi-step reasoning—problems requiring sequential or parallel processing
Systems requiring specialization—tasks needing expertise in multiple domains
Scalable solutions—systems that need to grow in capability




22.11.2 Not Suitable

Simple tasks—straightforward problems that one agent can handle
Low complexity—tasks with few steps and minimal dependencies
Single-step problems—direct input-output transformations
Tight latency requirements—when coordination overhead is unacceptable



Do not use multiple agents unless the complexity and specialization requirements justify the overhead.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
section: 22.12 Multi-Agent Systems in the Minimal Loop
text:
22.12 Multi-Agent Systems in the Minimal Loop
Multi-agent systems extend the execution phase of the Minimal Loop:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution (Multi-Agent) → Evaluation

Execution becomes:

A coordinated process—multiple agents working together
A network of interactions—communication and collaboration between agents
An emergent behavior—system-level properties arising from agent interactions

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.13 Final Insight
Multi-agent systems represent a shift from:

Single-process execution—one agent doing everything To:
Distributed intelligence—multiple agents collaborating



Systems are no longer single actors. They are coordinated collectives, working together to achieve shared goals.

title: 22  Multi-Agent Systems
text:
22.14 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, complex systems are built not by increasing the power of one agent, but by organizing many.

The strength of a system lies in how well its agents collaborate.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.1 Why Memory Matters
So far, we have described systems that:
These systems operate as if each interaction is isolated—each input is processed independently, with no memory of previous interactions.
However, many real-world systems require continuity:
Without memory:

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
Receive input—accept user queries or data
Produce output—generate responses or results




Conversations span multiple turns—users expect the system to remember what was said
Decisions depend on past interactions—context from previous turns informs current decisions
Systems must adapt over time—learning from experience and evolving behavior



Each interaction is isolated—no connection between turns
Context is lost—previous information is unavailable
Behavior becomes shallow—system cannot maintain coherent conversation



Memory is what allows systems to persist beyond a single interaction, enabling continuity and depth.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.2 What is Context?
Context is the information available to the system at the moment of execution. It represents what the system “knows” right now.
It includes:

Current input—the immediate query or task
Relevant past interactions—history that informs current processing
External knowledge—information retrieved from databases or other sources
System state—current status, configuration, or mode



23.2.1 Example
In a conversation:

Current message: “What was that about again?”
Previous messages: “I’m discussing AI memory systems.”
User preferences: “Prefers concise explanations”
Session data: “5 messages exchanged in this session”

All of these form the context for understanding and responding to the current message.


Context defines what the system “knows right now.” It determines how the system interprets and responds to input.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.3 Context as a Working Set
Context can be understood as a working set of information—the subset of all available information that is active and relevant to the current task.
It is:

Limited in size—only so much information can be considered at once
Relevant to the current task—focuses on what’s needed right now
Dynamically constructed—changes based on task requirements



23.3.1 Characteristics

Temporary—exists only during execution
Task-specific—tailored to current needs
Continuously updated—changes as processing progresses



Context is not everything the system knows. It is what the system is using right now to perform the current task.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.4 What is Memory?
Memory is the information stored beyond the current interaction. It represents:

What the system retains over time

Memory enables continuity—allowing the system to remember past interactions, learn from experience, and maintain coherence across sessions.


23.4.1 Types of Memory


23.4.1.1 1. Short-Term Memory

Recent interactions—last few turns of conversation
Session-based—persists only during current session
Volatile—lost when session ends

Uses: - Maintaining conversation flow - Tracking immediate context - Handling multi-turn dialogues



23.4.1.2 2. Long-Term Memory

Persistent storage—information kept across sessions
User data—preferences, history, patterns
Knowledge base—facts, relationships, and information

Uses: - Personalization—remembering user preferences - Learning—improving over time - Context retention—carrying information across sessions


Memory extends the system across time. Without memory, each interaction starts fresh, with no connection to the past.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.5 Context vs Memory
It is important to distinguish between these two concepts, as they play different roles in system behavior:


23.5.1 Context

Active—currently being used for processing
Limited—bounded by capacity constraints
Immediate—directly available for execution

Purpose: Drive current decision-making and response generation



23.5.2 Memory

Stored—persisted for future use
Potentially large—can contain vast amounts of information
Persistent—survives across sessions and time

Purpose: Provide history, enable learning, support continuity



23.5.3 Relationship
Memory → Retrieval → Context → Execution
Memory feeds context through retrieval. Context drives execution. Together, they enable systems to operate with awareness of both the immediate situation and accumulated history.


Memory feeds context. Context drives execution. The relationship connects the past to the present.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.6 Context Management
Context is limited by computational constraints—there’s only so much information that can be processed at once. Therefore, context must be managed carefully.


23.6.1 Challenges

Context window limits—models can only process a fixed amount of information
Irrelevant information—not all past interactions are useful now
Noise accumulation—too much information degrades performance




23.6.2 Strategies


23.6.2.1 1. Selection

Include only relevant information—filter what’s important
Prioritize recent interactions—newer information is often more relevant
Focus on task needs—select information that supports current objectives




23.6.2.2 2. Compression

Summarize past interactions—reduce long conversations to key points
Extract essential information—preserve meaning while reducing size
Create summaries—compress multiple turns into condensed representations




23.6.2.3 3. Prioritization

Keep important data—prioritize information that’s critical
Remove low-value data—discard information that’s not useful
Maintain balance—mix of recent and historically important information



Good context management improves system performance. It ensures the system focuses on what matters.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.7 Long-Term Memory Systems
Long-term memory requires infrastructure and mechanisms to:

Store—persist information for future use
Retrieve—access stored information when needed
Update—modify existing information as needed



23.7.1 Storage
Long-term memory can be stored in various forms:

Databases—structured storage for organized data
Vector stores—semantic storage for similarity-based retrieval
Files—unstructured storage for documents and media
Graph databases—relationship-focused storage for connected information

Choice depends on: - Type of information - Access patterns - Query requirements



23.7.2 Retrieval
Retrieving information from long-term memory requires mechanisms to:

Search—find relevant information based on queries
Match—identify information that satisfies requirements
Rank—order results by relevance

Approaches: - Keyword search—match exact terms - Semantic search—match meaning and concepts - Hybrid—combine keyword and semantic approaches



23.7.3 Update
Memory systems must support:

Add new information—incorporate new data
Modify existing data—update or correct information
Remove outdated data—delete information that’s no longer relevant



Memory is only useful if it can be retrieved effectively. Storage without retrieval is useless.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.8 Retrieval as a Core Mechanism
To use memory effectively, systems must retrieve relevant information at the right time. Retrieval is the bridge between memory and context.


23.8.1 Retrieval Flow
Query → Retrieve → Context → Generate

Query—identify what information is needed
Retrieve—search memory and fetch relevant information
Context—incorporate retrieved information into current context
Generate—use enriched context to produce response




23.8.2 Key Properties

Relevance—retrieved information must be related to current task
Efficiency—retrieval must be fast enough for real-time use
Accuracy—retrieved information must be correct and reliable



Retrieval connects memory to execution. It’s the mechanism that brings the past into the present.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.9 Memory in Multi-Agent Systems
In multi-agent systems, memory can be organized in different ways:


23.9.1 Shared Memory

All agents access the same data
Centralized storage
Consistent across agents

Advantages: - Consistency—all agents have same information - Simplicity—single source of truth - - Coordination—easier to synchronize
Disadvantages: - Bottleneck—single point of access - - Scalability—may limit performance - - Complexity—harder to manage concurrent access



23.9.2 Distributed Memory

Each agent maintains its own memory
Decentralized storage
Independent management

Advantages: - Flexibility—agents can specialize - Scalability—no single bottleneck - - Performance—parallel access
Disadvantages: - Inconsistency—agents may have different information - - Complexity—harder to coordinate - - Redundancy—potential for duplication



23.9.3 Trade-offs

Shared → consistency, but limits flexibility
Distributed → flexibility, but harder to coordinate

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.10 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes undermine effective memory systems:


23.10.1 1. Overloading Context

Too much information—exceeding capacity limits
Reduced performance—slower processing and degraded quality
Increased cost—more computation required

Solution: Select and compress. Include only what’s relevant.



23.10.2 2. Ignoring Memory

Systems forget important data—lose valuable information
No continuity—cannot maintain conversation
No learning—cannot improve over time

Solution: Design memory systems from the start. Make memory a first-class component.



23.10.3 3. Poor Retrieval

Irrelevant information—fetching data that’s not useful
Missed context—failing to retrieve important information
Slow performance—retrieval takes too long

Solution: Invest in retrieval mechanisms. Use semantic search, ranking, and relevance scoring.



23.10.4 4. Mixing Context and Memory

No clear separation—blurring the distinction
Hard to manage—difficult to optimize each component
Poor performance—both suffer from lack of focus

Solution: Maintain clear separation. Context is immediate working memory. Memory is persistent storage.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.11 Designing Memory Systems
Effective memory systems require careful design:


23.11.1 Key Questions

What should be stored?—identify valuable information
When should it be retrieved?—determine retrieval triggers
How should it be updated?—define update mechanisms




23.11.2 Design Principles

Clear separation—distinguish context from memory
Efficient retrieval—optimize for speed and relevance
Controlled updates—manage how information changes over time

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.12 Memory in the Minimal Loop
Memory influences the execution phase of the Minimal Loop:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution (with Context) → Evaluation

Context is constructed using memory before execution:

Retrieve—fetch relevant information from memory
Construct—build context from retrieved information and current input
Execute—use enriched context to generate response

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.13 Final Insight
Memory and context determine how much of the past influences the present.
Without them:

Systems are reactive—responding only to immediate input
Behavior is shallow—no depth or continuity

Intelligence is limited—cannot build on experience


With them:

Systems become adaptive—learning and evolving
Behavior becomes coherent—maintaining consistency over time

Intelligence emerges—accumulating knowledge and wisdom




Intelligence emerges from the ability to use the past in the present. Memory and context enable this capability.

title: 23  Memory & Context
text:
23.14 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, systems are no longer stateless processors. They are evolving entities that:

Remember—preserving information over time
Retrieve—accessing relevant information when needed
Adapt—learning and improving based on experience


Memory gives systems continuity. Context gives them focus.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.1 From Task to System
Up to this point, we have focused on:
This approach is useful for:
But real-world applications are not single tasks. They are:
This requires a fundamental shift:

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
Single tasks—individual operations or queries
Individual instructions—specific protocols for execution
Isolated executions—one-off actions without persistence



Understanding components—learning how each part works
Testing capabilities—verifying that functions work correctly
Simple applications—handling straightforward use cases



Multi-step—requiring sequences of operations
Stateful—maintaining information across interactions
Interactive—responding to user input and feedback



From solving one task → to building a system that continuously solves tasks

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.2 What is an Agent System?
An Agent System is a system that:

Continuously receives tasks, processes them through defined structures, and produces reliable outcomes

It is not:

A single prompt—a one-time request to a model
A one-time execution—a single operation that completes and ends
An isolated process—a standalone function with no connection to others

It is:

A structured pipeline—a sequence of processing stages
With memory—preserving information across time
With tools—accessing external capabilities
With coordination—managing multiple components and flows

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.3 The Evolution Path
Building an agent system follows a natural progression, from simple to complex. Understanding this path helps design systems incrementally, adding capabilities as needed.


24.3.1 Stage 1 — Single Task

One input—a single query or request
One output—a single response or result
No memory—each execution is independent

Example: - Summarize a document - Answer a question - Classify text
Use case: - Simple, one-off operations - Testing capabilities - Prototyping functionality



24.3.2 Stage 2 — Structured Task

Defined spec—clear specification of requirements
Structured instruction—explicit protocol for execution
Controlled output—predictable, consistent results

Example: - Summarize with specific constraints (length, format, content) - Answer with reference to sources - Classify with confidence scores
Use case: - More reliable single tasks - Production-ready individual operations - - Components of larger systems



24.3.3 Stage 3 — Multi-Step Task

Multiple instructions—sequence of operations
Sequential flow—each step builds on previous
Intermediate outputs—results passed between steps

Example: - Retrieve documents → Summarize → Format → Output - Analyze → Classify → Report → Store - Extract → Transform → Load (ETL)
Use case: - Complex workflows - Data processing pipelines - - Multi-stage reasoning



24.3.4 Stage 4 — Stateful System

Maintains context—preserves information across executions
Uses memory—stores and retrieves data over time
Adapts behavior—changes based on history

Example: - Conversation system that remembers previous turns - Personalized assistant that learns preferences - - Adaptive system that improves with use
Use case: - Interactive applications - Long-running processes - - Personalized experiences



24.3.5 Stage 5 — Full Agent System

Uses tools—accesses external APIs and services
Handles multiple tasks—supports various operations
Iterates and adapts—improves through feedback

Example: - Virtual assistant with search, calculation, and reasoning capabilities - - Research agent that can query databases, analyze data, and generate reports - - Autonomous agent that can plan, execute, and evaluate tasks
Use case: - Production systems - - Complex applications - - Autonomous operation

Task → Structured Task → Multi-Step → Stateful → Agent System
Each stage adds capabilities, transforming from a simple function to a sophisticated system.


Systems evolve through layering capabilities. Start simple, add complexity as needed.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
section: 24.4 Core Components of an Agent System
text:
24.4 Core Components of an Agent System
A complete agent system typically includes several layers, each with specific responsibilities:


24.4.1 1. Input Interface

Receives user input—accepts queries, commands, or data
Validates format—ensures input is correct and usable
Routes to appropriate handler—directs input to right component

Purpose: Ensure clean, valid input enters the system



24.4.2 2. Spec Layer

Defines behavior—specifies what the system should do
Sets constraints—establishes boundaries and limits
Determines success criteria—defines how to evaluate results

Purpose: Provide clear definition and guidance for execution



24.4.3 3. Instruction Layer

Converts spec into executable form—translates definition into protocol
Manages instruction library—stores and retrieves instructions
Handles instruction composition—combines multiple instructions

Purpose: Transform definitions into executable operations



24.4.4 4. Execution Engine

Runs instructions—executes the defined operations
Coordinates components—manages interaction between system parts
Handles tools—invokes external functions and services

Purpose: Actually perform the work defined in instructions



24.4.5 5. Tool Layer

External APIs—interfaces to external services
Functional capabilities—computational and operational functions
Resource access—connections to databases, files, and systems

Purpose: Extend system capabilities beyond internal processing



24.4.6 6. Memory System

Stores information—persists data across executions
Retrieves context—fetches relevant information for current task
Manages state—tracks system status and history

Purpose: Enable continuity and learning across time



24.4.7 7. Evaluation Layer

Checks output quality—validates results against requirements
Provides feedback—enables system improvement
Triggers corrections—handles failures and errors

Purpose: Ensure quality and enable continuous improvement


An agent system is a composition of layers. Each layer has a specific responsibility, working together to produce reliable outcomes.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
section: 24.5 The Main Flow of an Agent System
text:
24.5 The Main Flow of an Agent System
A typical agent system follows a structured flow from input to output:
Input
→ Context Construction
→ Spec / Instruction Selection
→ Execution (with Tools)
→ Output
→ Evaluation
→ Memory Update

Each step is:

Defined—has clear purpose and function
Structured—follows established protocols
Interconnected—relies on and feeds into other steps



24.5.1 Step-by-Step Breakdown


24.5.1.1 1. Input

Receive user query or task
Validate format and content
Route to appropriate handler




24.5.1.2 2. Context Construction

Retrieve relevant information from memory
Construct context for current task
Prepare environment for execution




24.5.1.3 3. Spec/Instruction Selection

Identify appropriate spec for task
Select or generate corresponding instruction
Prepare execution protocol




24.5.1.4 4. Execution

Run instruction
Use tools as needed

Handle intermediate results





24.5.1.5 5. Output

Generate response or result
Format for user or downstream system

Present to user or pass to next stage





24.5.1.6 6. Evaluation

Validate output against requirements
Check quality and correctness

Determine if output is acceptable





24.5.1.7 7. Memory Update

Store interaction for future reference

Update system state


Learn from results

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.6 Designing the System
Building an agent system requires structural thinking and careful design. The process involves:


24.6.1 Step 1: Define the Goal

What problem does the system solve?
What value does it provide?
Who will use it?

Purpose: Establish clear objectives and constraints



24.6.2 Step 2: Define Capabilities

What can the system do?
What tools does it need?
What information must it access?

Purpose: Identify required functionality and resources



24.6.3 Step 3: Define Modules

What components are needed?
How should they be organized?
What are their responsibilities?

Purpose: Design system architecture and component structure



24.6.4 Step 4: Define Flow

How do components interact?
What is the sequence of operations?
How does data flow through the system?

Purpose: Establish clear processes and communication protocols



24.6.5 Step 5: Define Interfaces

How do modules communicate?
What formats do they use?
How are errors handled?

Purpose: Ensure reliable integration between components


Design precedes implementation. A well-designed system is easier to build, test, and maintain.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.7 From Instructions to Pipelines
A system is not a single instruction. It is a pipeline of instructions—multiple operations working together to achieve complex goals.


24.7.1 Example
User Query
→ Retrieval Instruction (fetch relevant information)
→ Processing Instruction (analyze and extract)
→ Generation Instruction (create response)
→ Formatting Instruction (structure and style output)

Each instruction:

Has a clear role—performs a specific function
Produces structured output—results in defined format
Feeds into next step—output becomes input for subsequent instruction



Pipelines turn instructions into systems. They enable complex, multi-stage processing.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.8 Adding Memory and Feedback
To make the system adaptive and intelligent, it must incorporate memory and feedback mechanisms:


24.8.1 Memory

Store past interactions—preserve conversation history and results
Retrieve relevant data—access information that informs current processing
Maintain state—track system status and configuration

Purpose: Enable continuity and learning



24.8.2 Feedback

Evaluate outputs—assess quality and correctness
Identify errors—detect problems and failures
Refine behavior—improve instructions and processes

Purpose: Enable continuous improvement

This creates:

Continuous improvement—system gets better over time
Context-aware behavior—responses are informed by history
Adaptive operation—system adjusts based on experience

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.9 Scaling the System
As systems grow in complexity and capability, they must scale effectively:


24.9.1 Add Capabilities

More tools—access additional external services
More functions—perform a wider range of operations
More knowledge—incorporate additional information




24.9.2 Add Agents

Specialized roles—agents focused on specific tasks
Distributed processing—parallel execution across agents
Collaborative operation—agents working together




24.9.3 Improve Spec

Better definitions—more precise and complete specifications
More constraints—clearer boundaries and requirements
Better evaluation—more accurate assessment of results



Scaling is achieved by adding structure, not complexity. Well-architected systems scale gracefully.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.10 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes undermine effective agent system design:


24.10.1 1. Starting Too Complex

Over-engineering early—building more than needed
Unnecessary complexity—making systems harder to understand and maintain
Wasted effort—building capabilities that aren’t used

Solution: Start simple. Add complexity as needed. Follow the evolution path from single task to full system.



24.10.2 2. Skipping Spec

Undefined behavior—system actions are unclear
Unstable systems—inconsistent, unpredictable results
Difficult debugging—hard to identify and fix problems

Solution: Always define spec first. Clear specification leads to reliable systems.



24.10.3 3. Poor Interfaces

Data mismatch—incompatible formats between components
Integration failures—components can’t communicate effectively
System brittleness—changes break existing functionality

Solution: Design clear interfaces. Specify formats, protocols, and error handling.



24.10.4 4. Lack of Evaluation

No feedback loop—system can’t learn or improve
Unknown quality—don’t know if results are correct
Silent failures—errors go undetected

Solution: Implement evaluation at every stage. Check quality, measure performance, and track errors.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.11 System Thinking vs Task Thinking
The shift from task to system represents a fundamental change in mindset:


24.11.1 Task Thinking

Solve one problem—focus on individual operations
One-time execution—complete and forget
Isolated processes—no connection between operations

Limitations: - Can’t handle complexity - No continuity or learning - - Difficult to scale



24.11.2 System Thinking

Design repeatable processes—create sustainable workflows
Handle multiple inputs over time—maintain continuity
Build for scale—architect for growth and evolution

Benefits: - Handles complex problems - Learns and improves - - Scales effectively


Systems produce value repeatedly. Tasks produce value once.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.12 Final Insight
An agent system is not built in one step. It is constructed by:

Layering capabilities—adding functionality incrementally
Connecting components—integrating parts into a coherent whole
Refining definitions—improving specs and instructions over time



You do not build systems all at once. You evolve them from simple tasks, adding capabilities as needed, refining structure as you learn.

title: 24  Building an Agent System
text:
24.13 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, the goal is not to solve one problem. It is to build systems that solve classes of problems.

From single tasks to systems, structure is what enables scale.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.1 From Capability to Reliability
A system that works once is not enough. A prototype that produces a correct output in controlled conditions is not a production system.
Real systems must be:
This introduces a new requirement:

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
Consistent—producing similar results for similar inputs
Predictable—behaving as expected across many executions
Measurable—performance can be quantified and tracked



Reliability — the ability to produce correct results consistently

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.2 What is Reliability?
Reliability is the property that:

Given similar inputs, the system produces outputs that consistently meet defined success criteria

Reliability is not about being perfect or never making mistakes. It is about:

Consistency—similar inputs produce similar outputs
Correctness—outputs meet requirements and expectations
Stability—behavior does not degrade over time



25.2.1 Key Dimensions

Consistency—similar inputs → similar outputs
Correctness—outputs meet requirements
Stability—behavior does not degrade over time



Reliability is not about perfection. It is about consistency within defined bounds.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
section: 25.3 Why Reliability is Hard in LLM Systems
text:
25.3 Why Reliability is Hard in LLM Systems
LLM-based systems face unique reliability challenges that make achieving consistency difficult:
LLMs are inherently:

Probabilistic—outputs are sampled from a distribution, not deterministic
Context-dependent—results vary based on input context and history
Sensitive to input variation—small changes can produce large differences

This leads to:

Output variability—same prompt can produce different answers
Edge case failures—unusual inputs break the system
Hidden instability—problems appear only under specific conditions



Reliability must be engineered. It does not emerge automatically in probabilistic systems.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.4 Sources of Instability
Understanding why systems become unreliable helps design better solutions:


25.4.1 1. Weak Spec

Ambiguous definitions—requirements are unclear or incomplete
Missing constraints—no boundaries on acceptable behavior
Weak success criteria—no clear measure of correctness

Result: System interprets requirements inconsistently



25.4.2 2. Poor Instruction Design

Inconsistent formats—output structure varies
Unclear task definition—system doesn’t understand what to do
Missing structure—no framework for execution

Result: Behavior varies between executions



25.4.3 3. Context Variability

Different context → different results—context affects interpretation
Context window limits—can’t include all relevant information
Context contamination—irrelevant information included

Result: Inconsistent behavior across sessions



25.4.4 4. Tool Errors

External API failures—services unavailable or malfunctioning
Incorrect data—tools return wrong information
Tool misuse—wrong tool used or used incorrectly

Result: Unreliable external operations



25.4.5 5. Lack of Evaluation

No feedback loop—can’t detect or correct errors
Unknown quality—don’t know if results are correct
Uncaught failures—errors propagate silently

Result: Problems accumulate and compound


Most instability originates upstream—in definition and design, not execution.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.5 Principles for Stability
Achieving reliability requires applying specific principles throughout system design:


25.5.1 1. Strengthen Spec

Define clear constraints—explicit boundaries on behavior
Reduce ambiguity—eliminate multiple interpretations
Specify edge cases—handle unusual inputs explicitly

Result: System has clear, unambiguous requirements



25.5.2 2. Enforce Structure

Use structured outputs—exact format requirements
Standardize formats—consistent data structures
Validate structure—check format at every stage

Result: Predictable, parseable outputs



25.5.3 3. Control Context

Manage inputs carefully—validate and clean input data
Reduce noise—remove irrelevant information
Standardize context—consistent context construction

Result: Consistent processing environment



25.5.4 4. Add Redundancy

Multiple checks—verify results at multiple stages
Fallback mechanisms—alternative approaches when primary fails
Redundant processing—compare multiple outputs

Result: Errors caught before they affect users



25.5.5 5. Introduce Evaluation

Continuous validation—check every output
Feedback loops—use results to improve system
Quality metrics—track performance over time

Result: System learns and improves


Stability is achieved through layered control. Multiple mechanisms work together to ensure reliability.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
section: 25.6 What is Evaluation in This Context?
text:
25.6 What is Evaluation in This Context?
Evaluation is the process of:

Measuring whether system outputs meet defined success criteria


It provides:

A signal of correctness—knowing whether results are acceptable
A basis for iteration—information for improving the system
A mechanism for control—triggering corrections or retries



25.6.1 Why Evaluation Matters
Without evaluation:

No notion of correctness—can’t distinguish good from bad results
No direction for improvement—don’t know what to fix
No system stability—behavior can drift without detection

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.7 Automatic Evaluation
Manual evaluation does not scale for production systems. Therefore, we introduce:

Automatic Evaluation



25.7.1 Definition
Automatic evaluation is:

The use of rules, models, or metrics to assess outputs without human intervention

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.8 Types of Automatic Evaluation
Different approaches suit different evaluation needs:


25.8.1 1. Rule-Based Evaluation

Deterministic checks—yes/no questions about output
Examples:

JSON validity—output is valid JSON
Length constraints—output under specified length
Required fields—all mandatory fields present

Advantages:

Fast and precise
No false positives
Easy to implement

Disadvantages:

Limited scope
Can’t assess quality
Rigid





25.8.2 2. Model-Based Evaluation

Use another model to evaluate—judge output quality
Examples:

Relevance scoring—how relevant is output to input?
Hallucination detection—is output grounded in facts?
Sentiment analysis—is tone appropriate?

Advantages:

Can assess complex criteria
Flexible and adaptable
Can evaluate nuance

Disadvantages:

Can be expensive
May have biases
Slower than rules





25.8.3 3. Metric-Based Evaluation

Quantitative measures—numerical scores for quality
Examples:

Accuracy—percentage of correct outputs
Precision/recall—performance on classification tasks
Latency—response time

Advantages:

Objective and comparable
Trackable over time
Industry standards exist

Disadvantages:

May not capture all aspects of quality
Can be gamed
Need ground truth





25.8.4 4. Hybrid Evaluation

Combine multiple methods—use rules, models, and metrics together
Example:

Rule: Output must be JSON
Model: JSON must contain relevant information
Metric: Response time under 2 seconds

Advantages:

Comprehensive
Leverages strengths of each approach
More robust

Disadvantages:

More complex to implement
Higher cost
Requires coordination




Strong systems use layered evaluation—multiple methods working together to ensure quality.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.9 Evaluation Pipeline
A robust system includes an evaluation pipeline that checks quality at every stage:
Output → Validation → Scoring → Decision


25.9.1 Steps


25.9.1.1 1. Validation

Check format—ensure output meets structural requirements
Ensure constraints—verify output respects defined boundaries
Validate completeness—check that all required elements are present




25.9.1.2 2. Scoring

Assess quality—assign score based on evaluation criteria
Measure performance—calculate metrics like accuracy or relevance

Rate results—determine how well output meets requirements





25.9.1.3 3. Decision

Accept—output meets requirements, proceed to user
Reject—output fails validation, trigger retry or correction
Flag—output is marginal, requires human review



Evaluation is not passive. It drives system behavior and ensures quality.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.10 Feedback Loops
Evaluation enables feedback—using results to improve the system:


25.10.1 Immediate Feedback

Reject invalid outputs—don’t present to user
Trigger retries—attempt to produce better result
Apply corrections—automatically fix simple errors

Purpose: Ensure user receives only high-quality results



25.10.2 Long-Term Feedback

Improve spec—refine definitions based on evaluation results
Refine instructions—adjust protocols based on what works
Optimize parameters—tune system settings for better performance

Purpose: Continuous improvement over time


Feedback transforms systems from static to adaptive. Systems that learn from evaluation become more reliable.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.11 Techniques for Improving Reliability
Several techniques can enhance system reliability:


25.11.1 1. Multiple Runs (Sampling)

Generate multiple outputs—run instruction several times
Select best—choose highest quality output
Average or aggregate—combine multiple outputs

Purpose: Increase chance of good result



25.11.2 2. Self-Consistency

Compare multiple answers—run same instruction multiple times
Choose consistent result—select output that appears most frequently
Identify outliers—flag unusual outputs for review

Purpose: Reduce randomness and improve stability



25.11.3 3. Verification Steps

Add validation stages—check output at multiple points
Verify correctness—confirm output meets requirements

Cross-check—compare against expected patterns


Purpose: Catch errors before they propagate



25.11.4 4. Guardrails

Enforce constraints—prevent outputs that violate rules
Filter inappropriate content—block harmful or incorrect outputs
Limit behavior—keep system within defined boundaries

Purpose: Prevent unacceptable outputs


Reliability is built through layers of verification. Multiple checks ensure quality.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.12 Trade-offs
System design involves balancing competing requirements:


25.12.1 Reliability vs Cost

More checks → higher cost—additional evaluation increases computational expense
Fewer checks → lower cost—reduced verification saves resources
Balance: Choose evaluation depth based on cost constraints




25.12.2 Reliability vs Speed

More evaluation → slower execution—verification takes time
Less evaluation → faster response—reduced checks improve speed
Balance: Optimize for required latency while maintaining acceptable reliability




25.12.3 Reliability vs Flexibility

More constraints → less flexibility—strict rules limit options
Fewer constraints → more flexibility—loose rules allow more variation
Balance: Define constraints that ensure reliability without limiting necessary flexibility



System design requires balancing these factors based on specific requirements and constraints.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.13 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes undermine reliability:


25.13.1 1. Relying on Single Output

No validation—accept first output without checking
High risk—errors propagate to users
Unpredictable quality—no guarantee of correctness

Solution: Always validate outputs. Implement evaluation pipelines.



25.13.2 2. No Clear Metrics

Cannot measure improvement—don’t know if system is getting better
No baseline—can’t assess performance
Blind optimization—changing things without knowing if they help

Solution: Define clear metrics. Track performance over time.



25.13.3 3. Over-Reliance on Human Evaluation

Does not scale—can’t evaluate every output manually
Inconsistent—human judgment varies
Slow—manual evaluation is time-consuming

Solution: Implement automatic evaluation. Use human evaluation for special cases.



25.13.4 4. Ignoring Edge Cases

System fails under variation—unusual inputs break the system
Hidden failures—problems only appear in rare cases
False confidence—system works on test data but fails in production

Solution: Test diverse inputs. Identify and handle edge cases explicitly.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.14 Reliability in the Full System
Reliability integrates into the full Minimal Loop pipeline:
Intent → Spec → Instruction → Execution → Evaluation → Feedback

It ensures that:

Each stage is validated—quality checked at every point
Errors are detected—problems identified early
Systems improve over time—feedback drives refinement

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.15 Final Insight
Reliability is not a feature added at the end. It is a property of the entire system.


A system is only as reliable as its weakest definition. Every layer—from intent to evaluation—must be designed for reliability.

title: 25  Reliability & Evaluation
text:
25.16 Closing Statement
In the LLM era, building systems is not just about making them work. It is about making them work consistently.

Reliability is what turns capability into trust.

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.1 The Shift in Debugging
In traditional programming, debugging has a clear, well-defined meaning:
The object of debugging is clear and specific:
In the LLM era, this changes fundamentally. Systems are no longer defined primarily by code. They are defined by:
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
Finding bugs in code—locating errors in implementation
Fixing logic errors—correcting flawed reasoning or algorithms
Tracing execution paths—following program flow to identify issues



Code




Spec—the specification of what the system should do
Instruction—the protocol for how the system should execute
Context—the information available to the system at execution time



Debugging is no longer about fixing code. It is about fixing definitions.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.2 What Are We Debugging Now?
When an LLM system produces incorrect output, the problem is rarely:

A syntax error—grammatical or structural mistake in code
A runtime exception—program crashes or errors during execution
A logical bug—error in algorithmic reasoning

Instead, the problem is usually:

Ambiguity—unclear or multiple interpretations of requirements
Missing constraints—insufficient boundaries on acceptable behavior
Poor structure—lack of clear organization or framework



26.2.1 New Debug Targets
In the LLM era, debugging focuses on:

Spec—unclear or incomplete definition of requirements
Instruction—poorly designed execution protocol
Context—irrelevant or missing information



The bug is often in what we said, not how it was executed. The system follows our definition—when the definition is flawed, the result is flawed.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.3 Types of Failures
LLM systems can fail in different ways, each pointing to different underlying issues:


26.3.1 1. Incorrect Output

Wrong answer—response doesn’t match expected result
Irrelevant content—output addresses different question
Hallucination—system invents information

Likely causes: - Unclear spec—requirements not well-defined - Weak instruction—execution protocol insufficient - Poor context—missing or irrelevant information



26.3.2 2. Inconsistent Behavior

Same input → different outputs—results vary unpredictably
Random variation—no clear pattern in differences
Context-dependent inconsistency—behavior changes with session

Likely causes: - Weak constraints—insufficient boundaries - Poor instruction design—lack of structure - Context variability—different context construction



26.3.3 3. Format Violations

Output does not match expected structure—wrong JSON, missing fields
Incorrect format—wrong data types, organization
Parsing errors—output can’t be processed downstream

Likely causes: - Poor output design—unclear format requirements - Weak instruction—inadequate format specification - Missing validation—no format checking



26.3.4 4. Missing Information

Incomplete responses—output lacks required elements
Partial answers—only addresses part of query
Omitted details—important information missing

Likely causes: - Incomplete spec—requirements not fully specified - Weak constraints—no requirement for completeness - Poor context—missing source information

Each type of failure points to a different layer—spec, instruction, or context—where the fix should be applied.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.4 Debugging as Diagnosis
Debugging begins with diagnosis—identifying where the problem originates.


26.4.1 Key Question

Where is the problem?




26.4.2 Layered Diagnosis
Diagnosing LLM systems requires examining each layer:


26.4.2.1 Spec Layer

Is the goal clearly defined?
Are constraints complete?
Are success criteria explicit?
Is there ambiguity in requirements?

Questions to ask: - What exactly should the system do? - What are the boundaries of acceptable behavior? - How do we know when the system is correct?



26.4.2.2 Instruction Layer

Is the task explicit?
Is the output structured?
Are constraints enforced?
Is the protocol clear?

Questions to ask: - How does the instruction define the task? - What format is expected for output? - What rules constrain behavior?



26.4.2.3 Context Layer

Is relevant information included?
Is noise removed?
Is context consistent?
Is retrieval effective?

Questions to ask: - What information is available to the system? - Is the right information being retrieved? - Is context constructed consistently?


Correct diagnosis determines correct fixes. Debugging is systematic, not random.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.5 Debugging Spec
When the issue is in the spec, the problem is at the definition level—the system is trying to do the wrong thing.


26.5.1 Symptoms

Outputs are generally off-target—system consistently misunderstands requirements
System misunderstands intent—results don’t match original goals
Behavior is systematically wrong—same error pattern across multiple runs




26.5.2 Fixes

Clarify objective—make goals more explicit and precise
Add constraints—define boundaries and limitations
Define success criteria—specify how to evaluate correctness
Remove ambiguity—eliminate multiple interpretations




26.5.3 Example
Spec:
Summarize the document
Problem: Too vague—what kind of summary? How long? What to include?
Improved Spec:
Summarize the document into 5 key points, each under 20 words, focusing on main arguments and excluding minor details
Result: System produces summaries that match requirements more precisely.


Improve definition, not execution. When the spec is wrong, fixing execution won’t help.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.6 Debugging Instruction
When the issue is in the instruction, the problem is at the protocol level—the system doesn’t know how to do what’s asked.


26.6.1 Symptoms

Output format is inconsistent—results vary in structure
Behavior varies widely—same instruction produces different results
Format violations—output doesn’t match expected structure




26.6.2 Fixes

Define output structure—specify exact format requirements
Add explicit rules—create clear constraints on behavior
Simplify task—break complex instructions into simpler steps
Standardize format—use consistent data structures




26.6.3 Example
Instruction:
Explain the result
Problem: Too vague—what kind of explanation? How long? What format?
Improved Instruction:
Provide a 3-step explanation in bullet points, each under 15 words, covering: (1) what was found, (2) why it matters, (3) what to do next
Result: System produces consistent, structured explanations.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.7 Debugging Context
When the issue is in context, the problem is at the information level—the system doesn’t have the right information.


26.7.1 Symptoms

Irrelevant answers—response doesn’t address question
Missing key information—important facts omitted
Context-dependent failures—works sometimes, fails others




26.7.2 Fixes

Add relevant context—include information system needs
Remove noise—eliminate irrelevant information
Improve retrieval—enhance search and selection
Standardize context—consistent context construction



Context determines what the system sees. Better context leads to better results.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.8 Debugging as Iteration
Debugging is not a one-time fix. It follows a loop of continuous improvement:
Observe → Diagnose → Modify → Test → Repeat

Each iteration:

Reduces ambiguity—definition becomes clearer
Improves stability—behavior becomes more consistent
Moves toward correctness—results better meet requirements



26.8.1 Iterative Process

Observe—identify the failure or issue
Diagnose—determine which layer has the problem
Modify—adjust spec, instruction, or context
Test—run system to verify fix
Repeat—continue until issue resolved
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.9 Avoiding Common Pitfalls
Several common mistakes undermine effective debugging:


26.9.1 1. Blaming the Model

Assuming the system is flawed—blaming the LLM for poor results
Ignoring definition issues—overlooking problems in spec or instruction
Changing parameters without diagnosis—tuning settings without understanding cause

Solution: Focus on definition first. The model follows your definition—if definition is wrong, output will be wrong.



26.9.2 2. Random Adjustments

Changing parameters without diagnosis—tweaking settings randomly
Trial and error—making changes without understanding impact
No systematic approach—jumping between different fixes

Solution: Diagnose first, then fix. Identify the root cause before making changes.



26.9.3 3. Overfitting Fixes

Fixing one case but breaking others—optimizing for specific inputs
Narrow focus—addressing symptoms not causes
Fragile solutions—fixes that break easily

Solution: Understand the underlying problem. Fix at the appropriate layer.



26.9.4 4. Mixing Layers

Changing instruction when spec is the problem—treating symptoms not causes
Adjusting context when instruction is wrong—wrong layer for fix
Modifying everything at once—no way to isolate what works

Solution: Identify which layer has the problem. Fix at that layer only.


Debugging requires discipline, not guesswork. Systematic diagnosis leads to effective fixes.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.10 Debugging Tools and Techniques
Effective debugging requires appropriate tools and methods:


26.10.1 1. Test Cases

Define inputs and expected outputs—create test scenarios
Cover edge cases—test unusual or boundary conditions
Document behavior—track what works and what doesn’t

Purpose: Understand system behavior systematically



26.10.2 2. Controlled Inputs

Reduce variability—use consistent, controlled inputs
Isolate issues—test one variable at a time
Reproduce failures—create reliable test cases

Purpose: Make debugging predictable and repeatable



26.10.3 3. Logging

Track inputs, outputs, context—record what happens at each stage
Monitor performance—track latency, cost, success rates
Identify patterns—find recurring issues

Purpose: Understand system behavior and identify problems



26.10.4 4. Evaluation Metrics

Measure performance—quantify system behavior
Track improvement—monitor progress over time
Set benchmarks—establish standards for success

Purpose: Make debugging objective and measurable


Measurement enables debugging. Without metrics, debugging is guesswork.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.11 Debugging as Understanding
Debugging improves more than just the system—it improves understanding:

Each failure reveals:

Missing constraints—boundaries that weren’t specified
Hidden assumptions—implicit requirements that weren’t stated
Weak definitions—specifications that needed clarification


Debugging is learning:

The system—how it behaves and what it needs
The problem—what the real requirements are
Your understanding—how to define problems more clearly



Every bug is a signal. It tells you something about the definition that needs to be clarified.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
section: 26.12 From Debugging Code to Debugging Meaning
text:
26.12 From Debugging Code to Debugging Meaning
The fundamental shift in debugging:


26.12.1 Before

Debug logic—find errors in reasoning or implementation
Trace execution—follow program flow to identify issues
Fix code—correct syntax or logic errors

Focus: How the system is implemented



26.12.2 Now

Debug meaning—clarify what the system should do
Refine definition—improve spec and instruction
Adjust context—optimize information available to system

Focus: What the system should do

This requires:

Precision in language—clear, unambiguous definitions
Clarity in definition—explicit requirements and constraints
Awareness of system behavior—understanding how systems interpret definitions
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.13 Final Insight
Debugging in the LLM era is not about fixing what the system did.
It is about clarifying what the system should have done.


The system reflects your definition. If the output is wrong, the definition is incomplete. Debugging is the process of completing and refining that definition.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era
text:
26.14 Closing Statement
As programming shifts from implementation to definition, debugging shifts with it.

You are no longer debugging code. You are debugging your understanding.
crumbs: 26  Debugging in the LLM Era

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.1 From Correctness to Efficiency
So far, we have focused on:
However, a system that is correct but inefficient is not practical. A system that produces perfect results but is too slow or too expensive cannot be used in production.
Real systems must also consider:

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
Defining systems—creating clear specifications
Achieving correctness—ensuring systems produce right outputs
Ensuring reliability—making systems consistently accurate




Performance and Cost

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.2 The Three Core Metrics
In LLM systems, performance is primarily determined by three interrelated factors:

Token Usage—how much text the system processes and generates
Latency—how long the system takes to respond
Cost—how much money or resources the system consumes

These metrics are tightly coupled—they influence each other and must be balanced together.


27.2.1 Key Relationship
More Tokens → Higher Latency → Higher Cost
Processing more text takes more time and costs more money. Optimizing one metric often affects the others.


Efficiency is about controlling these three dimensions simultaneously. You cannot optimize one in isolation.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.3 What Are Tokens?
Tokens are the basic unit of processing for language models. They represent the chunks of text that models read and generate.
They can be:

Words—complete words in many cases
Parts of words—subword units for complex or rare words
Symbols—punctuation, numbers, special characters



27.3.1 Example
Sentence: “This is a test”
May be tokenized as: - “This” - “is” - “a” - “test”
Or possibly as: - “This” - ” is” - ” a” - ” test”
The exact tokenization depends on the model and its tokenizer.



27.3.2 Why Tokens Matter

Models process tokens, not words—computational cost is per-token
Pricing is based on token usage—most LLM APIs charge per token
Latency increases with token count—more tokens mean more processing time



Tokens are the currency of LLM systems. Every operation has a token cost.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.4 Token Usage and System Design
Token usage comes from multiple sources in the system:

Input context—information provided to the system
Instructions—the protocol defining execution
Output generation—the text the system produces



27.4.1 Sources of Token Growth


27.4.1.1 1. Long Context

Large prompts—extensive input provided to the system
Extensive memory—including too much conversation history
Verbose context—unnecessarily detailed information

Impact: Increases processing time and cost



27.4.1.2 2. Verbose Instructions

Unnecessary detail—overly specific or redundant instructions
Redundant text—saying the same thing multiple ways
Excessive examples—too many demonstration examples

Impact: Wastes tokens without improving results



27.4.1.3 3. Large Outputs

Long responses—generating more text than needed
Unstructured generation—allowing unrestricted output length
Verbose responses—wordy or repetitive outputs

Impact: Increases generation time and cost


Poor design leads to token waste. Efficient design minimizes token usage while maintaining quality.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.5 Latency
Latency is the time it takes for the system to:

Receive input—accept user query or data
Process—run through the model and tools
Produce output—generate and return response



27.5.1 Components of Latency

Model processing time—time for LLM to generate response
Network delay—time for data to travel between systems
Tool execution time—time for external APIs and services
System overhead—time for routing, validation, and other operations




27.5.2 Why Latency Matters

User experience—slow systems feel unresponsive
System responsiveness—real-time applications require fast responses
Throughput—slow latency limits how many requests can be handled



Fast systems feel intelligent. Slow systems feel broken, regardless of output quality.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.6 Cost
Cost is directly tied to token usage and system design:

Token usage—primary driver of API costs
Number of calls—frequency of API invocations
Model type—different models have different pricing



27.6.1 Cost Drivers

Large context windows—more tokens per request
Frequent API calls—more requests per unit time
Complex pipelines—multiple model calls per task
Large models—more expensive per token




27.6.2 Implication
Even small inefficiencies:

Scale significantly—small per-request costs multiply at scale
Increase operational cost—ongoing expenses accumulate
Limit feasibility—high costs make systems uneconomical



Cost is a function of design, not just usage. Good design minimizes cost while maintaining quality.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.7 Trade-offs Between Metrics
System design involves balancing competing requirements:


27.7.1 Token vs Accuracy

More tokens → more context → better accuracy
But higher cost—increased expense

Trade-off: Determine how much context is needed for required accuracy



27.7.2 Latency vs Quality

Faster responses → less processing
But potentially lower quality—less time for reasoning

Trade-off: Balance response speed with output quality requirements



27.7.3 Cost vs Reliability

More checks → higher cost
But better stability—more validation and evaluation

Trade-off: Determine acceptable failure rate vs. cost of prevention


System design requires balancing these factors based on specific requirements and constraints.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.8 Optimization Strategies
Several strategies can improve performance and reduce cost:


27.8.1 1. Reduce Token Usage

Compress context—summarize long histories
Remove redundancy—eliminate duplicate information
Use summaries—replace long text with concise summaries

Example: Instead of including full conversation history, include a summary of key points.



27.8.2 2. Optimize Instructions

Keep concise—use minimal text to convey requirements
Avoid unnecessary detail—focus on essential information
Remove redundancy—don’t repeat information

Example: Instead of detailed descriptions, use structured formats like JSON.



27.8.3 3. Control Output Length

Define limits—specify maximum output length
Use structured output—enforce format constraints
Request conciseness—ask for brief, focused responses

Example: Specify “under 100 words” or “3 bullet points” to limit output.



27.8.4 4. Efficient Context Management

Retrieve only relevant data—select specific information
Avoid full history—use summaries instead of full conversations
Prioritize recent information—focus on most relevant context

Example: Use retrieval to find only the most relevant documents, not all available.



27.8.5 5. Cache Results

Reuse previous outputs—avoid recomputing same inputs
Cache intermediate results—store results of expensive operations
Implement memoization—remember computation results

Example: Cache summary of a document so it’s not recomputed for subsequent queries.


Efficiency comes from better design, not shortcuts. Optimize structure, not just parameters.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.9 Architectural Optimization
System architecture significantly impacts performance:


27.9.1 1. Pipeline Design

Break tasks into smaller steps—divide complex operations
Avoid overloading single calls—distribute work across multiple calls
Parallelize independent operations—run multiple steps simultaneously

Example: Instead of one complex instruction, use multiple simpler instructions in sequence.



27.9.2 2. Selective Execution

Only run necessary components—skip unneeded processing
Conditional execution—run components only when needed
Early termination—stop processing when result is clear

Example: Only run expensive retrieval if the query requires external information.



27.9.3 3. Model Selection

Use smaller models where possible—not all tasks need largest models
Reserve larger models for complex tasks—allocate resources appropriately
Cascade models—use smaller models for filtering, larger for final processing

Example: Use a small model to classify queries, then route to appropriate specialist models.


Not all tasks require maximum capability. Match model size to task complexity.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.10 Measuring Performance
To optimize, you must measure. Performance metrics provide the data needed for improvement.


27.10.1 Key Metrics

Tokens per request—measure input and output token usage
Latency per step—track time for each operation
Cost per task—calculate expense for each operation




27.10.2 Approach

Log system behavior—record all operations and metrics
Analyze bottlenecks—identify slow or expensive components
Iterate design—make changes based on measurement data



Measurement enables optimization. Without metrics, optimization is guesswork.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.11 Common Mistakes
Several common mistakes undermine performance:


27.11.1 1. Ignoring Token Usage

Leads to unexpected costs—bills higher than expected
Limits scalability—cannot handle increased load
Reduces competitiveness—higher costs than alternatives

Solution: Monitor token usage. Set budgets and alerts.



27.11.2 2. Overloading Context

Reduces efficiency—more processing than needed
Increases latency—longer response times
Increases cost—more tokens processed

Solution: Be selective about context. Include only relevant information.



27.11.3 3. Over-Engineering Pipelines

Too many steps—unnecessary complexity
Increased cost—more operations than needed
Reduced maintainability—harder to understand and modify

Solution: Keep pipelines simple. Only add steps that add value.



27.11.4 4. Optimizing Too Early

Premature optimization—optimizing before understanding requirements
Reduced flexibility—over-optimized systems are hard to change
Wrong focus—optimizing non-critical components

Solution: Measure first, then optimize. Focus on bottlenecks.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.12 Performance as a System Property
Performance is not determined by a single component. It depends on:

Spec design—how requirements are defined
Instruction efficiency—how protocols are structured
Context management—how information is selected
System architecture—how components are organized



Efficiency emerges from the entire system. Optimize the whole, not just parts.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.13 Final Insight
In the LLM era, performance is not just about speed. It is about:

Efficient use of tokens—minimizing waste
Balanced latency—responding quickly enough
Controlled cost—operating within budget



The best systems are not just correct. They are efficient.

title: 27  Performance & Cost
text:
27.14 Closing Statement
As systems scale, performance and cost become critical constraints.

A well-designed system delivers value not just correctly, but efficiently.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.1 From Theory to System
We have explored fundamental concepts:
Now we integrate everything into a concrete, practical system:

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
Intent—defining what we want
Spec—specifying what it means
Instruction—making it executable
Memory—maintaining continuity
Tool use—extending capabilities
Multi-agent systems—coordinating multiple components



A Chat System

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.2 What is a Chat System?
A chat system is an agent system that:

Maintains a continuous interaction with a user over time

It must:

Understand user input—interpret queries and requests
Maintain context—remember conversation history
Generate relevant responses—produce appropriate answers
Adapt across turns—learn and adjust based on interaction



28.2.1 Core Requirements
A functional chat system must support:



28.2.2 1. Multi-turn interaction

Maintain conversation history—remember what was said
Track context—understand relationships between turns
Handle follow-up questions—respond to references to previous messages




28.2.3 2. Context awareness

Use past messages—incorporate conversation history
Maintain state—track ongoing topics and information
Reference previous exchanges—understand pronouns and references




28.2.4 3. Response generation

Produce relevant outputs—address user’s actual question
Maintain appropriate tone—match expected style and voice
Provide helpful information—give useful, accurate responses




28.2.5 4. Optional tool use

Retrieve data—access external information sources
Call APIs—interact with external services
Perform actions—execute operations on behalf of user




28.2.6 5. Memory management

Store conversation history—persist interactions
Retrieve relevant context—access past information
Manage context window—select most relevant information



Chat systems are inherently stateful—they must remember and adapt over time.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.3 Core Requirements
A functional chat system must support:


28.3.1 1. Multi-turn interaction

Maintain conversation history




28.3.2 2. Context awareness

Use past messages




28.3.3 3. Response generation

Produce relevant outputs




28.3.4 4. Optional tool use

Retrieve data
Call APIs




28.3.5 5. Memory management

Store and retrieve information



Chat systems are inherently stateful.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.3 System Architecture
A minimal chat system includes several components, each with specific responsibilities:


28.3.1 Components


28.3.1.1 Input Handler

Receives user input
Validates format
Routes to appropriate processor




28.3.1.2 Context Manager

Maintains conversation history
Constructs context for each turn
Manages context window limits




28.3.1.3 Memory System

Stores conversation history
Retrieves relevant past interactions

Manages long-term user information





28.3.1.4 Instruction Generator

Creates instruction for current turn
Incorporates context and requirements

Manages instruction templates





28.3.1.5 Execution Engine

Executes instruction

Uses tools if needed


Generates response





28.3.1.6 Output Renderer

Formats response for display

Applies styling and formatting


Presents to user






28.3.2 Flow
User Input
→ Context Construction
→ Instruction Generation
→ Execution
→ Output
→ Memory Update
Each step is explicitly defined and structured.


Each step must be explicitly defined. Clear boundaries between components ensure reliability.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.4 Context Construction
Before generating a response, the system must build appropriate context. This is critical for maintaining conversation coherence.


28.4.1 Context Sources

Current user message—the immediate input
Conversation history—previous turns in the conversation
Retrieved knowledge—information from external sources
User preferences—stored information about the user




28.4.2 Key Challenge

Context is limited—models can only process so much information
Must select relevant information—not all history is equally important
Must prioritize—decide what to include and what to exclude




28.4.3 Strategy

Include recent turns—prioritize most recent messages
Summarize older history—compress distant turns into summaries
Retrieve relevant data—fetch information related to current query
Include user preferences—maintain personalization



Context determines response quality. Better context leads to better answers.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.5 Instruction Generation
The system must translate context and current query into an executable instruction.


28.5.1 Example Instruction

Task: respond to user query
Input: context + user message
Output: reply in appropriate format
Constraints: maintain conversation tone, be helpful and accurate




28.5.2 Considerations

Tone—match expected conversational style
Format—appropriate structure for response
Constraints—any limits on length or content
Role—system’s persona and expertise



Instruction defines behavior at each turn. Clear instructions lead to consistent responses.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.6 Memory Integration
A chat system requires memory beyond immediate context to maintain long-term relationships and learning.


28.6.1 Types of Memory


28.6.1.1 Short-Term Memory

Recent conversation—last few turns
Session data—information about current session
Working context—active information for current task

Use: Maintain conversation coherence within a session



28.6.1.2 Long-Term Memory

User preferences—likes, dislikes, habits
Conversation history—past interactions over time
Knowledge base—facts and information learned

Use: Personalization and continuity across sessions




28.6.2 Flow
Memory → Retrieval → Context → Response
Memory feeds context, which drives response generation.


Memory enables continuity. Without it, each conversation starts fresh.

title: 28  Building a Chat System
text:
28.7 Tool Use in Chat
Chat systems often require tools to access external information or perform actions:


28.7.1 Examples

Search API—find current information on the web
Database query—access stored data
External services—weather, calculations, translations




28.7.2 Flow
User Query → Decide → Tool Call → Result → Response
The system determines when tools are needed, calls them, and integrates results.


Tools extend capabilities beyond conversation, enabling action and information retrieval.
